Moreover, the basic assumption of JAVELIN is that the emission line light curves are the result of the response to an ionizing continuum which is changing exactly in the same way as the observed continuum used in the calculations, in contradiction to some well documented previous results (Goad et al. 2016), as well as some of the cases presented in this work. In fact, JAVELIN computes a solution which assumes a transfer function of the continuum and solves simultaneously for both, the continuum and line emission interpolated light curves. We run JAVELIN using the same lag limits used during the ICCF calculations, implemented 5000 Markov chain Monte Carlo iterations during the `burn-in' phase, and 10000 iterations during the final parameter determination step, assumed the default models for description of the continuum (DRWP, or `Cont Model' in JAVELIN language) and line (`RMap Model') light curves, and solved for single continuum-line pairs at each time. Figure 6 presents the JAVELIN lag posterior distributions for the same sources presented in Figure 5. table Comparison between the histograms presented in Figure 5 and 6 shows that both methods roughly agree on the best determined lags. However, JAVELIN tends to present considerably compact probability distributions for most objects, in some cases with several well defined, extremely narrow peaks. As already observed by Fausnaugh et al. (2016), this is mostly because of the very strong -- and seldom demonstrated -- assumption that the emission line light curves are a simple lagged and smoothed version of the continuum emission. In fact, Fausnaugh et al. (2016) suggests that the actual dispersion is within 2 3 of the JAVELIN quoted errors. In a few cases (e.g., CT1061), the JAVELIN results are very close to those found by our ICCF analysis. JAVELIN results do not show spurious peaks at the edges of the probability distributions, which is also a consequence of the assumption of a BLR responding to the continuum light curves. Finally, it is interesting to notice some cases with contradicting results between the methods. For example, JAVELIN fails to determine a MgII lag for CT252, while the ICCF method detects a clear lag around 550 days which however, contains only 5 \ of the original CCCD distribution (this is in contrast with the CIV lag for CT975, with a 1 \ peak observed in the reliable region of the CCCD, which JAVELIN recovers successfully). At the same time, JAVELIN finds a well behaved peak at around 1000 days for the SiIV line in CT953, while the ICCF only finds a very shallow peak. Since our main aim is to find statistically sound lags for our sample of luminous quasars, for the analysis and discussion in the next sections we will use the more conservative ICCF and CCCD-based lags and error estimates as representative line lags for our sources. The radius--luminosity relation represents a milestone for the determination of the masses of black holes hosted by AGN. Here we will update the radius--luminosity relation for the CIV emission line first presented by Peterson et al. (2005, 2006) and later extended to higher luminosities by Kaspi et al. (2007) and Trevese et. (2014). To our knowledge, no determination of the radius--luminosity relations for the Ly has previously been attempted, as no results for objects above L_ (1350 ) = 10^ 45 ergs s ^ 1 had been reported until now. In what follows we only construct radius--luminosity relations for objects where the lags are inconsistent with zero at a 1 level, this is, when _ cent / _ > 1 . UV L_ (1350 ) luminosities were obtained from the mean spectra of each quasar and are reported in Table 1. The errors represent the rms variation observed in the R-band continuum light curves. Scatter in the radius--luminosity correlations is given in each plot, where the first value corresponds to the observed scatter and the second value corresponds to the scatter due to measurement errors. A linear regression was determined for each radius--luminosity relation using the bivariate method (BCES) of Akritas Bershady (1996), which takes into account errors in both, the lags and the luminosities. As our lag error determinations are not symmetrical, we use the mean of both confidence limits as a first guess for the lag error of each data point and iterate so that the final error bar considered ( _ or _ + ) is determined by whether the points are found above or below the best fit solution. Convergence was always found after a few iterations. The CIV Radius--Luminosity relation For luminosities below L_ (1350 ) = 10^ 45 ergs s ^ 1 , CIV lag measurements are compiled in Peterson et al. (2005, 2006) and Metzroth et al. (2006), and a very recent determination for NGC5548 is found in De Rosa et al. (2015). Two sources above this luminosity limit have been published: S5 0836+71 by Kaspi et al. (2007), and PG1247+267 by Trevese et al. (2014). Using the ICCF method it is not possible, however, to determine the lag for PG1247+267, as Trevese et al. (2014) also pointed out, and therefore we will not include this source in our CIV radius--luminosity determination. Our work adds to the list seven new high luminosity sources, namely, CT1061, CT286, CT564, CT650, CT953, J214355 and J221516. In Figure 7 we present the results from the linear regression to the CIV radius--luminosity relation together with the lag and luminosity measurements. Following Kaspi et al. (2007), we write the CIV radius--luminosity in the following way: R_ CIV 10 \ lt-days = (0.22 0.10) L_ (1345 ) 10^ 43 \ erg \ s ^ 1 ^ (0.46 0.08) The updated CIV radius--luminosity relation is very close to that reported by Kaspi et al. (2007), albeit with slightly larger uncertainties. This is because we have included the mean from two lag measurements of NGC4151 taken from Metzroth et al. (2006), which helped to bridge the luminosity gap between the more luminous Seyfert galaxies and the dwarf Seyfert NGC4395 ( L_ (1350 ) 10^ 40 ergs s ^ 1 ), but added more dispersion to the relation. Also, the two measurements for the NGC4395 lag have been averaged, reducing the weight of the measurements at the very low luminosity end. We remind the reader that a well determined radius--luminosity relation for CIV does not solve the issues of using CIV as a well calibrated mass estimator for AGN, as the main problems with this line is the difficulty to determine a velocity that would reflect a virialized component of the velocity field of the line (see further discussion in Section 6.3 and e.g., Baskin Laor 2005; Denney et al. 2012, 2016; Mej ia-Restrepo et al. 2016, and references therein). The Ly Radius--Luminosity relation From our sample, CT1061, CT286, CT564, CT650, CT953, J214355 and J221516 show significant lags and well determined uncertainties and will be considered for the determination of the Ly radius--luminosity relation.

gmw_00008957.name: NGC 5846A
gmw_00008957.overview: NGC 5846A is a compact elliptical galaxy located directly south of the dominant group elliptical galaxy NGC 5846 [arXiv:1108.5229]. It exhibits a short X-ray tail extending to its south, which provides evidence of ongoing ram pressure stripping during its infall [arXiv:1108.5229].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5846A as a compact elliptical south of NGC 5846, likely tidally stripped, and detects a short X-ray tail from ongoing ram-pressure stripping during infall.

quotes.quote: NGC 5846A, shown in Figure fig:n5846dss , is directly south of the dominant group elliptical galaxy NGC 5846. NGC 5846A was among the earliest galaxies confirmed to be a compact elliptical galaxy (cE).

The near-infrared indices for NGC5846A suggest that the progenitor of NGC5846A may have been a massive elliptical galaxy, and that the catastrophic tidal interaction, responsible for stripping 90 of its progenitor's stellar mass, may also have funneled gas into NGC5846A's core, resulting in an intermediate age population of stars near its nucleus (Davidge 2008). One might expect such strong tidal interactions would also remove any hot gas corona associated with the compact elliptical galaxy, leaving it devoid of X-ray emitting gas. Figure fig:ngc5846A shows, however, an X-ray tail extending 0.54 kpc ( 4 7 ) to the south of NGC5846A, evidence for ongoing ram pressure stripping of a residual gaseous corona of the compact elliptical galaxy. Since the radial velocity difference between NGC 5846A and NGC 5846 is 487 (NED), already transonic for 0.7 keV gas, the infall is likely supersonic.

gmw_00008958.name: GB6 J0140+4024
gmw_00008958.overview: GB6 J0140+4024 is a young radio source classified as a quasar [arXiv:1607.06937]. Its optical spectrum exhibits magnesium II and carbon III emission lines, along with broad iron II emission [arXiv:1607.06937].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes GB6 J0140+4024 as one of four young radio sources, finding prominent Mg II and C III lines and measuring broad Mg II and Fe II fluxes.

quotes.quote: GB6 J0140+4024 . This source encounter a low signal/noise ratio (S/N). However, the Mg ii and C iii lines are prominent (upper left panel in Figure fig1 ). The spectrum in rest frame which is corrected the Galactic extinction around Mg ii is plotted in Figure fig2 , along with the modeled components. The flux and FWHM of the broad Mg ii are 21.59 3.10 10^ 16 erg s^ 1 and 4389 714 km s^ 1 , respectively. The flux of Fe ii is 100.86 24.17 10^ 16 erg s^ 1 .

gmw_00008959.name: WISE J130500.31+005422.1
gmw_00008959.overview: WISE J130500.31+005422.1 is an obscured active galactic nucleus that lies on the AGN/composite boundary of the BPT diagram [arXiv:1811.02585]. The host galaxy is a late-stage merger characterized by substantial reddening and large galactic absorption [arXiv:1811.02585].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISE J130500.31+005422.1 as an obscured AGN on the BPT AGN/composite boundary, notes weak features prevent velocity-dispersion measurement, and describes it as a late-stage merger with large galactic absorption.

quotes.quote: WISE J130500.31+005422.1 is on the AGN/composite boundary in the BPT diagram, but its ratio of [ Ne 3 ] / [ O 2 ] clearly identifies it as an AGN shown in hain14salt . Therefore we further confirm that our targets are all AGNs.

For the two most distant AGN, WISE J130500.31+005422.1 and WISE J133331.15-012653.3, the low S/N and relatively weak features prevent a velocity dispersion measurement. In WISE J115158.63-004641.2 and WISE J143459.27-014432.8, both measurements are consistent with being at or below the limiting spectrograph resolution ( =100 km/s). Further higher spectral resolution measurements are needed for firmer constraints.

One of the target, WISE J130500.31+005422.1, is a late-stage merger with consistent large galactic absorption. Their Balmer decrements indicate substantial reddening in their host galaxies, which suggests the existence of different scales of obscuration in the heavily buried luminous AGNs. The galactic column densities are around 10^ 23 cm^ 2 , compared to the nuclear N_ H over 3.1 10^ 25 cm^ 2 of these CT quasars.

gmw_00008960.name: Astarte
gmw_00008960.overview: Astarte is a dusty, ultra-massive star-forming galaxy located at redshift z ~ 2 [arXiv:2101.07724]. It exhibits main-sequence-like star formation activity but possesses a low molecular gas fraction and a short depletion time of 0.22 Gyr [arXiv:2101.07724].

sources.summary: Astarte is described as a dusty, ultra-massive high-redshift galaxy with ALMA CO emission, MS-like star formation, low molecular gas fraction, short depletion time, and CO/IR ratios consistent with MS activity.

quotes.quote: In this work we study a system of two galaxies, namely Astarte and Adonis, at z 2 when the Universe was undergoing its peak of star formation activity. Astarte is a dusty star-forming galaxy at the massive-end of the main sequence (MS) and Adonis is a less-massive, bright in ultraviolet (UV), companion galaxy with an optical spectroscopic redshift. We analyze the physical properties of this system, and probe the gas mass of Astarte with its ALMA CO emission, to investigate whether this ultra-massive galaxy is quenching or not, and whether it has always been on the MS of star-forming galaxies. We use CIGALE - a spectral energy distribution modeling code - that relies on the energetic balance between the UV and the IR, to derive some of the key physical properties of Astarte and Adonis, mainly their star formation rates (SFRs), stellar masses, and dust luminosities.

We find that Astarte exhibits a MS-like star formation activity, while Adonis is undergoing a strong starburst (SB) phase. The molecular gas mass of Astarte is far below the gas fraction of typical star-forming galaxies at z=2. This low gas content and high SFR, result in a depletion time of 0.22 0.07 Gyrs, which is slightly shorter than what is expected for a MS galaxy at this redshift. The CO luminosity relative to the total IR luminosity suggests a MS-like activity if we assume a galactic conversion factor and a low transition ratio.

gmw_00008961.name: Adonis
gmw_00008961.overview: Adonis is a ultraviolet-bright companion galaxy to the more massive galaxy Astarte [arXiv:2101.07724]. Spectral energy distribution modeling indicates that Adonis is undergoing a strong starburst phase that places it far above the star-forming main sequence [arXiv:2101.07724].

sources.summary: Adonis is presented as the less massive UV-bright companion to Astarte with an optical redshift; SED modelling finds it lies far above the main sequence as a strong starburst.

quotes.quote: In this work we study a system of two galaxies, namely Astarte and Adonis, at z 2 when the Universe was undergoing its peak of star formation activity. Astarte is a dusty star-forming galaxy at the massive-end of the main sequence (MS) and Adonis is a less-massive, bright in ultraviolet (UV), companion galaxy with an optical spectroscopic redshift. We analyze the physical properties of this system, and probe the gas mass of Astarte with its ALMA CO emission, to investigate whether this ultra-massive galaxy is quenching or not, and whether it has always been on the MS of star-forming galaxies. We use CIGALE - a spectral energy distribution modeling code - that relies on the energetic balance between the UV and the IR, to derive some of the key physical properties of Astarte and Adonis, mainly their star formation rates (SFRs), stellar masses, and dust luminosities.
We find that Astarte exhibits a MS-like star formation activity, while Adonis is undergoing a strong starburst (SB) phase.

Adonis lies on 10 MS qualifying it to be a strong starburst, despite its relatively low SFR. While being a starburst, this type of source cannot be detected even by the deepest 3 mm ALMA survey (expected flux of 7 Jy), which have a 1- noise of 9.7 Jy Gonzales2020 .

gmw_00008962.name: REBELS-14
gmw_00008962.overview: REBELS-14 is an extremely UV-luminous galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 7.08 [arXiv:2202.01219]. It is a low-mass system [arXiv:2311.16071] that exhibits strong Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:2202.01219] and a recent, large burst of star formation [arXiv:2311.16071].

sources.summary: The paper uses REBELS-14 as a low-mass REBELS model galaxy with a typical low-bin SFH and strong optical nebular-line emission indicating a recent large star-formation burst.

quotes.quote: In Fig. fig:SFHs we show the SFHs (blue lines) from Topping22 for REBELS-14, 29 and 25, i.e. three galaxies with rather different SFHs and stellar masses (from M_* 10^9 M_ to M_* 2 10^ 10 M_ ) within our sample. In this Figure, we also show the SFHs used as input for our chemical evolution models for each galaxy (orange lines).

The choice of comparing the three individual galaxy models with binned data is justified by the fact that REBELS-14 show a SFH that is quite typical of the low stellar mass bin, while REBELS-29 and 25 have two SFHs that are recurrent in the high stellar mass bin. In addition, this choice allows us to better show the typical evolution of an individual galaxy for different dust evolutionary setups.

In fact, Hubble Space Telescope (HST) counterparts of REBELS-14 and REBELS-39 (which are part of the low-mass subsample) show very strong emission by rest-frame optical nebular lines ( Bowler17 ), which can be explained only by the presence of a very recent, large burst of star formation. In turn, this can limit the ability of non-parametric SFHs to overcome the so-called outshining problem, i.e. the underestimation of the contribution of older stellar populations due to overwhelming light form recent bursts (e.g. Leja19 ).

sources.summary: REBELS-14 is an extremely UV-luminous z=7.084 galaxy with high inferred emission-line EW, a strong Binospec Ly detection, typical Ly strength, and moderate escape fraction.

quotes.quote: REBELS-14 REBELS-14 is an extremely UV-luminous ( = -22.7 ) galaxy in the XMM3 field at =7.084 ( Bouwens2021_REBELS ; Schouws et al. in prep), also referred to as XMM3-227436 in Endsley2021_LyA . This source is inferred to have a stellar mass of = 8.7 and its red IRAC color ( [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] = 0.85 ^ +0.44 _ 0.37 ) suggests an EW of 1520 , approximately twice that typical of z 7 galaxies ( 760 ; e.g. Endsley2021_OIII ). The Binospec spectrum for REBELS-14 reveals a 19.0 detection with a peak wavelength of 9833.3 (Fig. fig:LyA_Detections ), corresponding to = 7.089 adopting a rest-frame wavelength of _ Ly = 1215.67 . We measure a total Ly flux of (21.5 1.4) 10 ^ 18 erg/s/cm ^2 , corresponding to an EW of 14.6 3.0 . This EW is similar to the median value found among UV-bright ( 22.5 20.5 ) galaxies at z 7 (10 ; Endsley2021_LyA ), indicating that the emission strength of REBELS-14 is fairly typical.

gmw_00008963.name: NGC 2798
gmw_00008963.overview: NGC 2798 is an early-type spiral starburst galaxy [arXiv:1106.4022] that hosts a central active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1305.2923]. It exhibits a peculiar morphology [arXiv:1106.4022] and high optical attenuation on its eastern side, which is shifted toward its close interacting companion, NGC 2799 [arXiv:1305.2923].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2798 as a starbursting early-type spiral with high dust/stellar flux, peculiar merger-like morphology, warm-dust offset, and obscured transition-galaxy behavior.

quotes.quote: The three galaxies with the largest dust/stellar flux ratios are NGC 1482, NGC 2146, and NGC 2798, which are all starbursting early-type spirals. NGC 1482 also has evidence of superwind outflows (Hota Saikia 2005), while NGC 2146 and NGC 2798 have peculiar morphologies, possibly indicative of interactions or mergers (but see Greve et al. \ 2006 on NGC 2146).

Three Sa galaxies---NGC 4594 in the lower left of the plot and NGC 1482 and NGC 2798 at the high f_ dust /f_ end---are also outside of the central locus of the plot, and appear to follow the trend of early-type galaxies. More clearly than the rest of the sample, the early-types exhibit a strong correlation between the dust/stellar ratios (Spearman rank r_s=0.56 , 95 significance). An interesting question to ask is why the early-types have such a large range of dust/stellar flux and mass ratios, spanning three orders of magnitude. One possibility is that these galaxies have a wide range of radiation field intensities and star formation rates (see Section SFRsec ), as well as widely varying gas/stellar ratios (Young et al. \ 2009). In particular, all but two of the early-types are lenticular galaxies, and many of them have similar masses and TIR luminosities. If we look at one pair of S0s, NGC 1316 and NGC 1291 have extremely low dust/stellar ratios, and appear to be similar to the elliptical galaxies (NGC 1404 and NGC 584). Looking at another pair of S0s, NGC 1266 and NGC 1377 have particularly high dust/stellar ratios. For NGC 1266, we believe this could be due to an active nucleus (see Dale et al. 2007; Smith et al. 2007) which could be heating dust in the central region; however, this galaxy is likely an exception, as galaxies in the sample were selected not to have luminous AGN (Kennicutt et al. \ 2003). For NGC 1377, Roussel et al. \ (2006) argues that it is undergoing an opaque nascent burst of star formation, and the intense radiation field is significantly heating the dust. These two S0's and the Sa's mentioned previously, NGC 1482 and NGC 2798, are most offset from the trend of the rest of the sample. They are relatively bright in the far-IR, but this is not accompanied by a large dust mass. The offset may be explained by their relatively warm dust temperatures, which we discuss in Section FIRcolors .
Earlier-type spirals with obscured star formation, such as NGC 1097, NGC 2146, NGC 2798, and NGC 7331, have also been described as transition galaxies (Zhu et al. \ 2011).

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 2798 as an AGN-bearing sample galaxy whose eastern side has unexpectedly high optical attenuation, with the attenuation peak shifted toward companion NGC 2799.

quotes.quote: We list only inclinations measured using 2D gas kinematic information (H or HI velocity fields), except for NGC 2798 where none was available.
NGC 2798, 3627, 4321 and 7331 exhibit evidence of a central AGN, although none is bright enough to dominate the stellar continuum light in the galaxy.

Three galaxies, NGC 2798, NGC 5713 and NGC 3077, have regions that even given the estimated errors would appear to be forbidden by a foreground screen model, having significantly more optical attenuation than would be expected given the amount of dust detected in emission. Closer examination of NGC 2798 reveals that these points fall on the eastern side of the galaxy, where the peak in optical attenuation appears shifted in the direction of a close interacting companion, NGC 2799.

gmw_00008964.name: ASASSN-15lh host
gmw_00008964.overview: The ASASSN-15lh host is a massive, passively evolving early-type galaxy [arXiv:1710.01045, arXiv:1610.01632] with an old stellar population and limited ongoing star formation [arXiv:1710.01045, arXiv:1610.01632]. It contains a weak active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1710.01045] and may have experienced a past galaxy-galaxy interaction [arXiv:1710.01045].

sources.summary: The paper describes the ASASSN-15lh host as a passive early-type galaxy with old stellar populations, LINER-like weak AGN emission, a massive central black hole, and signs of past star formation or interaction.

quotes.quote: Here, we use imaging from the Hubble Space Telescope, and spatially resolved, medium-resolution spectroscopy from the Very Large Telescope obtained with X-Shooter and MUSE to study the host of the very luminous transient ASASSN-15lh. The dominant stellar population at the transient site is old (around 1 to 2 Gyr) without signs of recent star formation. We also detect emission from ionized gas, originating from three different, time invariable, narrow components of collisionally excited metal and Balmer lines. The ratios of emission lines in the Baldwin-Phillips-Terlevich diagnostic diagram indicate that the ionization source is a weak active galactic nucleus with a black hole mass of M_ = 5_ 3 ^ +8 10^ 8 M_ , derived through the M_ relation. The narrow line components show spatial and velocity offsets on scales of 1 kpc and 500 , respectively; these offsets are best explained by gas kinematics in the narrow-line region.

This interpretation is consistent with the galaxy morphology and spectrum as a passively evolving galaxy, the location of the galaxy in an overdense larger scale environment, and the faint AGN emission in its center. All this suggests that the ASASSN-15lh host went through a very active phase of star formation and AGN activity roughly one or two Gyr in the past, possibly triggered by a galaxy-galaxy interaction.

sources.summary: The paper describes ASASSN-15lh's host as an old, massive, low-star-formation galaxy whose nuclear location and stellar population support a SMBH/TDE interpretation.

quotes.quote: The old, massive M_ 2 10^ 11 M_ host galaxy of ASASSN-15lh, with limited star formation ( SFR<0.3 M_ yr^ 1 ; Dong16 ), is however markedly different from those of core-collapse SNe (e.g. Leaman11 ) as well as of envelope-stripped SLSNe, which tend to be younger star forming systems with significantly lower stellar mass ( Lunnan14,Lunnan15,Perley16 ). This observation, together with the location of the transient, astrometrically consistent with the host galaxy nucleus, inspired a connection between ASASSN-15lh and the tidal disruption of a star by the host-galaxy super-massive black hole (SMBH, Godoy-Rivera16,Brown16,leloudas16,Perley16 ).

The best-fit model has a total stellar mass of 1.2 10 ^ 11 M_ , a current stellar age of 10 Gyr, and an e folding timescale of 2 Gyr, resulting in a current star-formation rate of 0.8 M_ yr^ 1 . These numbers are in broad agreement with those reported previously (e.g, Dong16,leloudas16 ). Other choices for the stellar population model produced qualitatively similar results, although usually with smaller current star-formation rates.

gmw_00008965.name: Shaheer I
gmw_00008965.overview: Shaheer I is an uncatalogued low surface brightness galaxy candidate located in the Camelopardalis constellation [arXiv:2309.14743]. It features an asymmetric, molar-tooth shape with no apparent nucleus and a blue color profile that suggests a population of young, hot stars [arXiv:2309.14743].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Shaheer I as an uncatalogued galaxy candidate found in Pan-STARRS, with a dim asymmetric molar-tooth shape, no apparent nucleus, blue colors, and uncertain distance requiring deeper imaging.

quotes.quote: The galaxy candidate Shaheer I was found during visual inspection of the digital pan-STARRS survey near the MCG+12-06-003 group of galaxies in the camelopardalis constellation. Due to its unusual shape and dim profile it caught interest leading to its coordinates being noted down. On searching through various catalogues and online databases, it was established that automated surveys had not detected the object as a potential galaxy.

Shaheer I is definitely the more elusive of the galaxy (or dwarf galaxy) candidates. Its shape is asymmetric, as is evident in the radial profile given in fig fig:7 . On first look it resembles a molar tooth (thus the title galactic tooth-fairy). With no apparent nucleus its low surface brightness could be the reason it has not been investigated prior. Its blue color profile would indicate that it is mostly comprised of young hot stars.

gmw_00008966.name: Shaheer II
gmw_00008966.overview: Shaheer II is an uncatalogued galaxy candidate that features an obvious galactic nucleus and a teardrop-like shape [arXiv:2309.14743]. It contains star cluster regions with a blue-white color profile along its top rim, while its southern region consists of a diffuse halo [arXiv:2309.14743].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Shaheer II as an uncatalogued galaxy candidate with an obvious nucleus, teardrop morphology, top-rim star-cluster regions, blue-white colors, diffuse southern halo, and possible high proper motion.

quotes.quote: After the field of view around Shaheer I was imaged at Taqwa observatory (see sec:Luminance ) and inspected, Shaheer II was found just 6.67 arc minutes to the south. Despite its brighter surface profile and obvious galactic nucleus, it had also not been detected in galactic surveys however some point source astrometric and photometric data (see table tab:2 ) is available through the extended Gaia-PS1-SDSS (GPS1+) proper motion catalog Tian_2020 for Shaheer II and the pan-STARRS release (PS1) survey for Shaheer I.

Among the two, Shaheer II is one which contains an obvious galactic nucleus and possesses a tear drop like shape. There are also star cluster regions towards what can be considered the top rim of the galaxy. These regions are more evident in the residual after the sérsic model is subtracted in fig fig:11 . Young stars might populate the regions owing to the blue-white colour profile. The presence of these brighter regions are only visible through current images in the top rim of the galaxy, while the southern region is a diffuse halo.

The large proper motion of the source found in GPS1+ (see table tab:2 ) suggests that it is a high propermotion/velocity galaxy (thus the title cosmic bullet) creating a cometary phenomena similar to the "Comet galaxy" introduced in .

gmw_00008967.name: App 1
gmw_00008967.overview: App 1 is an extragalactic H II-region-like galaxy located at a distance of 126.4 Mpc [arXiv:1906.09612]. Visual imaging reveals a diffuse core and a bluish loop interpreted as a spiral arm structure [arXiv:1906.09612].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies App 1 as an extragalactic HII-region-like galaxy at 126.4 Mpc, with emission-line ratios, weak [SII], and PanSTARRS morphology suggesting a diffuse core and spiral-arm structure.

quotes.quote: We derive for the App 1 emission-line ratios the values log( [ OIII ] 5007/H )=0.26 and log( [ SII ] /H )=-0.51, identifying App 1 as a HII-region-like galaxy, and not a Seyfert galaxy, in the BPT-diagram. Particularly, the weak [ SII ] emission lines turn the classification and favours the HII nature of this galaxy. The PanSTARRs picture of App 1 (see Fig.5) unveils a diffuse core with a blueish loop that we interpret as a spiral arm structure of a galaxy. The diameter of the structure is about 0.7 which equals a projected diameter of 25 kpc, and this fits the typical size for a normal Spiral galaxy. Both, the spectroscopic and visual results are in accordance with the assumption that App 1 is a HII galaxy.

gmw_00008968.name: Pre 21
gmw_00008968.overview: Pre 21 is a dwarf galaxy located at a comoving radial distance of approximately 25.6 Mpc [arXiv:1906.09612]. It is classified as a probable H II-region-like galaxy [arXiv:1906.09612] and features a possible 20-kilolight-year tidal stream that suggests a past close encounter with another galaxy [arXiv:1906.09612].

sources.summary: The paper finds Pre 21 at 25.6 Mpc, estimates a 2100-light-year source size, identifies a possible 20 kly tidal stream, and classifies it as a probable HII-region-like dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: The results of the second object, Pre 21, are shown in Tab.2, with a mean value of the redshift of z = 0.00597 0.00016. We conclude a comoving radial distance of 25.6 Mpc. With the visual object diameter the projected source size is about 2100 lyrs. Also, the PanSTARRS image of Pre 21 shows a streak of light pointing to the north, with a projected length of about 20 klyrs. We would interpret this feature as a possible tidal stream, and Pre 21 as a small dwarf galaxy that could have survived a close encounter with another galaxy.
As the [ SII ] emission-lines are weak, the 2nd classification ratio can only be roughly estimated to log( [ SII ] /H )<-1, and this attests Pre 21 to be probably a HII-region-like galaxy.

gmw_00008969.name: Pre 24
gmw_00008969.overview: Pre 24 is an H II-region-like galaxy located at a comoving radial distance of 62.4 Mpc [arXiv:1906.09612]. It exhibits a diffuse, elongated morphology with a projected diameter of approximately 13 kpc, which may be a disturbed spiral galaxy that underwent past interactions [arXiv:1906.09612].

sources.summary: The paper places Pre 24 at 62.4 Mpc, classifies it as HII-region-like, and interprets its elongated 13 kpc PanSTARRS morphology as a disturbed or irregular formerly spiral galaxy.

quotes.quote: The results of the object Pre 24 in Tab.3 yield a mean redshift of z = 0.01454 0.00029, with a comoving radial distance of 62.4 Mpc. The ratio values are log( [ OIII ] 5007/H )=0.64 and log( [ SII ] /H )=0.89 and classify Pre 24 as a HII-region-like galaxy. The PanSTARRS picture of Pre 24 (Fig.7) shows a diffuse object, elongated in the RA-direction with a projected diameter of about 13 kpc. This is significantly smaller than the standard spiral galaxy size, and we assume that this extragalactic object might have been a spiral galaxy, but it has undergone interactions with other galaxies, causing an irregular morphology. The result could be a active galaxy core, with a lost spiral structure, or the spiral arms are too weak to be detected in the image.

gmw_00008970.name: Ra 69
gmw_00008970.overview: Ra 69 is a compact, blueish object located at a comoving radial distance of approximately 186.0 Mpc [arXiv:1906.09612]. It has a projected diameter of about 13,000 light-years and may be a dwarf galaxy with an H II-region-like core or the bright bulge of an H II galaxy [arXiv:1906.09612].

sources.summary: The paper derives Ra 69's redshift distance as 186.0 Mpc and describes a compact blue object whose appearance could be a bright HII-galaxy bulge or dwarf galaxy core.

quotes.quote: With the results of the fourth object Ra 69 (Tab.4), we derive a mean redshift of z = 0.04360 0.00051. This redshift equals a comoving radial distance of 186.0 Mpc and the object visual diameter gives a projected diameter of about 13.000 lyrs. For Ra 69 no surrounding galactic structure can be seen in the PanSTARRS image (Fig.8). It's blueish and compact appearance is very similar to Pre 21, although Ra 69 is 4.7-times bigger than is "visual twin". This bright blue part could be seen as the bright bulge of a HII galaxy or Ra 69 could be a dwarf galaxy with a HII-region-like galactic core.

gmw_00008971.name: 3C 14
gmw_00008971.overview: 3C 14 is a red, radio-loud quasar located at a high redshift of z = 1.469 [arXiv:1501.00635, arXiv:2011.03130]. It features an extremely lobe-dominated radio morphology with an asymmetric structure, a 308 kpc extended radio jet, and moderate torus obscuration [arXiv:1501.00635, arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 14 as a high-redshift, extremely lobe-dominated radio quasar; although a LAT excess appeared near its position, improved localization favored CRATES J0036+1832 as the gamma-ray counterpart.

quotes.quote: 3C 14 is a high redshift ( z = 1.469) steep spectral radio quasar with an extremely lobe-dominated ( R =0.01) radio morphology (Laing et al. 1983). The 8.4 GHz VLA radio image shows an asymmetric structure with the jet-linked southeastern lobe while no counter jet for the north side lobe (Fernini 2014).

Similar to 3C 275.1, in the ray analysis we find a point-like significant ray excess ( TS 47 ) around the radio position of 3C 14. Since 3C 14 is also a high Galactic latitude ( l 49^ ) source and the nearest ray source is 2.3^ away, we re-fit the 6-year LAT data in the energy range from 800 MeV to 100 GeV and the central excess is still significant (TS 25 ), suggesting that the excess is intrinsic.

Indeed, the new ray position is found to be R.A. 9.2736^ and DEC. 18.5441^ , with a 95 C.L. error radius of 0.108^ . Surprisingly, 3C 14 falls out from location radius (see Figure 5). On the other hand, the flat spectral radio source, CRATES J0036+1832 is still within the location radius. It is thus reasonably to speculate that the counterpart of the ray source is CRATES J0036+1832 and 3C 14 is probably not able to radiate significant ray emission.

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 14 as a red radio-loud quasar with a 308 kpc jet, broken-power-law radio emission, negligible submm/FIR non-thermal contribution, and moderate torus obscuration.

quotes.quote: -3C 14 is a red quasars Smith1980 with less than 2 core contribution at 5 GHz and an extended radio jet of 308 kpc. The radio emission is best described with a broken power-law with an exponential cutoff at FIR wavelengths, consistent with the multiple radio structures present in MERLIN 18 cm data Akujor1994 . The SED fitting shows that the non-thermal emission contribution at submm/FIR wavelengths is negligible. The emission from the torus is best described with a combination of clumps with negligible opacity, a homogeneous disk with moderate optical depth and inclination angle of 67^ that result in moderate obscuration of the optical-UV emission. The optical-UV SED before obscuration correction (shown in black plus signs) is consistent with its classification as a red quasar.

gmw_00008972.name: 3C 43
gmw_00008972.overview: 3C 43 is a compact steep-spectrum quasar featuring a radio jet that spans less than 49 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2011.03130]. This jet exhibits a sharp, misaligned bend that likely indicates an interaction with the surrounding interstellar medium [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 43 as a CSS quasar with a compact jet, low core contribution, bent radio structure likely interacting with the ISM, and significant submillimeter non-thermal radiation.

quotes.quote: -3C 43 is a CSS quasar with a jet of <49 kpc and a core contribution of < 6 at 5 GHz Akujor1991,Ludke1998 . The radio emission is best described with a single power-law with an exponential cutoff at FIR wavelengths. indicate that this source has a misaligned and asymmetric structure with a sharp bent jet suggesting that the presence of the bent structure in this source could be due to the interaction of the jet with the ISM. The best-fit torus model is a combination of clumps with a small volume filling factor and a homogeneous disk with low opacity that result in a small amount of obscuration of the optical-UV emission. As illustrated in Figure fig:sed the submm data is dominated by non-thermal radiation from the radio structures.

gmw_00008973.name: 3C 181
gmw_00008973.overview: The radio-loud quasar 3C 181 features a radio jet extending 65 kpc [arXiv:2011.03130]. Its radio emission is characterized by a single power-law with an exponential cutoff, and its core contributes less than one percent of the emission at 5 GHz [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 181 with a 65 kpc radio jet, weak 5 GHz core, single-power-law radio emission, little optical-UV correction, and no detected underlying non-thermal X-ray component.

quotes.quote: -3C 181 has a radio jet of 65 kpc with < 1 core contribution at 5 GHz Mantovani1992 . The radio emission is best described with a single power-law with an exponential cutoff at 814 GHz. investigate the 850 band Scuba observation of this source and predict no non-thermal component contributing to this wavelength. The best-fit torus is a combination of clumps with a small filling factor and a homogeneous disk with moderate opacity which results in little correction of the optical-UV emission. Comparing the best-fit accretion disk with the X-ray data does not indicate the presence of an underlying non-thermal X-ray component.

gmw_00008974.name: 3C 190
gmw_00008974.overview: 3C 190 is a high-redshift, red radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of approximately 1.2 [arXiv:1910.06374, arXiv:2011.03130]. The galaxy hosts a deprojected radio jet spanning 124 kpc, a weak core, and multiple radio features that cut off at far-infrared wavelengths [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: 3C 190 is used as a high-redshift radio-quasar target for LOFAR RRL methods, with foreground RRLs detected but no significant signal at its systemic or absorption-feature redshifts.

quotes.quote: We observed the radio quasar 3C 190 ( z 1.2 ) with the LOFAR HBA. In reducing this data for spectroscopic analysis, we have placed special emphasis on bandpass calibration.

We demonstrate the utility of this method by applying it to existing low-frequency spectra of Cassiopeia A and M 82, and to the new observations of 3C 190. RRLs have been detected in the foreground of 3C 190 at z=1.12355 (assuming a carbon origin), owing to the first detection of RRLs outside of the local universe (first reported in Emig et al. 2019).

We ultimately find transition RRLs at z=1.12355 assuming carbon, as reported in . In Figure fig:3c190_cc , we also show the spectrum when extending the line-blanking region to 25 as in . We do not find a significant signal at the systemic redshift of 3C 190 or at the redshifts of the reported absorption features and place a 3 upper limit of 4.6 Hz for a line width of 7.5 .

sources.summary: The paper treats 3C 190 as a red CSS quasar with a 124 kpc deprojected jet, weak core, multiple radio features, FIR cutoff, and likely submillimeter non-thermal contamination.

quotes.quote: -3C 190 is a red quasar Ishwara2003 CSS and has a de-projected radio jet length of 124 kpc, and less than 1 core contribution at 5 GHz. The radio images Spencer1991,Ludke1998 indicate multiple features that require a double-power law in our fitting procedure (see Table tab:best_radio_fit ). As shown in Figure fig:sed , the radio emission cutoff occurs at FIR wavelengths. While this suggests that the non-thermal emission may contribute significantly to the submm wavelengths (hence SFR estimates based on submm data should be treated carefully), submm observations are required for constraining the radio fit. The emission from the torus is best described with a combination of clumps and a homogeneous disk with maximum acceptable opacity in the torus library (see Table tab:sieb_par ).

gmw_00008975.name: 3C 204
gmw_00008975.overview: 3C 204 is a radio-loud quasar featuring a bright radio core and a 543 kpc extended jet [arXiv:2011.03130]. This one-sided jet deflects near its end, and the system displays hot spots alongside perpendicular [O II] emission [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 204 as a radio-loud quasar with a 543 kpc jet, broken-power-law radio SED, bright core, hot spots, deflected one-sided jet, and perpendicular [O II] emission.

quotes.quote: -3C 204 has an extended jet of 543 kpc and 8 core contribution in the 5 GHz band. The quasar radio SED is well fitted with a broken power-law (see Table tab:best_radio_fit ). The VLA 4.9 GHz images show multiple features including a bright radio core, hot spots, and a one-sided jet that deflects towards the end Bridle1994 . There is some evidence of [ O 2 ] emission perpendicular to the jet axis Bremer1992,Bridle1994 at 90 kpc towards the north and 45 kpc towards the south. The emission from the torus is best described with a combination of clouds with negligible optical depth and a homogeneous disk with moderate opacity.

gmw_00008976.name: 3C 205
gmw_00008976.overview: 3C 205 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:2011.03130] that exhibits a low accretion rate [arXiv:1610.04221] and a 251 kpc extended radio structure [arXiv:2011.03130]. Its spectral energy distribution peak is dominated by a soft X-ray component [arXiv:1610.04221], and its torus provides only modest obscuration of the accretion disk [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 205 as a radio-loud quasar with 251 kpc extended radio structure, broken-power-law emission, modest accretion-disk obscuration, C IV absorption, and little non-thermal X-ray radiation.

quotes.quote: -3C 205 has an extended radio structure of 251 kpc with a core contribution of 3 in the 5GHz band. The radio emission is best described as a broken power-law (see Table tab:best_radio_fit ) which is consistent with the presence of the multiple radio structures Lonsdale1984,Lonsdale1986 . The emission from the torus is best described with a combination of clumps with small opacity and a homogeneous disk with moderate opacity which result in small amount of obscuration of the accretion disk radiation. 3C 205 spectra indicate the presence of a strong absorbing system (3.21 ) associated with C 4 absorption line Anderson1987 . The best-fitted accretion disk model predicts the presence of little non-thermal radiation at X-ray band.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0839 + 5754 as having a well-constrained SED peak dominated by the soft X-ray component, low accretion rate with strong narrow emission, and large linewidths treated as upper limits.

quotes.quote: In objects with well-constrained SED peaks, such as J0118 0052, this difference is smaller, and in J0839 + 5754 the difference is smallest of all, partly because the SED peak is dominated by the SX component, and therefore the peak temperature dependency on _BH is smaller.
In general, the lowest accretion rate objects show the strongest narrow emission lines (J0839 + 5754 and J2328 + 1500 are the clearest examples).

In two objects, J0839 + 5754 and J2328 + 1500, the full profile is not sampled by our data, and we measure very large linewidths. We therefore treat these results as upper limits, as we lack continuum measurements on either side of the emission line.

gmw_00008977.name: 3C 208
gmw_00008977.overview: 3C 208 is a radio-loud quasar at a redshift of z=1.112 that appears compact in infrared imaging [arXiv:1605.03196]. It hosts a 172 kpc radio jet and east-west radio lobes [arXiv:2011.03130, arXiv:1605.03196], with no optical evidence of nearby merging sources [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper models 3C 208 with a 172 kpc radio jet, broken-power-law emission, a mostly straight one-sided jet, little optical-UV obscuration, and no HST evidence for nearby mergers.

quotes.quote: -3C 208 has an extended radio jet of 172 kpc with 10 core contribution at 5 GHz. The radio emission is best fitted with a broken power-law that is consistent with the presence of multiple radio features including a bright core and the hot spots Bridle1994 . 3C 208 has a one-sided jet that is straight for most of its length but deflects towards the end Bridle1994 . The emission from the torus is best described with a combination of clumps with a small optical depth and homogeneous disk with moderate opacity which result in no significant obscuration at optical-UV wavelengths. The available HST images (F606W and F140W bands) do not indicate any evidence of nearby merging sources Hilbert2016 .

sources.summary: 3C 208 appears compact in IR imaging, lies in a source-rich field, has nearby optical counterparts, and shows radio core and east-west lobes.

quotes.quote: 3C 208 (z=1.112) In our IR image, 3C 208 appears as a compact object in our data, with 25-30 sources visible within 15 . The closest source is just over 1 northeast of the target. Most of these surrounding objects show a limited amount of signal in our optical image. There is corresponding radio emission coming from the core of the QSO, as well as lobes stretching roughly 6 east and west.

gmw_00008978.name: 4C 16.49
gmw_00008978.overview: The radio-loud quasar 4C 16.49 features a 216 kpc radio jet, a strong radio core, and a small counterjet [arXiv:2011.03130]. Its torus configuration results in negligible obscuration of the optical-UV emission from the accretion disk [arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: The paper models 4C 16.49 with a 216 kpc radio jet, strong core, jet and counterjet, double-power-law radio emission, little optical-UV obscuration, and possible non-thermal X-ray contribution.

quotes.quote: -4C 16.49 has a radio jet of 216 kpc with a 5 core contribution at 5 GHz. The radio images obtained with VLA at 2 and 6 cm show a strong radio core, jet and a small counter jet Lonsdale1993 . The radio emission is best described with a double power-law (see Table tab:best_radio_fit ). Due to the small number of reliable data points and lack of submm data we should be cautious in interpreting the optimization results and interpretation of the non-thermal contribution from the radio structure at shorter wavelengths. The best-fit torus model is a combination of clumps with negligible opacity and a homogeneous disk with the highest acceptable opacity in the torus library (see Table tab:sieb_par ).

This combination results in no significant obscuration of optical-UV emission from the accretion disk. The best-fitted accretion disk template also confirms the presence of significant non-thermal X-ray component.

gmw_00008979.name: 3C 432
gmw_00008979.overview: 3C 432 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z=1.785 [arXiv:1605.03196, arXiv:2011.03130]. It features a 155 kpc radio jet, bright hot spots, radio lobes, and extended narrow-line emission [arXiv:1605.03196, arXiv:2011.03130].

sources.summary: 3C 432 is an unresolved QSO with possible northwest narrow-line emission, several nearby sources, compact optical appearance, and radio lobes aligned with the IR feature.

quotes.quote: 3C 432 (z=1.785) 3C 432 appears in our IR data as an unresolved source within a field of objects. There is some dim extended emission, potentially a narrow line region, to the northwest of the QSO. There are also several distinct sources in the same direction as the extended emission, within 7.5 of the QSO. In addition a small source is located 2 to the northeast, and two extended sources are 4.9 to the south of the target. In our optical image, 3C 432 appears as a compact, isolated source.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3C 432 as a radio-loud quasar with a 155 kpc jet, lobes, bright hot spots, one-sided VLA jet, double-power-law radio structures, and possible photometric contamination.

quotes.quote: -3C 432 has jet of 155 kpc and core contribution of 3 at 5 GHz. The VLA images indicate multiple structures including radio lobes, bright hot spots, and a one-sided jet Bridle1994 . The emission from these radio structures is best described with a double power-law (Table tab:best_radio_fit ). The torus emission is best fitted with a combination of clumps with negligible opacity, and a homogeneous disk with high opacity that are viewed at 33^ and result in no significant obscuration of the accretion disk emission. The HST F606W and F140W images show an extended narrow line regions (within 8" ) along the direction of the radio lobes Hilbert2016 with several faint sources within this radius which could potentially contaminate the quasar SED.

gmw_00008980.name: Cas I
gmw_00008980.overview: Cas I is a Local Volume dwarf irregular galaxy [arXiv:2312.08108]. It has a disputed distance, with estimates placing it either at 1.6 Mpc or near 3.65 Mpc in the environs of IC342 [arXiv:2312.08108].

sources.summary: The paper treats Cas I as a Local Volume dwarf irregular with disputed TRGB distances, measures two HII-region oxygen abundances and extinction, and favors a distance near 3.65 Mpc in the IC342 environs.

quotes.quote: Cas I is a Local Volume (LV) dwarf irregular galaxy with a wide range of suggested distances. Tikhonov (2019), using the HST images and the Tip of Red Giant Branch (TRGB) method, places Cas I at D = 1.6 0.1 Mpc. Besides, he estimates the stellar metallicity of Cas I at the level of z 0.0004 (or Z(stars) Z /50). Such a nearby extremely low-metallicity dwarf, if real, would be a very valuable object for detailed studies.

We use the SAO 6-m telescope (BTA) spectroscopy to estimate gas metallicity in two representative \ regions in Cas I and to derive, via their observed Balmer decrements, the independent upper limit to the value of the MW extinction. We derive the values of 12+log(O/H) = 7.83 0.1 dex and 7.58 0.1 dex in two \ regions of Cas I, which correspond to Z(gas) of 5--10 times higher than Z(stars) estimated for its resolved stars. The measured Balmer decrements in these \ regions, result in the maximal MW extinction in this direction of A _ B = 3.06 0.14 mag in comparison to A _ B = 3.69 0.4 derived via the IR dust emission and is used in other estimates of the distance to Cas I.
The combination of the above distance estimates, accounting for their uncertainties, results in the most probable value of D 3.65 Mpc, what favours Cas I to reside in the environs of IC342.

gmw_00008981.name: NGC 6146
gmw_00008981.overview: NGC 6146 is an early-type elliptical galaxy with no signs of a recent merger [arXiv:1702.02945]. It exhibits no current active galactic nucleus activity [arXiv:1912.06451], has only upper limits on molecular gas [arXiv:1702.02945], and displays irregular warm-gas kinematics at large radii [arXiv:1912.06451].

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 6146 as a non-current-AGN case whose warm-gas kinematics become irregular at large radii and whose H flux map has central and northeast cone-like emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 6146 (No current AGN activity) NGC 6146 is irregular with respect to \ at radii 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, and 15 (corresponding to 13 5, 15 1, 16 6, 18 1, 19 6, 21 1, and 22 6, respectively). It is irregular with respect to \ at radii 11, 12, 13, and 14 (16 6, 18 1, 19 6, and 21 1), and its \ value is regular. Its H flux map exhibits high flux in the center as well as in a faint cone-like feature extending toward the north-east. Here, \ and \ follow the opposite of the trend from NGC 0932, in that their values are low in the center and rise toward the galaxy's edge. Perhaps here these two parameters are reacting more to the absence of warm gas than to disruption caused by stellar or AGN feedback.

sources.summary: The paper highlights NGC 6146 as the sample's sole non-merger early-type/elliptical galaxy, with no CO detection, upper-limit molecular gas, estimated H I mass, largest stellar mass, and lowest H flux.

quotes.quote: On six cases (NGC 3687, UGC 08250, NGC 5682, UGC10331, NGC 6063, and NGC 6146) the line is not detected and only upper limits on the presence of cold gas are reported.

In our sample we only have one early-type galaxy with no recent merger, NGC 6146. It has the largest stellar mass (11.27 dex) but does not seem to have CO present in EDGE maps (only has an upper limit for the molecular mass, 9.35 dex) and H I mass was estimated via average density (9.39 dex).
In our sample there is only 1 elliptical galaxy (NGC 6146), which shows the lowest H flux as expected.

gmw_00008982.name: UGC 08250
gmw_00008982.overview: UGC 08250 is a galaxy that has hosted a Type Ia supernova [arXiv:1702.02945]. It is characterized by a stellar mass that is smaller than its gas mass, and observations have yielded only upper limits on its cold molecular gas [arXiv:1702.02945].

sources.summary: The paper reports no CO detection for UGC 08250, identifies it as the lightest type Ia supernova host in the sample, and suggests its molecular-gas estimate may be overpredicted.

quotes.quote: On six cases (NGC 3687, UGC 08250, NGC 5682, UGC10331, NGC 6063, and NGC 6146) the line is not detected and only upper limits on the presence of cold gas are reported.

All galaxies have larger stellar than gas masses, with the exception of NGC 4961 and UGC 08250 which are in turn the lightest galaxies in each SN subsample, Ibc and Ia respectively. This may indicate that these galaxies have still to process most of its mass into stars, while for UGC 08250 it may also be that the estimation of the M _ mol may be over-predicting the amount of molecular gas.

gmw_00008983.name: SMG02
gmw_00008983.overview: The submillimeter galaxy SMG02 is classified as an active galactic nucleus-dominated system [arXiv:1403.2994]. High-resolution radio observations reveal two distinct components [arXiv:1004.0009], and the galaxy likely hosts an undermassive central black hole [arXiv:1004.0009].

sources.summary: The paper includes LOCK850.04 among multiple-counterpart SMGs whose spectroscopic counterpart matches the infrared luminosity, then classifies it as AGN-dominated and excludes it for large power-law uncertainties.

quotes.quote: Four sources (LESS10, LOCK850.03, LOCK850.04 and LOCK850.15) have multiple robust counterparts for which only one counterpart has a spectroscopic redshift. Following M12 we include these galaxies in our sample, as the 24 m and radio flux densities of the spectroscopic counterpart agree with the infrared luminosity computed from the FIR-submillimetre flux densities. This supports the assumption that the submillimetre emission originates from one counterpart.

We find 6/34 galaxies are classed as AGN in this way (AzLOCK.01, AzLOCK.10, AzTECJ100019+023206, LOCK850.04, LOCK850.15 and GN20 Although the observed S_ 8.0 /S_ 4.5 colour traces the rest-frame 1.6 m stellar bump at z 4 , we retain GN20 in our AGN sample as Riechers13 found that GN20 has an obscured AGN from power-law emission in the rest-frame MIR spectrum. ). Following the method of Hainline11 , we subtract a power-law with f_ ^ , where =2 or 3, from all photometry shortwards of 8 m (observed), incrementally adjusting the power-law contribution at 8 m to achieve the best-fit. In the following results we use the best-fit power-law subtracted values for the four AGN with weak power law components (AzLOCK.1, AzLOCK.10, LOCK850.15 and GN20). We use the results derived using a power-law slope of =3 as this provides the best-fit to the data, except for the case of GN20 where the data is best-fit by a power-law slope of =2 . We exclude AzTECJ100019+023206 as the lack of reliable photometry makes the AGN power-law fraction difficult to constrain, and LOCK850.04 is excluded as the uncertainties on the parameters due to subtraction of the dominant power-law are too large to make this galaxy a useful member of the sample.

sources.summary: SMG02 has two MERLIN components but no EVN detection; the authors derive only an SMBH mass upper limit and suggest its black hole is likely undermassive.

quotes.quote: SMG02: The MERLIN map shows two distinct components, but neither are detected in the EVN image.

At the same time, for the non-detections (SMG10, SMG01, SMG02, and SMG04), we can utilise the observed correlation to derive upper limits on M_ BH for these systems. This again requires we assume that no radio-quiet AGN are present, and that emission from large-scale jets is both negligible in these systems and in driving the observed M_ BH L_ 5GHz correlation, and therefore represents a fairly rough estimate. However, keeping these assumptions in mind we find the 3-- detections for SMG10, SMG01, SMG02 and SMG04 imply upper limits of log (M_ BH / M _ ) 8.1, 8.2, 7.5 and 7.6, respectively.
In only one case (SMG02) does it appear likely that the black hole is undermassive compared to the theoretical relation.

gmw_00008984.name: SMG04
gmw_00008984.overview: The high-redshift galaxy SMG04 is dominated by a single MERLIN radio component that is slightly elongated in the north-south direction [arXiv:1004.0009]. It lacks a strong VLBI detection, which provides a rough upper limit for its supermassive black hole mass [arXiv:1004.0009].

sources.summary: SMG04 is dominated by a slightly north-south elongated MERLIN component, has no significant VLBI detection, and receives only a rough SMBH mass upper limit.

quotes.quote: SMG04: The MERLIN map is dominated by a single component which is elongated slightly, north-south. There is no significant detection in the VLBI map, although we note that there is weak (3- i.e. \ 45 Jy beam ^ 1 peak) emission in the EVN map close to the expected position of the source.

At the same time, for the non-detections (SMG10, SMG01, SMG02, and SMG04), we can utilise the observed correlation to derive upper limits on M_ BH for these systems. This again requires we assume that no radio-quiet AGN are present, and that emission from large-scale jets is both negligible in these systems and in driving the observed M_ BH L_ 5GHz correlation, and therefore represents a fairly rough estimate. However, keeping these assumptions in mind we find the 3-- detections for SMG10, SMG01, SMG02 and SMG04 imply upper limits of log (M_ BH / M _ ) 8.1, 8.2, 7.5 and 7.6, respectively.

gmw_00008985.name: SMG06
gmw_00008985.overview: SMG06, also known as SMMJ10521 + 5719, is a high-redshift galaxy with a highly-inverted radio spectrum [arXiv:1004.0009] and PAH features [arXiv:astro-ph/0701816]. It exhibits radio variability [arXiv:1004.0009] and contains a highly obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1004.0009] that may be a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0701816].

sources.summary: SMG06 is robustly detected by EVN, unresolved with variable excess flux, has an inverted radio spectrum, and may be a highly obscured AGN-dominated system rather than a starburst galaxy.

quotes.quote: SMG06: This source is detected robustly by the EVN, is found to be unresolved by jmfit and has a flux density that is significantly in excess of the VLA value, presumably due to the source variability first noted by ivison2002 . The MERLIN image is slightly resolved, but has a peak flux density (296 Jy beam ^ 1 ) that is similar to the EVN value. SMG06 has a highly-inverted radio spectrum, = +0.83 , as also noted by ivison2002 .

It is also possible that the clearest case of an AGN detection in our sample, SMG06, may not actually be a starburst galaxy at all, but a highly-obscured AGN-dominated system. The presence of an AGN is virtually guaranteed by the source variability that results in the EVN flux being in excess of that detected by the VLA, but the large EVN flux also leaves very little or no flux that might originate from a powerful starburst. In this scenario, the slight resolution of the MERLIN image might be due to a radio jet that is resolved out by the EVN beam although we stress that the evidence for resolution in the MERLIN image is slight, the total flux only exceeding the peak flux by 9 per cent. However, given that the source has clearly brightened since the VLA observations in 2001, simultaneous EVN and VLA imaging might have detected a clear excess of flux with the larger beam.

sources.summary: The paper says SMMJ10521 + 5719 shows PAH features and weak continuum, lacks optical AGN features, but has radio and X-ray evidence for a possible radio-loud quasar.

quotes.quote: SMMJ10521 + 5719 The spectrum of this source shows PAH features plus a weak continuum. The source does not contain AGN features in the optical spectrum chapman05 . The mid-infrared AGN continuum is detected but the feature-to-continuum ratio is much higher than in the cases of SMMJ02399 0136 and SMMJ09431 + 4700, thus suggesting a smaller AGN contribution. The presence of an AGN is confirmed as well from radio and X-ray emission that show the possible presence of a radio loud quasar ivison02 .

gmw_00008986.name: SMG10
gmw_00008986.overview: SMG10 is a system containing two galaxies separated by 5 kpc and a velocity of 400 km/s, which may represent the early stages of a merger [arXiv:1009.2495]. High-resolution radio observations reveal two components that could indicate either a late-stage merger or a radio jet [arXiv:1004.0009].

sources.summary: SMG10 is the brightest radio source in the sample but undetected by EVN; MERLIN shows two components that may indicate a jet or late-stage merger, with an SMBH mass upper limit.

quotes.quote: SMG10: Despite being the brightest radio source in our sample (VLA flux density = 295 Jy), SMG10 is not detected by our EVN observations. An examination of the MERLIN map shows that this source contains two components, although the brighter of the two dominates, with a peak flux density of 157 Jy beam ^ 1 . It is possible that the weaker of the two components represents a jet, but it could equally be one component of a late-stage merger. The radio spectral index of the entire system, =-0.69 , is consistent with an optically-thin radio jet or a canonical starburst Ibar2010 . As noted by ivison2007c , matched high-resolution imaging at multiple frequencies are required to disentangle the various emission components in sources like SMG10.

At the same time, for the non-detections (SMG10, SMG01, SMG02, and SMG04), we can utilise the observed correlation to derive upper limits on M_ BH for these systems. This again requires we assume that no radio-quiet AGN are present, and that emission from large-scale jets is both negligible in these systems and in driving the observed M_ BH L_ 5GHz correlation, and therefore represents a fairly rough estimate. However, keeping these assumptions in mind we find the 3-- detections for SMG10, SMG01, SMG02 and SMG04 imply upper limits of log (M_ BH / M _ ) 8.1, 8.2, 7.5 and 7.6, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SMM J105141 as a two-galaxy system separated in velocity and projected distance, with resolved line profiles and ordered rotation in the northeast component.

quotes.quote: SMM J105141 (z = 1.21) -- There are two galaxies separated in velocity by 400 km s ^ 1 and spatially by 5 kpc, with no indications of disturbances. The south-west galaxy is about a factor 3 less luminous than its companion, but nearly equal in dynamical mass. The line profiles are well-resolved. The velocity field of the north-east system is indicative of ordered rotation, with velocities ranging from 60 to 180 km s ^ 1 .
Again we find that generally _ int /v_ obs > 1.5. It is likely that we are seeing two galaxies engaged in the early stages of merging, appearing close only in projection.

gmw_00008987.name: SMG11
gmw_00008987.overview: SMG11 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:1004.0009] located at an assumed redshift of 2.4 [arXiv:1004.0009]. Radio observations reveal a marginally resolved structure with a spatial extent of approximately 165 parsecs along its north-south axis [arXiv:1004.0009].

sources.summary: SMG11 is detected by EVN at lower significance than SMG06, recovers only 38 percent of the VLA flux, and shows resolved MERLIN and marginally resolved EVN structure.

quotes.quote: SMG11: This source is also detected by the EVN, but at a considerably lower significance than SMG06, partly due to the primary-beam attenuation. Even taking this into account, only 38 per cent of the VLA flux is recovered. The MERLIN image is dominated by a single (resolved) component with a peak flux density of 209 Jy beam ^ 1 . The EVN detection is also marginally resolved, approximately along the north-south axis. The deconvolved size along this axis is 20 mas which is equivalent to a spatial extent of 165 pc at the assumed redshift of 2.4 for this source.

gmw_00008988.name: SDSS J004617.02+252041.5
gmw_00008988.overview: SDSS J004617.02+252041.5 is a strong gravitational lensing candidate galaxy [arXiv:1909.08715]. Imaging of the system reveals a single clearly distorted source galaxy image, while its counter-image is likely obscured by the central lens galaxy [arXiv:1909.08715].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this SDSS object as a strong-lensing candidate in CFHT images, with one distorted source image and GALFIT/SIS modeling used to estimate the unresolved counter-image.

quotes.quote: From these images, we can conclude that SDSS J012832.31 023500.0, SDSS J012903.47+035901.7 and SDSS J015102.97+042013.1 are not strong lensing systems, and SDSS J004617.02+252041.5 or SDSS J231904.19+193242.4 are strong lensing candidates. In the images of these two targets, we clearly detect one distorted source galaxy image, but their counter images are probably out-shined by the central galaxy. Further investigation will be discussed in the next section.

As discussed in Section sec:cfht , due to limited seeing, none of the 5 systems have an image with clear separation. However, for 2 of them, SDSS J004617.02+252041.5 and SDSS J231904.19+193242.4, we capture one clearly separated source image, which could reveal more information about the system and provide us with a reasonable estimation of the position of the counter-image. Using GALFIT 2010AJ....139.2097P , we successfully removed the lens galaxies for these two systems and obtained the position of the visible source image with fitting. With a simple Singular Isothermal Sphere (SIS) model, we estimate the separation of their counter-image to their respective central galaxy, shown in tab.lens_info .

gmw_00008989.name: SDSS J231904.19+193242.4
gmw_00008989.overview: SDSS J231904.19+193242.4 is a candidate strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1909.08715]. Imaging reveals a single clearly distorted source galaxy, while its counter-image is likely obscured by the light of the central lens galaxy [arXiv:1909.08715].

sources.summary: The paper identifies this SDSS object as a strong-lensing candidate in CFHT images, with one distorted source image and GALFIT/SIS modeling used to estimate the unresolved counter-image.

quotes.quote: From these images, we can conclude that SDSS J012832.31 023500.0, SDSS J012903.47+035901.7 and SDSS J015102.97+042013.1 are not strong lensing systems, and SDSS J004617.02+252041.5 or SDSS J231904.19+193242.4 are strong lensing candidates. In the images of these two targets, we clearly detect one distorted source galaxy image, but their counter images are probably out-shined by the central galaxy. Further investigation will be discussed in the next section.

As discussed in Section sec:cfht , due to limited seeing, none of the 5 systems have an image with clear separation. However, for 2 of them, SDSS J004617.02+252041.5 and SDSS J231904.19+193242.4, we capture one clearly separated source image, which could reveal more information about the system and provide us with a reasonable estimation of the position of the counter-image. Using GALFIT 2010AJ....139.2097P , we successfully removed the lens galaxies for these two systems and obtained the position of the visible source image with fitting. With a simple Singular Isothermal Sphere (SIS) model, we estimate the separation of their counter-image to their respective central galaxy, shown in tab.lens_info .

gmw_00008990.name: VCC1833
gmw_00008990.overview: VCC1833 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy that exhibits traces of recent star formation episodes [arXiv:2308.16768]. Its inner regions are dominated by young, metal-rich stellar populations, which transition to older, metal-poor populations at larger galactocentric distances [arXiv:2308.16768].

sources.summary: VCC1833 has young, metal-rich central populations that become older and metal-poor outward, shows a significant positive age gradient, and is one of the galaxies with recent star-formation traces.

quotes.quote: VCC1833: This galaxy is dominated by young, metal-rich, and less enhanced stellar populations in its inner regions, which grow older and metal-poor at larger galactocentric distances. We do not see a clear variation in [ /Fe ] values across this galaxy.
We detect significant positive age gradients in three dEs of our sample, namely VCC0170, VCC0407 and VCC1833.

From this work, we conclude that: Except for VCC0170, VCC0407 and VCC1833, the main sample dEs show flat age radial profiles with negligible fluctuations in value (-0.02 < _ Age < 0.12). Among the exceptions here VCC0170 is still actively forming stars in its inner regions and the other two show traces of recent star formation episodes.

gmw_00008991.name: HE 0435-5304
gmw_00008991.overview: HE 0435-5304 is a quasar and an ultraluminous infrared galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.428 [arXiv:2201.04685]. This relatively massive galaxy hosts an active galactic nucleus and is actively forming stars [arXiv:2201.04685].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HE 0435-5304 as a quasar/ULIRG at redshift 0.42788, deriving AGN properties and host parameters that indicate an actively star-forming massive galaxy.

quotes.quote: Received 30.03.2021; accepted 26.11.2021 HE 0435-5304 from Hamburg European Southern Observatory survey (Hamburg-ESO, HE) is a quasar that appears in the literature with two conflicting redshift values: 1.2 and 0.4 . It was used in the studies of the intergalactic medium through fitting of the narrow absorption lines in its ultraviolet (UV) spectrum. This source is also known historically as a luminous infrared galaxy (LIRG). We present optical spectra of HE 0435-5304, aiming to precisely measure its redshift and to study its physical properties. In particular, properties of its active nucleus, which is studied in the context of the source being identified here as an ultra-luminous infrared galaxy (ULIRG), allow us to place this quasar in the context of the general population.

Host galaxy The main physical properties of HE 0435-5304 based on the CIGALE SED fitting procedure are listed in Fig. tab:cigale , while Fig. fig:cigale shows the final fit of the SED. We find that HE 0435-5304 is an ULIRG with total dust luminosity ( L_ IR total ^ * ) calculated as the sum of the dust luminosity (L _ dust ) and the AGN luminosity emitted at IR wavelengths (L _ dust AGN ) equal to 10^ 12.14 0.11 L _ . The galaxy is relatively massive ( 10^ 10.12 0.62 M _ ) and is actively star forming at a rate of SFR=48.90 12.78 M _ /yr, with a specific SFR (defined as SFR over M _ star ) of log SSFR=-8.44 Myr ^ 1 .

gmw_00008992.name: DRP-A539a
gmw_00008992.overview: DRP-A539a is an extremely compact, young star-forming dwarf galaxy located within the Abell 539 cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0611067]. It is likely falling into the cluster core for the first time, where its gas is being compressed by the hot intracluster medium [arXiv:astro-ph/0611067].

sources.summary: The paper describes DRP-A539a as an extremely compact, young star-forming dwarf in Abell 539, likely an infalling object whose gas was compressed by the hot intracluster medium.

quotes.quote: The projected distance of DRP-A539a from the center of A539 is 300 kpc, which corresponds to 0.2 r_ 200 . This short distance together with the measured radial velocity of DRP-A539a, ensures that this object is well within the region defined by the caustic of the cluster (see Figure 2 of rin03 In the particular case of A539, the caustic appears well defined and any possible contamination is low. However, these authors remark that although the caustic separates cluster members from foreground and background galaxies in a more efficient way than the velocity sigma clipping, still some interloppers may lie within the caustics. ). The existence of such an active star forming object at such a short distance from the center of a cluster is unusual and opens several questions about its nature and origin.

Nonetheless, DRP-A539a does not appear to be associated to any gas-rich bright galaxy, suggesting that either the parent molecular cloud is the result of an ancient episode of gas stripping, where the stripped galaxy is already far away, or rather we are facing a star forming dwarf galaxy (SFDG) falling for the first time into the cluster core. Theoretical models predict that SFDGs lose their external gas very rapidly when they enter the cluster potential well, due to the ram pressure exerted by the ICM mor00 and also, according to bek03 ,the timescale for transformation of gas into stars due to ICM pressure is of the order of 10 Myr; for this reason we argue that DRP-A539a is in the very early stages of the infall process.

gmw_00008993.name: DRP-A634a
gmw_00008993.overview: DRP-A634a is a compact starburst galaxy that is likely a member of the Abell 634 cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0611067]. Its intense star formation activity may be the result of a preprocessing interaction with the galaxy LSB-A634a [arXiv:astro-ph/0611067].

sources.summary: The paper presents DRP-A634a as a compact starburst in Abell 634, likely a cluster member whose intense activity may come from interaction with LSB-A634a during preprocessing.

quotes.quote: The heliocentric velocity measured for DRP-A634a differs from the cluster heliocentric velocity by 1.34 _ A634 km s ^ 1 , and this object is located at a projected distance of 398 kpc from the cluster center. Both values, heliocentric velocity and projected distance, would place DRP-A634a within the caustic of every galaxy cluster presented in rin03 , at the 2 confidence level In the case the cluster center quoted by str99 is adopted, the corresponding projected distance would amount to 16 kpc and DRP-A634a will be a cluster member at a higher confidence level. ; Especially in the case of A194, a cluster presenting velocity dispersion and extension in the sky very similar to those of A634. The non detection in X-ray of A634 together with the substantial distance of DRP-A634a to the cluster center, may weaken the hypothesis that the strong star formation activity shown by this object could be the result of compression of molecular clouds due to the pressure exerted by the ICM.

Even though the LSB-A634a + DRP-A634a system is not likely to be a single galaxy, still both objects could be physically related. Under this assumption, we propose that the strong star formation activity shown by DRP-A634a is the result of the encounter between these objects. In fact, examples of recent star formation bursts associated to small groups of galaxies which are falling into a cluster have already been reported in the literature sak02,gav03,cor06 , and are thought to be associated to the so-called preprocessing of galaxies before entering the cluster environment.

gmw_00008994.name: TKRS4259
gmw_00008994.overview: TKRS4259 is a green-valley galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.4729 [arXiv:0907.0231]. Although classified as an early-type galaxy, it exhibits a disturbed morphology with a low surface brightness tidal feature, likely triggered by a merger event that caused a starburst approximately 1 Gyr ago [arXiv:0907.0231].

sources.summary: The paper identifies TKRS4259 as a disturbed green-valley foreground galaxy; its halo Mg II gas likely originated from material driven or stripped during a merger-triggered starburst.

quotes.quote: Although a Gini/M20 analysis (J. Lotz, 2008, private communication) indicates TKRS4259 is an early-type galaxy, it shows signs of a disturbed morphology or dust lane in all of the HST/ACS bands (i.e., a low surface brightness tidal feature; Figure fig.im ). Both its location in the CMD and its morphology indicate the galaxy may be transitioning from a previously star-forming galaxy to a fully quenched object. TKRS galaxies of similar M_B and redshift have approximately solar oxygen abundance or greater KK2004 .

Using multiwavelength analysis of the luminous components of a galaxy at z = 0.4729 in concert with spectroscopy of a bright, close transverse background galaxy at z = 0.6942 , we demonstrate that the cool gas traced by Mg 2 absorption in the foreground galaxy halo at impact parameter 16.5 h_ 70 ^ 1 kpc most likely originated in the ISM of the host galaxy or its progenitors. This galaxy has little ongoing star formation and exhibits only weak AGN activity; however, it experienced a starburst 1 Gyr ago, which may have been triggered by a merger event. The cool halo gas could have easily been driven or stripped away from the star-forming regions during this past violent phase, and could have survived in the hot halo since that time.

gmw_00008995.name: G 10-1
gmw_00008995.overview: G 10-1 is a DYNAMO galaxy located at a redshift between 0.075 and 0.15 [arXiv:1405.7410]. It is classified as a perturbed rotator that may be experiencing a merger, and it exhibits a molecular gas fraction of 0.22 [arXiv:1405.7410].

sources.summary: The paper observes G 10-1 as a DYNAMO galaxy with z=0.075-0.15 sample context, high star formation, perturbed-rotator classification, and a measured molecular gas fraction of 0.22.

quotes.quote: The systems observed were D 13-5, G 04-1, G 10-1 and H 10-2. Stellar masses for these systems range from 1-7 10^ 10 M _ and redshifts span the range z=0.075-0.15 . Star formation rates for D 13-5, G 10-1 and H 10-2 are 20 10 M _ yr ^ 1 . The star formation rate of G 04-1 is somewhat higher at 50 10 M _ yr ^ 1 . Galaxies G 04-1 and D 13-5 are classified as rotating disks, while H 10-2 and G 10-1 are classified as a perturbed rotators due to asymmetries in their velocity fields, and may be experiencing merging.
The DYNAMO galaxies have f_ gas =0.18 0.07 , 0.31 0.03 and 0.22 0.21 for D 13-5, G 04-1, and G 10-1 respectively.

gmw_00008996.name: XID 403
gmw_00008996.overview: XID 403 is a luminous X-ray source located at a redshift of z=1.608 [arXiv:2303.03438]. Its high rest-frame X-ray luminosity indicates supermassive black hole accretion, suggesting the source is either an active galactic nucleus or associated with a tidal disruption event [arXiv:2303.03438].
