quotes.quote: Target selection and properties We obtained NOEMA observations of three sources, A1703-zD1 Bradley12, Smit14 , EGS-5711424617 and EGS-1952445714 hereafter EGS-5711 and EGS-1952 respectively; Smit15 .

These galaxies were initially selected with the Lyman break technique as HST /F814W drop-out galaxies and subsequently identified as sources with blue Spitzer /IRAC [ 3.6 ] - [ 4.5 ] colours, implying high equivalent width [ O iii ] +H emission Smit14,Smit15 . The spectral energy distribution (SED) for each galaxy is shown in Appendix app:A . We selected these targets due to their observed brightness ( m_ UV 24 -- 25), but also as they are representative of `normal' star-forming galaxies (SFR < 100 M _ yr ^ 1 ) at z 7, with intrinsic (corrected for lensing magnification, A1703-zD1 is magnified 9 ) UV SFRs of 5 -- 38 M _ yr ^ 1 .

For EGS-1952 we find no evidence for [ C ii ] with S/N>3, co-spatial with the source. Spectra at the location of the source can be found in the appendix (Figures fig:EGS1952_LSB , fig:EGS1952_USB .) Further, we find no continuum signal for both EGS-5711 and EGS-1952 and as a result, we only provide upper limits for the continuum flux and an upper limit estimate for the [ C ii ] line flux, based on the spectral line obtained for A1703-zD1, scaled to the median rms of the EGS-5711 and EGS-1952 datacubes.

gmw_00009047.name: FAST J0139+4328
gmw_00009047.overview: FAST J0139+4328 is an isolated dark dwarf disk galaxy with a redshift of z=0.0083 [arXiv:2302.02646]. It has no optical counterpart, exhibits rotation-like gas kinematics, and is dominated by dark matter [arXiv:2302.02646].

sources.summary: The paper reports FAST J0139+4328 as an isolated dark dwarf disk galaxy without an optical counterpart, with rotation-like gas kinematics and a high dynamical-to-baryonic mass ratio.

quotes.quote: Based on a new survey using the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST), combined with the Pan-STARRS1 images, we identified an isolated cloud without any optical counterpart, named FAST J0139+4328. The newly discovered cloud appears to be a typical disk galaxy since it has a double-peak shape in the global profile and an S-like rotation structure in the velocity-position diagram.

Furthermore, we obtained that the mass of this galaxy is (8.3 1.7) 10^ 7 , and the dynamical mass to total baryonic mass ratio is 47 27, implying that dark matter dominates over baryons in FAST J0139+4328. These findings provide observational evidence that FAST J0139+4328 is an isolated dark dwarf galaxy with a redshift of z=0.0083 .

When compared to other gas-dominated galaxies, FAST J0139+4328 nearly follows the best-fit relation, implying that this galaxy is also a normal disk galaxy. Nevertheless, the disk galaxy FAST J0139+4328 has no optical counterpart, so the internal extinction from dust should be relatively minimal.

gmw_00009048.name: d0958+66
gmw_00009048.overview: The blue compact dwarf galaxy d0958+66 is a low-HI-mass member of the M81 group [arXiv:1207.3023]. It features a face-on neutral hydrogen disk, with offset peaks observed between its far-ultraviolet and H-alpha emissions [arXiv:1207.3023].

sources.summary: d0958+66 is one of the newly detected low-HI-mass M81-group BCDs; its HI disk is face-on, with offset FUV and emission peaks and matched recalibrated SFRs.

quotes.quote: A few things are apparent from the figures: (i) although the star forming disc is at the central peak of the HI profile when the HI is imaged at a low resolution (Figure fig:uv5ov ), the peaks of FUV and emission don't concur with the peaks of the HI emission when HI is images at high resolution. (ii) There are offsets between the peaks of the \ emission and that of the FUV emission, these are most pronounced in d0958+66 and d1028+70. (iii) The stellar disc as appears in the RGB images is better traced by the FUV emission.
The recalibrated SFR estimates match quite well for d0958+66, but for the other two BCDs, the SFR as derived from is approximately twice the SFR estimated using FUV flux.

As those authors emphasize, stochastic sampling of the IMF could occasionally result in small clusters with a disproportionate number of massive stars (which otherwise would be evidence for a ``top heavy'' IMF), and this appears to be the case here, especially for the galaxy d0958+66.

gmw_00009049.name: Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy
gmw_00009049.overview: The Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy is a Milky Way satellite located at a heliocentric distance of approximately 86 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1802.01581]. It is characterized by an old, metal-poor stellar population and hosts an extended distribution of RR Lyrae stars in its outskirts [arXiv:1802.01581].

sources.summary: The paper matches an RR Lyrae overdensity to Sextans, gives its coordinates, distance, stellar population, size, and adds 65 candidate members in its outskirts.

quotes.quote: The position of these stars, both in equatorial coordinates and distance, matches the location of the Sextans dwarf spheroidal galaxy R.A. = 153.2512^ , DEC = 1.6147^ , d_ H = 86 4 kpc, McCon12 . Sextans is a Milky Way satellite discovered by Irwin1990 with an absolute magnitude of M_V = -9.3 . The system is characterized by a relatively old and metal poor population (age= 12 Gyr; [ Fe/H ] = 1.9 ), as described by Mateo91 and Kirby2011 , respectively. The Sextans dwarf is a relatively extended satellite with a half-light radius r_h of 695 pc Irwin95, Roderick2016 and a surface brightness of 28 mag arcsec ^ 2 , typical of Local Group dSph galaxies.

From our sample, 65 of the RRL candidates are located within 1.75 degrees of Sextans, and between heliocentric distances of 76 and 90 kpc ( 48 ab's, 17 's). Their distribution, as well as the position of the HiTS fields are shown in Figure fig:sextans and Figure fig:colordistancias , in different scales. Figure fig:sextans also shows an ellipse marking the tidal radius from a King profile of the galaxy Roderick2016 and known RRLs in the galaxy from the literature. As can be seen in the figure, the HiTS fields do not cover the center of Sextans but only the outskirts of the dwarf.

gmw_00009050.name: UGC 4798
gmw_00009050.overview: UGC 4798 is a spiral galaxy located at a distance of approximately 119.2 Mpc [arXiv:2104.03723]. It is classified as an HI-rich galaxy with a star-formation rate of 1.97 solar masses per year, and it has hosted four reported supernovae [arXiv:2104.03723].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 4798 as SN 2019cad's spiral, HI-rich host, gives its distance, stellar mass, star-formation rate, supernova history, and central oxygen abundance.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of SN 2019cad was identified as UGC 4798, a spiral galaxy at a redshift of 0.0267 deVaucouleurs91 http://ned.ipac.caltech.edu/ . Adopting the recessional velocity corrected for Local Group infall into Virgo reported by HyperLEDA http://leda.univ-lyon1.fr ( Makarov14 ; v_ vir =8152 3 ), we obtain a distance modulus =35.38 0.13 mag, which is equivalent to a distance of 119.2 8.4 Mpc. SN 2019cad was located 3 6 east and 7 6 north from the galaxy centre. Its host has been classified as a H i rich galaxy Wang13G with a stellar mass of _ 10 M _* =10.59 \ Chen12G and a star-formation rate (SFR) of 1.97 0.01 yr ^ 1 Cormier16G . SN 2019cad is the fourth SN reported in UGC 4798.

To estimate the oxygen abundance of UGC 4798, we use the public spectrum of the central region of the galaxy obtained from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey SDSS; SDSS19 . Applying the O3N2 diagnostic method from Pettini04 , we obtain an oxygen abundance of 12+ O/H = 8.72 0.08 . Employing the same method, Modjaz11 reported an oxygen abundance at the position of SN 2005mf of 12+ O/H = 8.66 0.09 , which is around the solar abundance e.g. Pettini04 .

gmw_00009051.name: RX J1011.2+5545
gmw_00009051.overview: RX J1011.2+5545 is a narrow-line radio quasar located at a redshift of z=1.246 [arXiv:astro-ph/0303530]. It features a steep, moderately absorbed X-ray spectrum and a radio source resolved into a nucleus and lobes [arXiv:astro-ph/0303530].

sources.summary: The paper presents XMM-Newton and VLA observations of z=1.246 narrow-line radioquasar RX J1011.2+5545, finding a steep absorbed X-ray spectrum, radio nucleus plus lobes, and no detected reflection features.

quotes.quote: We present new XMM-Newton X-ray observations of the z=1.246 narrow-line radioquasar RX J1011.2+5545 serendipitously discovered by ROSAT . The flat X-ray spectrum previously measured by ROSAT and ASCA is shown to be the result of a steep 1.8 power law spectrum seen through a moderate intrinsic absorbing column ( N_ H 4 10^ 21 ). The position of the X-ray source is entirely coincident with the nucleus of the radio source that we have resolved in new sensitive VLA observations at 3.6 and 6 cm, implying that scattering in the radio lobes is not responsible for the bulk of X-ray emission.
No reflection features are detected in the X-ray spectrum of the narrow-line radioquasar, although an Fe line with equivalent width of up to 600 eV cannot be ruled out.

gmw_00009052.name: UGC10806
gmw_00009052.overview: UGC10806 is a pseudoisolated galaxy with a radial velocity of +932 km/s [arXiv:0906.1493]. It has a physical companion, PGC 167108, located 11 kpc to its east, while two western objects are distant background galaxies [arXiv:0906.1493].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC10806 as a pseudoisolated galaxy with a nearby physical companion, while two projected western companions are distant background objects.

quotes.quote: Only 4 of the galaxies out of the 29, UGC3234, UGC10806, UGC11251, and UGC12504 have neighbors whose radial velocities lie with 200 km/s of the galaxy considered to be isolated. The mutual linear distances for these pairs are small, and equal to 47 kpc (UGC3234 and 0504+16), 11 kpc (UGC 10806 and PGC 167108), 159 kpc (UGC11251 and 1827+38), and 79 kpc (UGC 12504 and PGC 2773857), with an average value of 74 kpc. The closest example of these four cases, the galaxy UGC10806 with a radial velocity of +932 km/s, is shown in Fig. 2 which reproduces the 8' 15' neighborhood of the pseudoisolated galaxy from the Palomar survey of the sky (POSS-II, R). The two faint "companions" to the west of UGC10806 have radial velocities of +7354 and +7569 km/s; that is, they are objects in the distant background. The physical companion to the east of UGC10806 has a velocity of +936 km/s.

gmw_00009053.name: NGC 3934
gmw_00009053.overview: NGC 3934 is a disk galaxy characterized by a dust-lane structure and a wide type-II shell structure [arXiv:1108.5879]. It is a rare example of a shell galaxy located in a likely dense galaxy region and is believed to be the product of a wet major merger [arXiv:1108.5879].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies NGC 3934 as a dust-lane disk and rare shell galaxy in a dense region, arguing simulations support a wet major-merger origin.

quotes.quote: We do not find evidence that NGC 3934 is a polar-ring galaxy, as suggested by the literature, but find that it is a disk galaxy with a prominent dust-lane structure and a wide type-II shell structure. NGC 3934 is a quite rare example of a shell galaxy in a likely dense galaxy region. The comparison between physically motivated SPH simulations with multi-band integrated photometry suggests that NGC 3934 is the product of a major merger.

On the other hand NGC 3934 appears to be the result of a major merger (see also Weil Hernquist 1993), whose final phase began 3 Gyr ago. Our simulation points at a "wet' merger, i.e. a merger where dissipation plays a meaningful role on the evolution, as observations suggest. Moreover, the simulation performed is able to account for both the whole SED of this galaxy from the UV to FIR spectral range, and for the morphology, including the shell structure and the distribution of the UV emission.

gmw_00009054.name: NGC 3933
gmw_00009054.overview: NGC 3933 is a spiral galaxy located within a galaxy group [arXiv:1108.5879]. Simulations of the galaxy indicate that it has experienced an unperturbed evolution, maintaining a star formation rate comparable to that of the Milky Way [arXiv:1108.5879].

sources.summary: The paper derives radio luminosity and star-formation rate for spiral NGC 3933 and finds a simulation matching an unperturbed evolution for the galaxy.

quotes.quote: From Condon et al. (1998) we derive for NGC 3934 F _ 1.4 =2.8 10^ 28 W m^ 2 Hz^ 1 corresponding with our adopted distance (see Table Table4 ) to a luminosity L _ 1.4 =8.34 10^ 21 W Hz^ 1 and for NGC 3933 F _ 1.4 =3.0 10^ 29 W m^ 2 Hz^ 1 , corresponding to a luminosity L _ 1.4 =8.94 10^ 20 W Hz^ 1 . The ratio L _ 1.4 (NGC3934)/L _ 1.4 (NGC 3933) 10 is the same as the one we found for the 60 m flux ratio. The 1.4GHz luminosity is insensitive to dust obscuration and for this reason is a good tracer of the star formation rate (SFR _ 1.4 ). We adopt the calibration of Hopkins et al. (2003) and we found an SFR=4.6 M _ yr ^ 1 for NGC 3934 and SFR=0.83 M _ yr ^ 1 for NGC 3933.
The simulation matching NGC 3933, a typical spiral in the group, is consistent with an unperturbed evolution of this system with an SFR similar to that of the Milky Way.

gmw_00009055.name: PGC 213894
gmw_00009055.overview: PGC 213894 is a spiral galaxy that belongs to the NGC 3933 group [arXiv:1108.5879]. Surface photometry indicates the galaxy contains both a bulge and a disk component, and its spectrum exhibits several emission lines [arXiv:1108.5879].

sources.summary: The paper adds PGC 213894 to the NGC 3933 group, measuring emission-line velocity and surface photometry that indicate a disk component despite a contaminating bright star.

quotes.quote: The spectrum of the spiral PGC 213894 shows the O [ III ] 5007, H_ and [ NII ] 6548-6583 emission lines. All emission lines were fitted with a Gaussian profile and the final measured galaxy radial velocity is the mean value with the error being the standard deviation. The velocity, corrected to the Sun, is V_r =3542 40 km sec ^ 1 , which is compatible with the average group velocity (see Table Table4 ) and implies that the galaxy is a member of the group.

We obtained the detailed surface photometry of both NGC 3934 and of the new member of this group, PGC 213894. In order to extract the effective radius _e and the effective surface brightness _e of these galaxies, we performed a best fit of their surface brightness profiles. The images, the luminosity, ellipticity, and position angle profiles suggest that these galaxies have a disk component in addition to the bulge. We then use a model composed of a bulge, following the ^1/4 law (de Vaucouleurs 1948), and an exponential disk (Freeman 1970). PGC 213894 has a bright star (SDSS J115212.60+165255.4) at 11 \ NE of the nucleus.

gmw_00009056.name: SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2
gmw_00009056.overview: SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2 is a galaxy located at a redshift of approximately z=0.115 [arXiv:1906.12236, arXiv:2109.01092]. Its nuclear region exhibits star-forming and H II diagnostics with no infrared indication of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2109.01092].

sources.summary: The paper associates a late-time X-ray source with SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2 at z=0.1146 and reports a power-law spectrum with luminosity near 3.2e42 erg/s.

quotes.quote: We scale the normalization of the power law model (that describes the background) on the basis of the ratio between the size of the source region and that of the background region. has a redshift of z=0.1146 (d _L=532.6 Mpc) and is associated with the galaxy SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2 ( 2014ApJ...793...38A ). Given the relatively high observed count rate of we investigate if the source spectrum is affected by pile--up by employing the ciao tool pileup map on an image created including all photon energies for both observations of . The count rate per frame in both observations is less than 0.02, implying a pile--up fraction lower than 1 . Therefore, we conclude that pile--up is insignificant for our observations of and by extension, given that the other sources we observed have a lower count rate per frame, those spectra are not affected by pile--up either.

Fitting the two observations together, the spectrum of is well--fit by a power law with index =2.5 0.1, with an unabsorbed 0.3--7 keV flux of (9.5 0.6) 10^ 14 translating to a 0.3--7 keV luminosity L _X=(3.2 0.2) 10^ 42 , where in the calculation of the luminosity uncertainty we, here and below, only included the uncertainty in the flux measurement and not that in the distance determination. The observed, absorbed, 0.3--7 keV flux is (8.5 0.5) 10^ 13 . The C-statistic of the fit was 226.6 for 223 bins and 221 degrees of freedom.

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2 as PTF09axc’s host, measures its nuclear spectrum, and finds star-forming/H II diagnostics with no WISE AGN indication.

quotes.quote: PTF09axc was first reported by Arcavi2014 as part of their archival search of the Palomar Transient Factory (PTF) data for blue transients with 21 M_ R (peak) 19 . The discovery date is 2009 June 20 and the source is associated with the galaxy SDSS J145313.07 + 221432.2 -- hereafter SDSSJ1453 -- at redshift z=0.115 Arcavi2010 . We have taken two 1800 s low resolution optical spectra of the nucleus of the host galaxy after the transient had faded Arcavi2014 on 2019 July 14 and 15 using the Auxiliary-port CAMera (ACAM) mounted at the Cassegrain focus of the William Herschel Telescope (WHT) located at the Roque de los Muchachos Observatory on La Palma, Spain under program W19AN009. Using the V400 grating, GG395A order blocking filter, and the AUXCAM CCD results in a wavelength coverage of 3950 - 9400 and resolution R 430 for a 1 arcsec slit.

The ratio of the flux of the emission lines in the optical spectrum of the nuclear region of SDSSJ1453 falls in the H II/starforming region, with the 1 error bar extending into the composite galaxy region (see Fig. BPT-PTF09axc ). The WISE colour difference for SDSSJ1453 is W1-W2 = 0.15 0.08 < 0.8 , which does not indicate the presence of an AGN in this galaxy.

gmw_00009057.name: SDSS J145703.17 + 493640.9
gmw_00009057.overview: SDSS J145703.17 + 493640.9 is a barred lenticular galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.064 [arXiv:1906.12236, arXiv:2002.02498]. The galaxy features a blue outer ring with star formation, a blue disk component, and extended arms [arXiv:2002.02498].

sources.summary: The paper associates the source with SDSS J145703.17 + 493640.9 at z=0.064, finds no light-curve flares, and fits a very soft blackbody-like X-ray spectrum.

quotes.quote: Given the high number of counts detected, we produced a light curve of the observation with ID 19532 with 1 ksec--long bins to investigate if flares are present: none were found. has a redshift of z=0.064 ( d_L=287.4 Mpc) and is associated with the galaxy SDSS J145703.17 + 493640.9 ( 2014ApJ...793...38A ). The spectrum of is relatively soft compared to the spectrum of : no photons with energies above 2 keV are detected. We fitted the source spectrum with a redshifted black body including a power law model for the background using Cash statistics.

We find a best-fit value for the black body temperature of 0.18 0.02 keV. The unabsorbed source flux, subtracting the flux due to the background power law in the 0.3--7 keV range is 1.9 10^ 14 giving a 0.3--7 keV luminosity of L _X=2 10^ 41 . The absorbed 0.3--7 keV flux is (1.7^ +0.3 _ 0.5 ) 10^ 14 .

sources.summary: The PTF09ge host is an early-type/barred lenticular galaxy with star formation in a blue outer ring, blue disk components, and extended arms from blue knots.

quotes.quote: PTF09ge however has star formation missed by the 0.9 kpc aperture spectrum in its outskirts, revealed by the blue ring in our imaging (Figure fig:color ).
The host galaxies of iPTF15af and PTF09ge are barred lenticular galaxies; both have blue disk components in their outskirts in addition to bright central bulges.
The host of PTF09ge has extended arms reaching from the two blue knots in the disk.

gmw_00009058.name: SDSS J110840.11 + 340552.2
gmw_00009058.overview: SDSS J110840.11 + 340552.2 is a quiescent Balmer-strong galaxy [arXiv:2002.02498] located at a redshift of z=0.043671 [arXiv:1906.12236]. The galaxy features an edge-on disk with a bright central bulge [arXiv:2002.02498] and has been detected as an X-ray source [arXiv:1906.12236].

sources.summary: The paper associates the source with SDSS J110840.11 + 340552.2 at z=0.043671, detecting eight significant photons and estimating a power-law X-ray luminosity.

quotes.quote: Using the goodness command in XSPEC we obtained that 58 of the realizations yield a lower fit statistic (again when all simulations are drawn from the best-fit model). ht has a redshift of z=0.043671 ( d_L=193.3 Mpc) and is associated with the galaxy SDSS J110840.11 + 340552.2 ( 2014MNRAS.445.3263H ). For foreground extinction, N_H , we use a rounded--off value of 1 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 given the A_V=0.048 from NED ( 2011ApJ...737..103S ). The value of N _H is kept fixed during the fit. Eight photons are detected at a position consistent with that of the optical source in outburst.

Owing to the relative short exposure compared to the other observations we report on in this manuscript, on average only 0.3 background counts would fall in the source extraction region. Given this very low back ground event rate the eight--count detection is highly significant: i.e. it occurs due to chance in approximately one out of 8 10^8 cases.
The best--fit power law index is = 3.2 1.0. The unabsorbed 0.3-7 keV flux is (2^ +2 _ 1 ) 10^ 14 , giving a 0.3--7 keV luminosity of (9^ +9 _ 5 ) 10^ 40 .

sources.summary: The ASASSN-14ae host is a quiescent Balmer-strong galaxy with an edge-on disk, bright central bulge, disk-favored fits, and early-type-like asymmetry.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of ASASSN-14ae is an edge-on disk that also has a bright central bulge.
Both ASASSN-14ae and iPTF15af are best fit by models with disks added for 2--3 of the bands considered here.
The host galaxies of ASASSN-14ae and iPTF15af are quiescent Balmer-strong, and have asymmetries consistent with early type (quiescent) galaxies.

gmw_00009059.name: SDSS J163355.96 + 301416.6
gmw_00009059.overview: SDSS J163355.96 + 301416.6 is a galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.184 [arXiv:1906.12236]. The galaxy has a bulge half-light radius of 0.3 arcseconds [arXiv:1706.08965] and an estimated central velocity dispersion of 64 km/s [arXiv:1706.08965].

sources.summary: For PTF--09djl, Keck and WHT velocity dispersions disagree; the WHT fit and aperture are inadequate, so the paper adopts the Keck spectrum as the most reliable central-dispersion measurement.

quotes.quote: Regarding PTF--09djl, there appears to be an inconsistency of 40 km s ^ 1 between the Keck (64 7 km s ^ 1 ) and WHT (104 13 km s ^ 1 ) values. We note that the overlapping wavelength coverage of the WHT spectrum with the templates is small ( 500 ), and a visual inspection of the best-fitting template with the galaxy spectrum reveals that the fit is poor. Moreover, our WHT spectra use a 1 1 arcsec slit width, while the bulge half-light radius of this galaxy is 0 3 and hence does not satisfy the criterion of Gebhardt2000 (see discussion below). On the other hand, the best-fitting solution to the Keck spectrum is satisfactory. We therefore adopt the value as measured from the Keck spectrum as the best representation of the central velocity dispersion of this source.

For the WHT spectra of PTF--09djl we are not in this regime (as discussed in Section sec:independent ). We therefore adopt the value obtained from the Keck spectrum, obtained with a slit width of 0 5, as the most reliable measurement.

sources.summary: The paper associates the undetected source with SDSS J163355.96 + 301416.6 at z=0.184 and derives a Chandra flux and luminosity upper limit.

quotes.quote: Using the goodness command we obtained that 96 of the realizations yield a lower fit statistic (again when all simulations are drawn from the best-fit model). has a redshift of z=0.184 ( d_L=893.2 Mpc) and is associated with the galaxy SDSS J163355.96 + 301416.6 ( 2014ApJ...793...38A ). For foreground extinction, N_H , we use a rounded--off value of 1 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 given the A_V=0.049 from NED ( 2011ApJ...737..103S ). The value of N_H is kept fixed during the fit. No X--ray photons with energies between 0.3--7 keV have been detected in a circle with a radius of 1 centered on the optical outburst position of .

W3PIMMS https://heasarc.gsfc.nasa.gov/cgi-bin/Tools/w3pimms/w3pimms.pl provides a 95 upper limit to the (absorbed) 0.3--7 keV X--ray flux of F _X 3 10^ 15 both for the power law model and for the black body model. This yields an upper limit to the source 0.3--7 keV luminosity of L _X 3 10^ 41 for both models. ^* Parameter fixed to this value.

gmw_00009060.name: CBS 126
gmw_00009060.overview: CBS 126 is a broad-line Seyfert 1 galaxy and a bright X-ray active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1110.6047]. It exhibits a high degree of optical continuum polarization, strong spectral variability, and a large soft X-ray excess [arXiv:1110.6047].

sources.summary: The paper treats CBS 126/Ton 1187 as a broad-line Seyfert 1 and bright X-ray AGN, noting optical polarisation, strong soft excess and spectral variability, and a massive black hole.

quotes.quote: CBS 126 (= Ton 1187, _ 2000 = 10h 13m 03.2s, _ 2000 = +35^ 51^ ' 24^ '' , z = 0.079) is a broad line Seyfert 1 galaxy with H = 2980 200 km s ^ 1 Grupe04 . It was detected as a bright X-ray AGN during the ROSAT All-Sky Survey (RASS, Voges99 ) in 1990 November Grupe98 and was observed again by ROSAT with the High Resolution Imager in 1995 October Grupe01 . Our attention was drawn to CBS 126 because it shows a relatively high degree of optical continuum polarisation.

CBS 126 is therefore a good candidate for finding ionised X-ray absorber features. It was again observed in X-rays as part of a Swift fill-in project to study the spectral energy distributions of AGN Grupe10 in 2006 June. The source shows similar properties to NLS1s such as strong spectral variability (see Fig. lc_swift ) and a large soft excess below 1 keV, which are typical for NLS1s but unusual for BLS1s. CBS 126 harbours a black hole with an estimated mass in excess of 7.6 10^ 7 M _ SDSS DR7 catalogue Shen11 . This, together with the high-velocity BLR clouds and the enormous soft excess make CBS 126 an interesting source.

gmw_00009061.name: SDSS J211646
gmw_00009061.overview: SDSS J211646 is an active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:2208.12286]. Over a ten-year period, the source exhibited a continuous decline in its W1 band flux, which is interpreted as fading dust emission accompanied by a decreasing Eddington ratio [arXiv:2208.12286].

sources.summary: The paper highlights SDSS J211646 as one of two AGN candidates with a clear continuous W1 flux decline over 10 years, interpreted as fading dust emission and a lower Eddington ratio.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, two sources show a clear continuous flux decline over 10 yr. These two lightcurves are shown in Figure fig:NeoFading (left, SDSS J211646) with > W1 0.49 mag and (right, SDSS J111800) with > W1 0.34 mag, which suggests a continuous fading of the AGN dust emission over the past 10 yrs. The change of the Eddington ratio for those two sources are from = 0.03 to =0.02 for SDSS J211646 and = 0.16 to =0.12 for SDSS J111800.

gmw_00009062.name: SDSS J111800
gmw_00009062.overview: SDSS J111800 is an active galactic nucleus candidate [arXiv:2208.12286]. Over a ten-year period, the source exhibited a continuous decline in W1 band flux of more than 0.34 magnitudes, which is interpreted as fading dust emission accompanied by a decreasing Eddington ratio [arXiv:2208.12286].

sources.summary: The paper highlights SDSS J111800 as one of two AGN candidates with a clear continuous W1 flux decline over 10 years, interpreted as fading dust emission and a lower Eddington ratio.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, two sources show a clear continuous flux decline over 10 yr. These two lightcurves are shown in Figure fig:NeoFading (left, SDSS J211646) with > W1 0.49 mag and (right, SDSS J111800) with > W1 0.34 mag, which suggests a continuous fading of the AGN dust emission over the past 10 yrs. The change of the Eddington ratio for those two sources are from = 0.03 to =0.02 for SDSS J211646 and = 0.16 to =0.12 for SDSS J111800.

gmw_00009063.name: SDSS J1308
gmw_00009063.overview: SDSS J1308 is classified as a changing-look behavior active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2208.12286]. It exhibits an appearance of continuum flux without a corresponding change in its broad emission components, suggesting its bright phase may persist to the current epoch [arXiv:2208.12286].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1308 as a changing-look-behavior AGN with significant continuum-flux appearance but no broad-emission change, and suggests its burst or bright phase may last to the current epoch.

quotes.quote: The bottom panels of Figure fig:Obj_Spec shows two AGN with a significant continuum flux change (appearance for SDSS J1308 and disappearance for SDSS J1413) stronger than 3 error of the associating continuum, but do not show a difference in broad emission components. We hereafter call those two sources as changing-look behavior (CLB) AGN to distinguish from the CL AGN with a clear sign of the flux changes of the broad emission.
For SDSS J1308 which shows an appearing feature, even though the two spectra are taken well before the ALLWISE light-curve range, the burst or bright-end phase might last until the current epoch.

gmw_00009064.name: SDSS J1413
gmw_00009064.overview: SDSS J1413 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits changing-look behavior [arXiv:2208.12286]. This behavior is characterized by a disappearance of its continuum flux stronger than three times the error of the associating continuum, without a corresponding change in its broad emission components [arXiv:2208.12286].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1413 as a changing-look-behavior AGN with continuum-flux disappearance, no broad-emission change, and an SDSS epoch coinciding with the faintest NEOWISE light-curve phase.

quotes.quote: The bottom panels of Figure fig:Obj_Spec shows two AGN with a significant continuum flux change (appearance for SDSS J1308 and disappearance for SDSS J1413) stronger than 3 error of the associating continuum, but do not show a difference in broad emission components. We hereafter call those two sources as changing-look behavior (CLB) AGN to distinguish from the CL AGN with a clear sign of the flux changes of the broad emission.

Those observing epochs of SDSS spectra as shown in Figure fig:NeoCurve give a sign that, for SDSS J1413, the second epoch of the SDSS spectra was taken at the faintest phase of the light-curve in the NEOWISE , which is consistent with the ``disappearing'' sign in the SDSS spectra. In addition, the current NEOWISE light-curve already reaches the same flux level as the ALLWISE fluxes which are close to the first epoch of the SDSS spectra, suggesting that the current optical spectra might show appearing feature again.

gmw_00009065.name: IC 2479
gmw_00009065.overview: IC 2479 is an active galactic nucleus that is currently in a fading state [arXiv:1706.03071]. Its central engine has experienced a luminosity decline of over two orders of magnitude within the last 10,000 years [arXiv:1706.03071].

sources.summary: The paper presents IC 2479 as an early fading AGN, citing bright extended [O III], weak core emission, X-ray confirmation, and a two-order luminosity decline within 10^4 years.

quotes.quote: IC 2479 is one of the first fading AGN discovered through the galaxy zoo project lin08 . first mentioned that high ionization lines including [ OIII ] 5007 are bright in the [ OIII ] blob, while the [ OIII ] emission power around the AGN core is orders of magnitude weaker, suggesting that AGN is fading. confirmed this hypothesis through the X-ray observations with XMM-Newton and Suzaku .
Considering the distance from the central engine to the [ OIII ] blobs and input power, they estimated that the central engine of IC 2479 faded over two orders of magnitude within 10^4 yrs.

gmw_00009066.name: CQ0105+0000
gmw_00009066.overview: CQ0105+0000 is a compact galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.279 [arXiv:1207.1193]. It hosts an old stellar population with a stellar mass of approximately 10^10 solar masses [arXiv:1207.1193].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CQ0105+0000 as an old, compact galaxy at z=0.279, part of unresolved compact systems with stellar masses around 10^10 solar masses and high densities.

quotes.quote: Galaxies Three of the four galaxies we have identified are old and very compact stellar systems (CQ0105+0000, CQ0822+0004, and CQ0832+0121).

Based thereon we infer stellar masses and stellar ages of several Gyr (the exact value depending on the assumed metallicity) and masses of the order 10 ^ 10 M _ . Interestingly, they are all point sources - even in the K band, where the seeing is about 0.6 arcsec in the UKIDSS images. This implies that the galaxies must have stellar densities in excess of 10 ^ 10 M _ kpc ^ 2 , which is similar to the compact Distant Red Galaxies found at redshifts around 2 e.g., VanDokkum98 and among the highest measured at any redshift Franx08 .
CQ0105+0000 ( z=0.279 ) This object is a compact galaxy with an old stellar population at z=0.279 . Both in the SDSS and UKIDSS imaging the object is consistent with being a point source.

gmw_00009067.name: CQ0822+0004
gmw_00009067.overview: CQ0822+0004 is an old, very compact stellar system located at a redshift of z=0.378 [arXiv:1207.1193]. Its spectrum displays strong Balmer absorption lines without emission lines, indicating a post-starburst galaxy with an estimated age of about one gigayear [arXiv:1207.1193].

sources.summary: The paper classifies CQ0822+0004 as an old compact stellar system whose spectrum shows Balmer absorption, giving an age near a gigayear and a likely post-starburst nature.

quotes.quote: Galaxies Three of the four galaxies we have identified are old and very compact stellar systems (CQ0105+0000, CQ0822+0004, and CQ0832+0121).

Implications for the study of compact Galaxies Three of the galaxies we have detected are old stellar systems (CQ0105+0000, CQ0822+0004, and CQ0832+0121). The spectrum of CQ0822+0004 has Balmer absorption lines and hence most likely has an age of about a Gyr, whereas the other two have older stellar populations.
CQ0822+0004 ( z=0.378 ) This spectrum shows strong Balmer absorption at z=0.378 , but no emission lines. This is most likely a compact post-starburst galaxy (so-called E+A galaxy).

gmw_00009068.name: CQ0832+0121
gmw_00009068.overview: CQ0832+0121 is a compact galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.166 [arXiv:1207.1193]. It is characterized as an old stellar system similar to CQ0105+0000 [arXiv:1207.1193].

sources.summary: The paper identifies CQ0832+0121 as an old compact galaxy at z=0.166, grouped with similarly unresolved compact systems and described as similar to CQ0105+0000.

quotes.quote: Galaxies Three of the four galaxies we have identified are old and very compact stellar systems (CQ0105+0000, CQ0822+0004, and CQ0832+0121).

Implications for the study of compact Galaxies Three of the galaxies we have detected are old stellar systems (CQ0105+0000, CQ0822+0004, and CQ0832+0121). The spectrum of CQ0822+0004 has Balmer absorption lines and hence most likely has an age of about a Gyr, whereas the other two have older stellar populations.
CQ0832+0121 ( z=0.166 ) This object is a compact, old galaxy (similar to CQ0105+0000). The object is not flagged as a QSO based on the SDSS photometry.

gmw_00009069.name: IGR J06073--0024
gmw_00009069.overview: IGR J06073--0024 is a high-redshift, hard-X-ray-selected blazar and flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1210.6221]. The source exhibits a compact radio morphology and is optically classified as a broad-line active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1210.6221].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J06073--0024 with PMN J0606-0025, a compact flat-spectrum radio source optically classified as a high-redshift broad-line AGN and concluded to be a hard-X-ray blazar.

quotes.quote: IGR J06073--0024 Just outside the INTEGRAL 90 error circle (see Figure 06073 and Table xrt_log ), we find an X-ray source that is consistent with the counterpart found in the CRATES catalogue and reported in NED as PMN J0606-0025. It is detected at several radio frequencies from 1.4 to 8.4 GHz and shows a compact radio morphology (see Figure 06073 ). It has a NVSS 1.4 GHz flux of 133.9 4 mJy, it is detected at 5 GHz in the Parkes-MIT-NRAO (PMN; griffith95 ) survey with a flux of 116 12 mJy and in the MIT-Green Bank survey bennett86 with a flux of 96 mJy, therefore some degree of flux variability at this frequency cannot be ruled out. In the CRATES catalogue, the source is reported to have an 8.4 GHz flux of 98 mJy, which combined with the 1.4 GHz flux, provides the flat spectral index reported in Table ibis_log .

The CRATES source has recently been optically classified and found to be a broad emission line AGN (FWHM = 5600 km s ^ 1 ) at z=1.08 masetti12 ). This initially tentative identification is now supported by the X-ray detection.
We therefore conclude that IGR J06073--0024 is a newly discovered flat spectrum radio quasar at high z, hence a new hard X-ray selected blazar.

gmw_00009070.name: PMN J0722-3814
gmw_00009070.overview: PMN J0722-3814 is an active galactic nucleus that is possibly a blazar-type source [arXiv:1210.6221]. Its WISE colors locate the object on the WISE blazar strip, in a region compatible with flat-spectrum radio quasars [arXiv:1210.6221].

sources.summary: The paper links PMN J0722-3814 to the brighter X-ray counterpart of IGR J07225--3810; its radio, X-ray, and WISE properties support a blazar-like AGN classification.

quotes.quote: Source N1 coincides instead with the CRATES association proposed in Table ibis_log and reported in NED as PMN J0722-3814. As is evident in Figure 07225 , this CRATES source is slightly offset from the bright radio source (NVSS J072227-381457) falling inside the INTEGRAL error circle. This offset may be due to the inability of the PMN survey (used as a reference for the CRATES position) to resolve the emission in two nearby objects as done by the NVSS (see Figure 07225 ).

Consistent with the NVSS/XRT positions, we find an optical USNO B-1 source with optical magnitude B=19.1 (0.1 mJy in flux), which provides Log(R _ L ) 3. The XRT spectrum for source N1 has enough statistics to allow determination of the photon index, which, despite the large errors, is rather flat, at 1.52 ^ +0.45 _ 0.46 ; the X-ray 2--10 keV flux is 5.76 10 ^ 13 erg cm ^2 s ^ 1 . The source is also detected by WISE with the following magnitudes: W _1 =14.67, W _2 =13.61, W _3 =10.22 and W _4 =8.33.

Its WISE colours, W _2 W _3 =3.39, and W _1 W _2 =1.06, locate this source on the WISE blazar strip in a region compatible with the colours of flat spectrum radio quasars. We therefore conclude that source N1 is an AGN possibly of the blazar type given its overall properties (flat X-ray spectrum, WISE colours, and radio characteristics); as such, it is a convincing counterpart of the variable INTEGRAL source.

gmw_00009071.name: IGR J14488--4008
gmw_00009071.overview: IGR J14488--4008 is a newly discovered radio galaxy with a complex radio morphology that features two bright lobes [arXiv:1210.6221]. The galaxy likely hosts a compact core and is classified as a type 2 active galactic nucleus due to its X-ray absorption [arXiv:1210.6221].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J14488--4008 as a new radio galaxy with complex two-lobed morphology, a likely compact core, and X-ray absorption suggesting a type 2 AGN.

quotes.quote: IGR J14488--4008 As is evident from Figure 14488 , the CRATES source listed in Table ibis_log has an extremely complex radio morphology, with multiple structures that resemble a radio galaxy with two bright lobes fully contained within the INTEGRAL error circle. Also in this case, there are two X-ray sources (N1 and N2 in Table xrt_log and in Figure 14488 ) falling within IBIS positional uncertainty; one of the two (N1) is brighter and harder since it is also detected above 3 keV. This source is associated to one of the CRATES excesses (CRATES J1448-4008), as well as to one of the NVSS (NVSS J144851-400846) peaks reported in this region. This excess is located at the centre of the radio structure and is also coincident with the optical position of the main galaxy, PGC589690 paturel03 . N1 could therefore be the compact core of a newly discovered radio galaxy.
In addition to the Galactic absorption, the data require an intrinsic column density N _ H = (6.2 ^ +2.1 _ 1.9 ) 10 ^ 22 cm ^ 2 , suggesting that it might be a type 2 AGN.

It is difficult to assess its nature at this stage, but its X-ray characteristics (flux variability, X-ray weakness, as well as X-ray spectral softness) suggest that this source is unlikely to be the counterpart of the persistent INTEGRAL object, thus making N1 the most likely association to IGR J14488--4008.

gmw_00009072.name: IGR J18129--0649
gmw_00009072.overview: IGR J18129--0649 is an active galactic nucleus that is bright at radio frequencies [arXiv:1210.6221]. The source is classified as radio-loud, with WISE colors that place it in a region compatible with quasars [arXiv:1210.6221].

sources.summary: The paper treats IGR J18129--0649 as a valid INTEGRAL counterpart bright at radio frequencies; WISE colours and radio loudness validate an AGN nature despite uncertain optical class.

quotes.quote: IGR J18129--0649 The radio source listed in Table ibis_log lies well within the INTEGRAL error box. It coincides with the only detection made by XRT in this region (see Table xrt_log for details), which suggests that it is a valid counterpart of the INTEGRAL source. It is quite bright at radio frequencies, with a flux ranging from 2720 mJy at 0.074 GHz cohen07 to 339 mJy at 20 GHz murphy10 .

Using the edelson12 formalism, we estimate S _ I =0.9, i.e. a value below the cut of 1.73 used to reliably identified type 1 AGN. Furthermore, we note that the WISE colours are W _2 W _3 =2.6 and W _1 W _2 =1.34, placing the source outside the WiSE blazar strip in a region compatible with the location of quasars. The source has no counterpart in the USNO-B1 catalogue, with only an upper limit to the B magnitude of 20.7 (or 0.02 mJy in flux). Combining the 4.85 GHz flux of 766 mJy measured by the PMN survey with that in the B band gives a radio loudness parameter Log(R _ L ) 4.5; i.e., the source is certainly radio-loud. While the association of PMN J1812-0648 with the INTEGRAL source is secure and its AGN nature validated by observational evidence, its optical class is unclear, and only optical follow-up observations can assess its true nature.

gmw_00009073.name: PKS 1934--470
gmw_00009073.overview: PKS 1934--470 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=0.8 [arXiv:1210.6221]. The source exhibits a compact radio morphology and is classified as a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1210.6221].

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 1934--470 as the radio-associated N2 counterpart to IGR J19386--4653; literature calls it a flat-spectrum quasar and compact radio data imply a radio-loud AGN.

quotes.quote: The second one, N2, is instead associated with the CRATES object (also PKS 1934--470 in NED) listed in Table ibis_log , thus suggesting that this is a viable counterpart to the INTEGRAL detection. Source N2 has been previously seen in X-rays since it is listed in the WGACAT catalogue white00 , with a large positional uncertainty (50 ) that is, however, compatible with the XRT location. The WGACAT source is also reported in the Roma-Blazar catalogue massaro09 and is classified by landt04 as a flat spectrum radio quasar at z=0.8; unfortunately, this source is located 35 from the XRT detection casting some doubts on their association. On the other hand, we could not find any optical counterpart within the XRT positional uncertainty, thus making the case even more puzzling. At radio frequencies, source N2/PKS 1934--470 is well studied, with detections reported at various frequencies going from 138 mJy at 0.843 GHz bock99 to 90 mJy at 8.6 GHz mcconnell12 ; this provides a radio spectral index of -0.2, still flat but substantially different from what is reported in Table ibis_log .

The radio morphology is that of a compact source. At 5 GHz the reported fluxes range from 104 mJy mcconnell12 to 174 mJy . Combining these values with the sensitivity limit of the USNO-B1 survey in this region (B 20.8 or 0.02 mJy in flux), we estimate a radio loudness Log(R _ L ) 3.7, which suggests that N2/PKS 1934--470 is a radio loud AGN.

gmw_00009074.name: HATLASJ120127.5 014043
gmw_00009074.overview: HATLASJ120127.5 014043 is a high-redshift galaxy confirmed to be strongly gravitationally lensed [arXiv:1801.01831]. Although it lacks a spectroscopic redshift, its sub-millimeter redshift is estimated to be approximately 3.80, and the reconstructed source is resolved into two emission knots separated by 3.5 kpc [arXiv:1801.01831].

sources.summary: The paper confirms HATLASJ120127.5 014043 as lensed using new SMA data, notes its missing spectroscopic redshift, estimates z_sub-mm near 3.8, and resolves two emission knots.

quotes.quote: HATLASJ120127.5 014043 : This is the H ATLAS source that we have confirmed to be lensed with the new SMA data. It is the only object in our sample for which we still lack a spectroscopic measure of the redshift of the background galaxy. The redshift estimated from the Herschel /SPIRE photometry is z_ sub-mm =3.80 0.58 . The reconstructed source is resolved into two knots of emission, separated by 3.5 kpc.

gmw_00009075.name: HATLASJ133649.9+291801
gmw_00009075.overview: HATLASJ133649.9+291801 is a high-redshift galaxy that acts as a strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:1801.01831]. It represents the single-lens system with the smallest Einstein radius in its sample, measuring 0.4 arcseconds [arXiv:1801.01831].

sources.summary: The paper reports HATLASJ133649.9+291801 as the smallest-Einstein-radius single-lens system in the sample, with lens-modelling, source-size, and magnification results consistent with B13.

quotes.quote: HATLASJ133649.9+291801 : This is the single-lens system in our sample with the smallest Einstein radius, _ E =0.4^ . Our lens modelling gives results consistent with those of B13.

HATLASJ090740.0 004200, HATLASJ132630.1+334410 and HATLASJ133649.9+291800 are the only systems where our findings are quite consistent with those of B13 for both the source size and the magnification.

gmw_00009076.name: HATLASJ134429.4+303036
gmw_00009076.overview: HATLASJ134429.4+303036 is a strongly lensed background galaxy that is the brightest source at 500 microns in its sample [arXiv:1801.01831]. Its lensed images exhibit a typical cusp configuration, which is produced by a foreground lens galaxy that is significantly elongated in the north-south direction [arXiv:1801.01831].

sources.summary: The paper describes HATLASJ134429.4+303036 as the brightest 500-micron lensed galaxy in the sample, with cusp-like lensed images, an elongated north-south lens, and a higher Einstein radius.

quotes.quote: HATLASJ134429.4+303036 : This is the 500 m brightest lensed galaxy in the entire N17 sample. The observed lensed images indicate a typical cusp configuration, similar to what was observed in the well studied lensed galaxy SDP.81 e.g. 2015MNRAS.452.2258D , where the background galaxy lies on the fold of the tangential caustic. According to our modelling the lens is significantly elongated ( q_ L =0.53 ) in the North-South direction, consistent with what is indicated by available HST /WFC3 imaging data for the light distribution of the foreground galaxy (see Fig. 3 of N17). We estimate a higher Einstein radius than the one reported by B13, although the two results are still consistent within 2 .

gmw_00009077.name: LEDA 792493
gmw_00009077.overview: LEDA 792493 is a low-mass dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2112.06564]. It is a gas-rich and relatively unperturbed system with a low tidal strength and a high size ratio [arXiv:2112.06564].

sources.summary: The paper describes LEDA 792493 as a low-mass outlier with the lowest tidal strength and highest size ratio, interpreting it as a relatively unperturbed, gas-rich dwarf galaxy.

quotes.quote: Most of the low-mass galaxies reside close to the best-fit linear relation except for LEDA 792493 (ID 63, in the upper-left corner) which has the lowest S_ sum and highest size ratio of the low-mass subsample. LEDA 792493 is a relatively unperturbed, rich dwarf galaxy. If we exclude LEDA 792493 from this analysis, we obtain an R value of 0.47 and a p value of 0.06.

gmw_00009078.name: S17
gmw_00009078.overview: S17 is a disk galaxy that is viewed from an edge-on perspective [arXiv:1709.08642]. Because of this orientation, the galaxy requires a thick-disk model to fully fit its structure [arXiv:1709.08642].

sources.summary: The paper flags S17 as the only galaxy not fully fit by the standard decomposition because its edge-on disk requires a thick-disk model, excluding it from FP analysis.

quotes.quote: We were able to fit all the galaxies with the exception of S17. In this case, inspection reveals the presence of an edge-on disk, for which a thick-disk model is required. GASP2D is not yet able to fit a thick-disk model, so only \ and \ fits of S17 were performed.

Only galaxy S17 is excluded from this analysis on account of its edge-on disk component (Sect. sec:fitproc ), so the total sample used in the FP analysis consists of the 40 galaxies listed in Table tab:spec_sample .

gmw_00009079.name: J0457-7820
gmw_00009079.overview: J0457-7820 is a gravitationally lensed quasar system at a redshift of z = 3.145 [arXiv:2206.07714]. It features three blue point sources imaged around a central galaxy, with a second galaxy located to the northwest [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0457-7820 as a Gaia/WISE-selected triple-image quasar candidate with three blue point sources, spectra showing a z=3.145 quasar, and two fitted lensing galaxies.

quotes.quote: J0457-7820 This system was selected through the Gaia multiplet WISE search, with three Gaia detections ( G =18.81, 19.07, and 19.60) and W1--W2=0.95. Archival NOAO DeCAM imaging shows three bright blue point sources around a galaxy. The follow-up NTT-EFOSC2 spectra, positioned to capture light from all three images simultaneously, are low signal-to-noise but clearly show three traces of a z = 3.145 quasar. Three g band and three i band exposures, each of 120s were taken with the Magellan Instant Camera, MagIC. A second galaxy is apparent to the North-West of the system.

gmw_00009080.name: J0818-2613
gmw_00009080.overview: J0818-2613 is a quadruply imaged quasar system located in a high-stellar-density environment [arXiv:2206.07714]. The background source is a broad-absorption line quasar at a redshift of approximately 2.16 [arXiv:2209.14320, arXiv:2206.07714], which exhibits a red continuum and multiple velocity outflows [arXiv:2012.10051, arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0818-2613 as a quadruply imaged quasar lens and reports previously unknown deflector redshift plus a first velocity-dispersion measurement.

quotes.quote: J0818-2613 Stern_2021 first discovered this quadruply imaged quasar system using Gaia data release 2 and measured the quasar redshift, \ = 2.164 , using Keck-LRIS spectroscopy. This lens system was also independently reported by Lemon_2022 with a measured quasar redshift, \ = 2.155 . Schmidt_2022 performed lens modeling using HST images of the system. In this paper, we report the redshift and velocity dispersion of the deflector.

In this paper we have : Developed a forward modeling technique and used it to extract 1D spectra of eight lensing galaxies, even in the presence of much brighter lensed quasar images. Used the extracted spectra to measure the deflector redshifts in all of our targets, including two that have never been measured before, in the J0818-2613 and J1817+2729 systems. For the remainder of the targets, our measured redshifts agree with those in the literature to within 1 . Made the first measurements of the stellar velocity dispersions for six of the eight lensing galaxies in our sample.

sources.summary: The paper describes GraL J081828.3 261325 as a quadruply imaged quasar with close and western components, possible lens-galaxy flux, red continuum, and absorption.

quotes.quote: GraL J081828.3 261325 --- This is a quadruply-imaged quasar at z = 2.164 . Two components are closely spaced, with the other two a few arcseconds to the west. Faint red flux potentially associated with the lensing galaxy is evident in Figure 1.

This source was identified from a revised ERT search for lensed quasars performed in 2019 combined with the wavelet method discussed in  2. The Keck observation, presented in Figure 2, targeted the compact pair and shows a spatially extended system in the two-dimensional spectrum, indicative of a lensed quasar, though the seeing was insufficient to resolve the individual components. Instead, a single wide extraction was used. The quasar itself has a red continuum and weak Ly emission, indicative of moderate absorption either at the quasar itself or along the line of sight.

sources.summary: The paper confirms J0818-2613 as a four-Gaia-detection BAL quasar lens in a high-stellar-density field, with spectra at two position angles showing multiple velocity outflows.

quotes.quote: J0818-2613 This system was confirmed during the 2019 WHT observations. It was discovered as four Gaia detections associated with a red ALLWISE detection. Spectra were taken at two position angles, each through the bright pair, and clearly show that the source is a z = 2.155 BAL quasar with multiple velocity outflows e.g., korista1993 . The system is in a high stellar density environment, since it is at a galactic latitude of +5.4 ^ , an area which has been purposefully omitted from previous searches due to high contamination rates. The strong constraints from requiring four Gaia detections around photometric quasars allows us to now probe such high-density regions.

gmw_00009081.name: J1326+3020
gmw_00009081.overview: J1326+3020 is a gravitationally lensed system containing a background quasar at a redshift of z = 1.852 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The foreground lensing galaxy, which is a member of a cluster, is located at a redshift of z = 0.339 [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J1326+3020 as a lens after WHT spectra show a quasar and foreground galaxy, while imaging and raw-pixel hints indicate a faint counterimage.

quotes.quote: J1326+3020 The WHT spectrum reveals two traces, one of a quasar at z = 1.852, and another of a galaxy at z = 0.339. The Legacy Survey imaging shows a blue point source in the model-subtracted residual image, suggesting this as a faint counterimage. Indeed there are hints of the Ly line in the blue arm raw pixels of the WHT-ISIS data, thus we classify this as a lens. The lens is a member of a cluster, whose members were targeted for spectroscopy by holden1999 . They obtained a spectrum for the lensing galaxy of this system, reporting a broad emission line around 5437 , which they attributed to M g II , giving a tentative redshift of z = 0.94, however our spectra confirm this is in fact the Ciii ] line in agreement with an archival eBOSS spectrum centred on the galaxy.

gmw_00009082.name: J0608+4229
gmw_00009082.overview: J0608+4229 is a strongly lensed quasar [arXiv:2012.10051, arXiv:2206.07714] with a compact image configuration [arXiv:2012.10051]. Modeling of the system reveals four distinct quasar images [arXiv:2206.07714], and spectroscopy shows an absorption system at a redshift of z = 2.112 [arXiv:2012.10051].

sources.summary: The paper confirms GraL J060841.4+422937 as a compact lensed quasar with two spatially distinct spectra, a nearby absorption system, and a model similar to GraL J060710.8 215217.

quotes.quote: GraL J060841.4+422937 --- This candidate was also selected from the more recent ERT run using \ DR2, and has a compact, 1 3 separated configuration. The source had a high (86 ) likelihood of being a quasar from the Million Quasars Catalog ver. 6.4c; Flesch:15 . Keck spectroscopy was obtained in December 2019 at two PAs, both of which clearly show two spatially distinct spectra of the same lensed quasar. Figure  fig:spectra1 presents the spectra obtained at PA=275 . An absorption system with multiple features from \ to \ is detected at z = 2.112 , which is presumed too close to the quasar redshift to be associated with the lensing galaxy.

GraL J060841.4+422937 --- The image configuration and model obtained for this system are similar to GraL J060710.8 215217. The fourth image is predicted to be 0 2 from the brightest component detected in \ data.

sources.summary: The paper confirms J0608+4229 through three Gaia detections, WHT spectra with similar extracted components, and Pan-STARRS modelling consistent with four quasar images.

quotes.quote: J0608+4229 This system has three Gaia detections with G =18.22, 19.86, 20.12. It was confirmed during the Feb 2019 WHT observing run, in which the system was targeted at two position angles: both through the brightest Gaia detection and each of the other two detections. The forced fitting extracts very similar spectra for each component. The multiple extractions of the brightest image are in good agreement, with only a slight mismatch in the relative amplitudes in the red arm, likely due to the flux-matching procedure of the blue and red arm spectra. Modelling the system as three PSFs reveals residual flux just south of the brightest image, and modelling the systems as four PSFs fit the Pan-STARRS data to the noise, as shown in Figure fig:J0608 .

gmw_00009083.name: J0416+7428
gmw_00009083.overview: J0416+7428 is a double lensed quasar system featuring a rare, low-redshift main lensing galaxy at z = 0.098 [arXiv:2206.07714]. This lensing galaxy belongs to a group with two nearby galaxies and exhibits star-forming emission in its outskirts [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0416+7428 as a double with a very low-redshift bright lens, nearby group galaxies, star-forming emission at the lens redshift, and unusual lens rarity.

quotes.quote: J0416+7428 This double has a very low redshift lens ( z = 0.098), which appears to be part of a group with two nearby galaxies apparent in the Pan-STARRS imaging. In the 2D spectra, narrow emission lines are seen both 4.6 arcseconds from the main lensing galaxy in the direction of the more distant image, and also 13 arcseconds in the other direction coincident with diffuse extended continuum emission for the latter. These lines are consistent with H and Nii emission at the lens galaxy redshift, therefore we attribute the former to a star-forming region in the outskirts of the main lensing galaxy, and the latter to star formation in the outskirts of the galaxy group member to the West. The lensing galaxy has an i band brightness of 16.22. The low redshift of the lensing galaxy is rare amongst lensed quasars, with the only known lensed quasar with a redshift lower than this being the Einstein cross huchra1985 .

gmw_00009084.name: J0921+3020
gmw_00009084.overview: J0921+3020 is a doubly imaged quasar system at a redshift of z = 3.33 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The system is gravitationally lensed by a galaxy at z = 0.428 that belongs to a surrounding galaxy cluster [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper presents J0921+3020 as a 2.93-arcsecond double with z=3.33 source, a blended faint image, lens absorption at z=0.428, and cluster membership.

quotes.quote: J0921+3020 This 2.93 separation double has a source redshift of z = 3.33, with one bright image ( G =18.66 with SDSS and BOSS spectra), and one faint image ( G =20.86) blended with a galaxy. The lens redshift of this double is seen in the difference spectra between the two components, showing clear absorption lines at z = 0.428. There are two galaxy clusters surrounding this lens, with spectroscopic redshifts from SDSS spectra of z = 0.334, z = 0.427. Therefore, the lens belongs to the latter cluster. The system lies in the Smithsonian Hectospec Lensing Survey (SHELS) four degree field, and both objects have SHELS spectra of z = 3.33 quasars (Geller et al., 2014).

gmw_00009085.name: J0325-2232
gmw_00009085.overview: J0325-2232 is a likely gravitationally lensed system containing two images of a quasar at a redshift of z = 1.35 [arXiv:2206.07714]. A lensing galaxy is positioned between the two images, causing contamination, reddening, and microlensing [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0325-2232 as a likely lens whose two quasar images have discrepant continua, galaxy-contaminated imaging, red-filter flux reversal, and image-specific absorption.

quotes.quote: J0325-2232 The spectra show emission lines of a z = 1.35 quasar in both components, however the continua and relative emission line amplitudes are clearly discrepant, with stronger broad emission lines in the redder image. Legacy Survey imaging shows a galaxy between the two components, which is well fit by a Sersic positioned between the PSFs and slightly nearer the South-Western image, B. In the g band of both Pan-STARRS and Legacy Survey imaging, A is brighter than B, however B is brighter in both Gaia (by 0.21 mag in the broad G filter) and in the ground-based red filters, even when including the galaxy in the fit. Absorption is present around 4785 \ and 4890 \ in only the redder image. This system is likely a lens with contamination and reddening by the lensing galaxy coupled with microlensing.

gmw_00009086.name: J0826+7002
gmw_00009086.overview: J0826+7002 is a gravitationally lensed system containing two quasars at a redshift of 1.62 [arXiv:2206.07714]. Pixel modelling of the system reveals three lensing galaxies situated close to the brighter quasar image [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper reports J0826+7002 as a recovered GDR2 lens candidate whose better WHT observations reveal two z=1.62 quasars and three nearby lensing galaxies.

quotes.quote: J0826+7002 The system was originally targeted in a GDR1 search lemon2018 , as it was selected as a Gaia singlet near a morphological Pan-STARRS galaxy, however the WHT spectra were not deep enough to confirm the fainter object as a quasar. It was targeted again after it was recovered by the same search technique in GDR2, and better seeing conditions and lower airmass observations reveal both Gaia detections to be quasars at z = 1.62. Both images are detected in GDR1 and GDR2, but the separation is 5.82 , hence the system was not discovered by the unWISE modelling or WISE- Gaia doublet selection. Pixel modelling shows three lensing galaxies lying closer to the brighter image. There is a third Gaia detection in Gaia EDR3 on the Western galaxy of these three, perhaps hinting at a third image of the quasar blended with the galaxy.

gmw_00009087.name: J0803+3908
gmw_00009087.overview: J0803+3908 is a bright, extremely luminous quasar located at a redshift of z = 2.97 [arXiv:2206.07714]. High-resolution imaging reveals that the system is gravitationally lensed into a compact fold-configuration quad [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0803+3908 as a compact fold-configuration quad selected by unWISE modelling, confirmed with CFHT imaging and spatially resolved spectra at z=2.97.

quotes.quote: J0803+3908 This system was originally selected with the unWISE modelling technique, given it has two Gaia detections separated by 0.91 . Good-seeing CFHT band data reveal a fold configuration quad. Figure fig:J0803 shows these data, with a 4 PSF fit and subtraction, showing faint extended residuals possibly due to a lensing galaxy. Very good seeing conditions allowed spatially resolved spectra between the bright pair and the Northern image, confirming the source to be at z = 2.97. schindler2018 confirmed this source as a bright quasar at z = 2.975 in their North Galactic Cap sample of the Extremely Luminous Quasar Survey (ELQS-N), however they did not comment on any possibility of it being gravitationally lensed. Such catalogues present a good opportunity for finding compact lensed quasars thanks to the magnification bias of lensing.

gmw_00009088.name: J0232-2429
gmw_00009088.overview: J0232-2429 is a secure strong gravitational lens system consisting of two quasars at a redshift of 1.58 [arXiv:2206.07714]. Located in the outskirts of a galaxy cluster, the system displays residual red flux consistent with a lensing galaxy [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper upgrades J0232-2429 from a possible quasar-star projection to a secure lens after spectroscopy resolves two similar z=1.58 quasars and imaging finds lens residuals.

quotes.quote: J0232-2429 This system was originally selected by lemon2018 , who classified it as a likely quasar+star projection, despite poor seeing during observations and the small separation of 1.44 . However, we obtained further spectroscopy to confirm this classification, and resolve two quasars at z=1.58 with similar emission line profiles and continuum. There is residual red flux in the PSF-subtracted Legacy Survey image, consistent with a lensing galaxy. We therefore upgrade the classification to a secure lens. The system is in the outskirts of a cluster ( 1 \ from the centre) which has galaxies with photometric redshifts of 0.85.

gmw_00009089.name: J0907+6224
gmw_00009089.overview: J0907+6224 is a double gravitationally lensed quasar system with a source redshift of z = 1.86 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The system has a separation of 2.48 arcseconds, and its spectra show narrow absorption features in the C IV line alongside contamination from the lensing galaxy [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0907+6224 as a double quasar lens with SDSS and BOSS spectra, z=1.86 source redshift, narrow C IV absorption, and galaxy-contaminated continua.

quotes.quote: J0907+6224 This double has both SDSS and BOSS spectra. It has a source redshift of z = 1.86 and a separation of 2.48 . The BOSS spectrum shows two narrow absorption features in the Civ line. The different continua between the BOSS and SDSS spectra is likely attributed to the different fibre apertures (2 and 3 \ respectively), and contamination from the lensing galaxy. The SDSS pipeline catalogues this as two objects, with an i band magnitude of 18.93 for the Northern component, allowing a colour selection from SQLS to select this as a potential lens.

gmw_00009090.name: J0936-1211
gmw_00009090.overview: J0936-1211 is a lensed quasar system featuring a background quasar at a redshift of z = 2.00 [arXiv:2206.07714]. This background source is aligned within 2.1 arcseconds of a foreground galaxy at z = 0.260, which acts as the gravitational lens [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0936-1211 as a lensed quasar because spectra reveal a blue z=2 quasar and foreground galaxy, with Gaia geometry and PSF modelling indicating a counterimage.

quotes.quote: J0936-1211 The NTT-EFOSC2 spectrum shows two clear traces, one of a z = 2.00 quasar with a very blue continuum, and one red trace with absorption features of a z = 0.260 galaxy. There are hints of broad emission features including Civ and the blue continuum in this latter trace. Indeed, the two Gaia detections coupled with the alignment of the background quasar within 2.1 \ of the foreground galaxy strongly suggests the presence of a counterimage. Two PSFs and a galaxy are required for clean residuals of the Pan-STARRS imaging, with the second PSF in the location of an expected counterimage, namely 0.9 \ from the galaxy opposite the bright image. We therefore classify J0936-1211 as a lensed quasar, however, deeper imaging is needed to definitively confirm this.

gmw_00009091.name: J0405-3730
gmw_00009091.overview: J0405-3730 is a double-image quasar system located at a redshift of z = 3.585 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The system is gravitationally lensed by at least two galaxies, which are postulated to be undergoing a merger that triggers star formation [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0405-3730 as a z=3.585 quasar lens whose spectra and imaging suggest two lensing galaxies, merger-driven star formation, and a complex lens configuration.

quotes.quote: J0405-3730 The 2D spectra of this double show the resolved traces of the images of a quasar at z = 3.585, but also three narrow emission lines located between the two quasar traces, but much closer to the brighter trace. These lines are consistent with Oii , H and Sii at z = 0.2295, and a narrow absorption line in the brighter trace can also be attributed to Na at this redshift. The imaging of this system shows that at least two galaxies are responsible for the lensing, one between the two images but offset and highly elliptical and another offset. We postulate that the lens is a merger between two galaxies, triggering star formation explaining the aforementioned narrow lines. High-resolution imaging will be needed to better understand this system.

gmw_00009092.name: J0833+2612
gmw_00009092.overview: J0833+2612 is a broad-absorption line quasar at a redshift of approximately 3.26 [arXiv:2206.07714]. It is identified as a quadruple gravitational lens system, with imaging revealing a faint counterimage and radio data suggesting the source may be radio-loud [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0833+2612 as a likely quad BAL quasar lens selected by Gaia offset, with spectra resolving two z~3.26 traces and imaging showing a faint counterimage.

quotes.quote: J0833+2612 This lens was selected as a single Gaia detection offset from a Pan-STARRS morphological galaxy (Section offset ). The slit was positioned at 167.4 degrees East of North, and clearly resolves two traces of a BAL quasar at z 3.26. The DECALS residual imaging shows a likely faint counterimage, confirming this system as a quad. CFHT and z band imaging also show a counterimage, and our PSF-subtracted modelling of these data is shown in Figure fig:J0833 . There is a coincident signal in VLASS at the location of the system, suggesting the source could be radio-loud.

gmw_00009093.name: J2316+0610
gmw_00009093.overview: J2316+0610 is a strong gravitational lensing system with a lens galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.378 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The background lensed source is a broad-absorption line quasar at a redshift of z = 1.955 that is resolved into two components [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J2316+0610 as a lens using deeper NOT data resolving two z=1.955 quasars, and notes a BAL source plus a z=0.378 lens redshift.

quotes.quote: J2316+0610 This system was originally selected by lemon2019 who suggested a possible source redshift of z = 1.96, however their spectra were too noisy to conclusively confirm the system as a lens. Our deeper NOT data clearly resolve two quasars at z = 1.955, thus confirming this system as a lens. The source is a BAL quasar with multiple components, confirmed by a recent eBOSS spectrum. We measure a lens redshift of z = 0.378, consistent with the redshift of a nearby galaxy with a BOSS spectrum.

gmw_00009094.name: J0247+7706
gmw_00009094.overview: J0247+7706 is a gravitationally lensed system featuring two quasar traces at a redshift of z = 2.73 [arXiv:2206.07714]. These components share many common narrow absorption lines, and imaging shows faint hints of an extended lensing galaxy positioned between them [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0247+7706 as a lens after ALFOSC-NOT spectra resolve two z=2.73 quasar traces, common narrow absorptions, and imaging hints of an extended galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0247+7706 The ALFOSC-NOT spectra clearly resolve two quasar traces at z = 2.73 and many narrow absorption lines common to both components. Faint hints of an extended galaxy are seen in the PSF subtracted Pan-STARRS imaging. When adding a Sersic component to the fit, flux is allocated in all bands to this component, and its best-fit position is between the two PSFs closer to the fainter image. Given this and the very similar spectra, we classify this system as a lens, however deeper imaging should be taken to verify the detection of the lensing galaxy and characterise its properties.

gmw_00009095.name: J0247-6349
gmw_00009095.overview: J0247-6349 is a strong gravitational lensing system [arXiv:2206.07714]. The system consists of a z = 2.295 quasar that appears as two point sources on either side of a red lensing galaxy [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper classifies J0247-6349 as a lens despite poor seeing because spectra show z=2.295 quasar emission and images show two point sources around a red galaxy.

quotes.quote: J0247-6349 The seeing for this spectrum was particularly poor, leading to low signal-to-noise and a blended spectrum. The 2D spectral modelling allocates emission line flux to both components, however cannot be taken as reliable. Nevertheless, the emission lines are clearly from a z = 2.295 quasar, and the Legacy Survey images show two point sources either side of a red galaxy. Therefore we classify this system as a lens. This is supported by the independent spectra and discovery of this system by spiniello2019 .

gmw_00009096.name: J0350-4611
gmw_00009096.overview: J0350-4611 is a double-component system containing a quasar at a redshift of z = 1.505 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The system is a candidate strongly lensed broad absorption line quasar, with the lensing galaxy visible in imaging residuals [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0350-4611 as a possible useful lensed BAL quasar: low-S/N spectra show z=1.505 quasar emission in both components and lens residuals appear in imaging.

quotes.quote: J0350-4611 The NTT-EFOSC2 spectra of this double are low signal-to-noise but each component shows emission lines of a z = 1.505 quasar. The Civ profile is not apparent, and there is a significant drop in flux at the expected wavelength and blueward of Civ in both components. The lens is seen in the Legacy Survey PSF-subtracted residuals. Deeper spectra should be taken to understand the nature of this system, as it is possibly a useful lensed BAL quasar.

gmw_00009097.name: J1255+0737
gmw_00009097.overview: J1255+0737 is a double-quasar lens candidate located at a redshift of z = 2.145 [arXiv:2206.07714]. Spectra resolve two quasar images, while Gaia EDR3 data show a third detection associated with a galaxy two arcseconds to the east [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper reports J1255+0737 as a z=2.145 double-quasar lens candidate, with WHT spectra resolving both images and Gaia EDR3 showing a third detection at the lens galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1255+0737 WHT-ISIS long-slit spectra were taken with the slit position angle at 5.6 degrees East of North. The spectra reveal two quasars at z = 2.145. In Gaia EDR3 there is a third detection associated to a galaxy 2 arcseconds to the East, which could be due to a another image of the source blended with the galaxy, as in the case of SDSSJ1405+0959 rusu2014 . High-resolution imaging is needed to see if this is an interesting case of a lens with two lens planes and three images.

gmw_00009098.name: J1307+0642
gmw_00009098.overview: J1307+0642 is a strong gravitational lensing system featuring a foreground lensing galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.23 [arXiv:2206.07714]. This galaxy lenses a background quasar at a redshift of z = 2.03, which is resolved into two X-ray and optically blue point sources [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J1307+0642 as a lens after deeper spectra detect quasar emission, while imaging shows multiple blue Gaia detections around the foreground galaxy.

quotes.quote: J1307+0642 This system was first selected as a lens candidate by lemon2019 , however, the spectra were too shallow to detect quasar emission lines. The new 1200s WHT-ISIS exposure shows clearly two traces of a z = 2.03 quasar, with the fainter image blended with a z = 0.23 galaxy. A SDSS spectrum confirms our redshift of the lensing galaxy. Archival Chandra data -- originally from observations of 3C281, which is situated less than 3 arcminutes from this system -- resolve two X-ray point sources coincident with two optically blue point sources either side of a bright galaxy. The fluxes of the two components are 3.7 1.9 10e-14 and 0.9 0.4 10e-14 in the broad 0.5-7keV band.

gmw_00009099.name: J1003+0651
gmw_00009099.overview: J1003+0651 is a gravitationally lensed quasar system [arXiv:2206.07714]. It features a foreground lensing galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.225, which lenses a background quasar at z = 2.56 into two resolved traces [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J1003+0651 as a lensed quasar: three Gaia detections align with a z=0.225 SDSS galaxy, and WHT data resolve two z=2.56 quasar traces.

quotes.quote: J1003+0651 This double has three GDR2 detections, centred on a SDSS spectroscopic galaxy at z = 0.225. A feature around 4300Å is indicative of a quasar, and subtraction of the SDSS model galaxy template reveals quasar emission lines at z = 2.56. Our WHT data resolve two quasar traces at the same redshift, either side of the lensing galaxy, thus confirming this system as al lensed quasar.

gmw_00009100.name: J1526-1400
gmw_00009100.overview: J1526-1400 is a double lensed-quasar system with a source redshift of z = 0.648 [arXiv:2206.07714]. The system features a bright, low-redshift lensing galaxy at z = 0.096 that is detected by Gaia [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1526-1400 as a double with the lowest-redshift lensed-quasar source known in the sample and a bright z=0.096 lensing galaxy detected by Gaia.

quotes.quote: J1526-1400 This double has the lowest redshift source yet discovered for lensed quasars, z = 0.648, and a particularly bright, low-redshift lensing galaxy at z = 0.096. Similar to J1003+0651, the lensing galaxy is detected by Gaia . Given the brightness of this lens ( i= 16.02), and that of J0416+7428, we can consider how many bright lensing galaxies are expected based on the OM10 mocks. With the two doubles presented here, and Q2237+030 huchra1985 , we have 3 lenses with multiple Gaia detections, and lensing galaxies brighter than i = 16.5. This seems to be consistent with the 4-7 expected across the whole sky based on OM10 but it is perhaps surprising given that our selection techniques are based mostly on source colours.

gmw_00009101.name: J2017+6204
gmw_00009101.overview: J2017+6204 is a broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of z=1.72 [arXiv:2012.10051, arXiv:2206.07714]. It is a strongly lensed system, with adaptive optics imaging resolving four lensed images and the lensing galaxy [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper confirms GraL J201749.1+620443 as a compact lensed quasar candidate, unresolved in Keck seeing but showing a reddened BAL quasar spectrum.
