quotes.quote: GraL J201749.1+620443 --- This source was presented in as a candidate lensed quasar with three detected lensed images, an ERT probability of P_ ERT = 0.74 , and a compact maximum separation of just 0 7. This was the smallest lens system candidate presented in that paper, as well as lowest ERT probability that we have confirmed; only one candidate had an even lower P_ ERT = 0.60 in that paper, which we spectroscopically identified as an asterism of Galactic stars (J011559.5+562506; Table 6). The Keck spectrum did not clearly resolve this quasar as spatially extended, which is consistent with the compact lens configuration, comparable to the 1 0 seeing of that night. The spectrum itself shows a reddened BAL quasar with several unidentified narrow absorption features (e.g., at 4737, 4778, and 7090 ). No radio or X-ray counterparts are reported by NED.

sources.summary: The paper reports J2017+6204 as a z=1.72 BAL quasar lens, with adaptive-optics imaging resolving four images and the lensing galaxy after blended NOT spectra.

quotes.quote: J2017+6204 Two NOT-ALFOSC 900s spectra were taken at position angles of 124.2 and 50.7 degrees East of North, however both showed a blended trace of a z = 1.72 BAL quasar. Adaptive optics imaging with OSIRIS on Keck 2 resolves the four images and lensing galaxy, and is shown in Figue fig:osiris . The system was also independently observed by stern2021 who obtained unresolved spectroscopy, corroborating our reported redshift.

gmw_00009102.name: J0030-3358
gmw_00009102.overview: J0030-3358 is a double-image gravitationally lensed system [arXiv:2206.07714]. The lensing galaxy is located at a redshift of z=0.715 and exhibits a 4000 Å continuum break along with calcium and G-band absorption features [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0030-3358 as a double lens with a fainter-image continuum break and absorption features placing the lens at z=0.715, plus possible C IV absorption.

quotes.quote: J0030-3358 A jump in the continuum of the fainter image of this double is likely associated to the 4000 \ break of the lensing galaxy, as associated Ca H, K and G-band absorption features are also seen, placing the lens at z =0.715. This is supported by the Legacy Survey photometric redshift estimate of 0.84 0.06 zhou2021 , with a small discrepancy likely due to blending with the quasar light. There is absorption on the blue side of the Civ line seen in both images, however this could also be attributed to Milky Way absorption since it is coincident with z =0 Ca H and K absorption.

gmw_00009103.name: J0343-2828
gmw_00009103.overview: J0343-2828 is a lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z = 1.655 [arXiv:2206.07714]. It is gravitationally lensed by two foreground galaxies at z = 0.385, which produce non-point images and a fifth image between the lenses [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0343-2828 as a lensed galaxy rather than a quasar, with NTT spectra showing a z=1.655 source, z=0.385 lens, and HST-resolved non-point images.

quotes.quote: J0343-2828 This lens was selected as a potential lensed quasar candidate through the single Gaia detection offset method using DR2 ( G_ DR2 = 21.29), and looked promising given the compact PSFs. However, spectroscopic follow-up with the NTT in October 2019 confirmed the source as a z = 1.655 galaxy, with a lens at z = 0.385. The coordinates are: R.A., Dec. = (55.7978, -28.4779). HST imaging ( HST Proposal 15652, P.I.:Treu) shows that indeed the images are not consistent with point sources, and further show two lensing galaxies with a fifth image between them schmidt2022 . J0500-5534 This lens was selected as a potential lensed quasar through the single Gaia detection offset method in Gaia DR2 ( G_ DR2 = 20.28) and a marginally red colour of W1--W2=0.53.

gmw_00009104.name: J0500-5534
gmw_00009104.overview: J0500-5534 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.620 [arXiv:2206.07714]. Its imaging is well-fit by a Sersic lens profile with four point sources in a short-axis cusp configuration [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper presents J0500-5534 as a Gaia-selected lensed galaxy whose DES imaging fits four point sources plus a Sersic lens and whose FIRE spectrum shows z=0.620 emission lines.

quotes.quote: J0500-5534 This lens was selected as a potential lensed quasar through the single Gaia detection offset method in Gaia DR2 ( G_ DR2 = 20.28) and a marginally red colour of W1--W2=0.53. The coordinates are: R.A., Dec. = (75.1017, -55.5796). The DES imaging is well-fit by four point sources and a Sersic galaxy profile, with the positions forming a short-axis cusp configuration, which is indeed well-fit by an SIE+shear lens model. Follow-up spectroscopy with FIRE on the 6.5-meter Baade Magellan telescope shows clearly resolved narrow emission lines of a z = 0.620 galaxy. Figure fig:lensedgals shows the 1D spectrum, with the following emission lines marked: H , [ Siii ] , and He i .

gmw_00009105.name: J0920+4521
gmw_00009105.overview: J0920+4521 is a lensed galaxy system that features a blue arc located east of a group of four galaxies with similar colors [arXiv:2206.07714]. Spectroscopy indicates the presence of foreground luminous red galaxies at a redshift of z = 0.546, alongside a background source at z = 0.956 [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0920+4521 as a lensed galaxy system with a blue arc near four similar-colour galaxies and spectroscopy indicating foreground LRGs plus a background source.

quotes.quote: J0920+4521 This system was discovered through a search for multiple blue catalogued detections in SDSS within a few arcseconds of a red photometric galaxy. Legacy Survey imaging shows a blue arc East of a group of four galaxies all with similar colours (see Figure fig:lensedgals ). Two of these galaxies have existing SDSS spectra, one an LRG at z = 0.546, and the other with narrow emission lines at z = 0.956. However, closer inspection of the latter spectrum shows also an LRG spectrum consistent with z = 0.546, suggesting the emission lines are from a background source, possibly a counterimage of the observed arc. Follow-up spectroscopy with ISIS on the WHT were taken on 31 March 2017 at two position angles -- along the arc, and through the brightest part of the arc and the galaxy with the spectrum with narrow emission lines.

gmw_00009106.name: J1059+4251
gmw_00009106.overview: J1059+4251 is a high-redshift lensed galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.793 [arXiv:2206.07714]. Hubble Space Telescope imaging reveals that the system features a complex lensing configuration containing three lensing galaxies [arXiv:2206.07714].

sources.summary: The paper reports J1059+4251 as a lensed galaxy found around red galaxies, with WHT spectroscopy of a bright arc at z=2.793 and HST imaging showing three lensing galaxies.

quotes.quote: J1059+4251 This system was discovered through a search in SDSS imaging for multiple photometric quasar candidates around photometric red galaxies. Follow-up spectroscopy with ISIS on the WHT on 31 March 2017 targeting the bright arc reveals a z = 2.793 galaxy. Figure fig:lensedgals shows the 1D spectrum and the DECaLS grz colour image of the system. HST imaging reveals three lensing galaxies and a complex lensing configuration. This system is examined in detail in citro2021 .

gmw_00009107.name: RX J0437--47
gmw_00009107.overview: RX J0437--47 is a Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1807.10246, arXiv:astro-ph/0412636] located at a redshift of approximately 0.05 [arXiv:1807.10246, arXiv:astro-ph/9903198]. The active nucleus exhibits modest X-ray variability [arXiv:1807.10246] and a transient, periodic extreme-ultraviolet signal [arXiv:1807.10246, arXiv:astro-ph/0412636].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes four NuSTAR observations of HE 0436-4717, finding modest X-ray variability, a weakly variable power law, distant narrow Fe K emission, constant reflection, and lower limits on coronal temperature.

quotes.quote: We present the multi epoch spectral analysis of HE 0436-4717 , a bright Seyfert 1 galaxy serendipitously observed by the high energy satellite NuSTAR four times between December 2014 and December 2015. The source flux shows modest variability within each pointing and among the four observations. Spectra are well modelled in terms of a weakly variable primary power law with constant photon index ( =2.01 0.08). A constant narrow Fe K emission line suggests that this feature has an origin far from the central black hole, while a broad relativistic component is not required by the data.

This source is a type 1 Seyfert galaxy Vero06 lying at redshift z =0.053 Wiso00 , and hosting a supermassive black hole with mass M_ BH =5.9 10^ 7 M_ , Grup10 . The spectral coverage of this active galactic nucleus is very peculiar since it is one of the eight AGN that have been detected in the extreme ultraviolet (EUV) band Barstow03 . Moreover, a long monitoring of 20 days by the Extreme UltraViolet Explorer (EUVE) allowed to find a possible periodic variability (P=0.9 days) in the EUV Halp96,Helpern03,Leighly05 .

sources.summary: The paper describes RX J0437.1 4711 as having a 20-day EUVE light curve, a 0.908-day periodic signal, ordinary Seyfert 1 classification, and transient periodicity.

quotes.quote: Three objects had exceptionally long light curves: 33 days for Ton S180, 26 days for 1H 0419 577, and 20 days for RX J0437.1 4711. Periodicity analysis as outlined above revealed evidence for a 2.08-day signal in Ton S180 with global confidence level of 98 , a 0.908-day signal in RX J0437.4 4711, and a 5.8-day signal in 1H 0419 577 with a global confidence level of only 64 . Interestingly, the periodic signal in RX J0437.1 4711 appears to be transient; periodicity analysis of the first and second halves of the light curve show no detectable signal, and a signal at 0.89-day with global confidence of 96 , respectively.
Ton S180 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, 1H 0419 577 is a Seyfert 1.5, and both NGC 4395 and RX J0437.1 4711 are both classified as ordinary Seyfert 1 galaxies.
On the other hand, we observe that the periodicity appears to be transient in both NGC 4395 and RX J0437.1 4711 in that in both cases it is detected only in the second halves of the light curves.

sources.summary: The paper obtains the first optical spectrum for RX J0437--47, finds a z=0.051 Seyfert 1 galaxy, and classifies it as a broad-line Seyfert 1.

quotes.quote: RX J0437--47: This was the only source in the sample without a published optical spectrum. Low-resolution optical spectra were therefore acquired from the 3.9m Anglo-Australian Telescope using the RGO and FORS spectrographs, with total integration times of 1000 and 900 sec respectively, in 2 \ seeing conditions. The combined, flux-calibrated spectrum, shown in Figure 1, is that of a Seyfert 1 galaxy at a redshift of z = 0.051 .

gmw_00009108.name: SDSS-H5
gmw_00009108.overview: SDSS-H5 is an early-type galaxy that contains a partially-depleted core [arXiv:1610.00801]. Two-dimensional modeling of its light distribution reveals a break radius of 0.55 kiloparsecs and a very smooth transition region [arXiv:1610.00801].

sources.summary: The paper remeasures SDSS-H5's depleted core with a 2D model, reducing its break radius to 0.55 kpc and warning against overestimated sharp-transition core sizes.

quotes.quote: Here, this ``binary black hole scouring scenario'' is probed at its extremes by investigating the two galaxies reported to have the largest partially-depleted cores found to date: 2MASX J09194427+5622012 and 2MASX J17222717+3207571 (the brightest galaxy in Abell 2261). We have fit these galaxy's two-dimensional light distribution using the model, and found that the former galaxy has a break radius R_ b,cS =0.55 kpc, three times smaller than the published value. We use this galaxy to caution that other reportedly large break radii may too have been over-estimated if they were derived using the ``sharp-transition'' (inner core)-to-(outer S ersic) model.

From our fit, we obtained a break radius R_ b,cS 0.55 kpc for SDSS-H5, a value 3 times smaller than the R_ b,cS reported by , and compatible with their cusp radius R_ = 0.6 kpc. We attribute this large discrepancy to the fact that used a simplified formulation of the model, assuming a sharp transition between the and the power-law, while our fit revealed that the transition is instead very smooth ( 1.2 ; Comparing our fit results with the literature ).

gmw_00009109.name: MACSJ1149-WR1
gmw_00009109.overview: MACSJ1149-WR1 is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.76 [arXiv:2404.10037]. It is experiencing a young starburst that likely hosts a large number of Wolf-Rayet stars, with no spectral evidence supporting the presence of an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2404.10037].

sources.summary: The paper studies MACSJ1149-WR1 as a z=2.76 star-forming Wolf-Rayet galaxy, detecting auroral lines, deriving ISM temperatures and abundances, finding a young starburst, and rejecting an AGN interpretation.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Host properties R.A. & degree & 1.773851e+02 Dec. & degree & 2.237533e+01

quotes.quote: We report JWST/NIRSpec observations of a star-forming galaxy at z=2.76 , . We securely detect two temperature-sensitive auroral lines, 6312 (7.4 ) and 7320+7331 doublets (10 ), and tentatively 5755 ( 2.3 ) for the first time in an individual galaxy at z>1 . We perform a detailed analysis of its interstellar media (ISM), and derive electron temperatures, various heavy element abundances (O/H, N/O, S/O, and Ar/O) in the hot ionized region, and the neutral fraction in the warm ionized region.

\ shows a broad feature at the wavelength of 4686, which consists of a broad ( 1000 km/s), blue-shifted ( 110 km/s) line component. Taken together with its mildly elevated N/O abundance, we conclude that \ is experiencing a young starburst ( 10 Myr), likely hosting a large number of Wolf-Rayet stars. None of its spectral features support the presence of AGN, including: (i) the absence of broad components and velocity shifts in Hydrogen recombination lines, (ii) low 1.257 m / \ ratio, and (iii) the absence of high-ionization lines.

gmw_00009110.name: SCG J101231+680942
gmw_00009110.overview: SCG J101231+680942 is a radio source identified across multiple historical radio surveys [arXiv:1607.04056]. It exhibits extended emission at the resolution of the Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope, with a power-law spectral index fitted between 38 MHz and 1.4 GHz [arXiv:1607.04056].

sources.summary: The paper treats SCG J101231+680942 as a radio source known from previous surveys, fits a 38 MHz-1.4 GHz spectral index, and finds the GMRT excess may reflect extended emission rather than a secure ultra-steep spectrum.

quotes.quote: SCG J101231+680942 This source has been identified in a number of previous radio surveys; for example in the NVSS its identifier is NVSS J101231+680940. Additional flux density measurements for this source exist at 38, 74, 151 and 325 MHz, from a number of historic radio surveys. These flux density measurements are listed in Table tab:ussc , with the appropriate correction factor required to bring them into line with the 2012MNRAS.423L..30S flux density scale. Subsequently, we use the corrected flux densities to fit a spectral index in the form of a power-law between 38 MHz and 1.4 GHz. We present the flux density as a function of frequency in the top panel of Figure fig:uss_spix , as well as the spectral index fitted to the data from the literature, = -1.27 0.22 .

This fit suggests a spectral index that is marginally shallower than the two-point spectral index indicated in Table tab:uss_src . Based on the multi-frequency spectral index, we cannot conclusively say whether this source has an ultra-steep-spectrum; from Figure fig:uss_spix , the integrated flux density recovered by the GMRT appears to be in excess of that expected from the flux density measurements in the literature. However, from Figure fig:uss_src (top panel) it appears that this source is extended at the resolution of the GMRT, so it is possible that this excess is a result of the superior sensitivity to faint emission compared to previous surveys.

gmw_00009111.name: SCG J102419+671742
gmw_00009111.overview: SCG J102419+671742 is a radio galaxy also identified as 8C 1020+675 [arXiv:1607.04056]. It is classified as an ultra-steep spectrum radio source that exhibits a complex morphology with extended radio emission [arXiv:1607.04056].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SCG J102419+671742/8C 1020+675 in prior surveys, finds it extended in GMRT and NVSS, fits a steep -1.31 spectrum, and classifies it as a USS radio source with complex extended morphology.

quotes.quote: SCG J102419+671742 SCG J102419+671742 (also known as 8C 1020+675) has been identified in a number of previous radio surveys between 38 MHz and 1.4 GHz. From Figure fig:uss_src (bottom panel) it is extended at both the resolution of the GMRT and NVSS. Historic values of the flux density for 8C 1020+675 are presented in Table tab:ussc , with their associated flux scale and the conversion factor necessary to make them consistent with the 2012MNRAS.423L..30S flux scale. Figure fig:uss_spix (bottom panel) also presents the flux density as a function of frequency for this source, along with the multi-frequency spectral index fit. We model the spectral index behaviour of 8C 1020+675 as a power-law between 38 MHz and 1.4 GHz, deriving a spectral index of = -1.31 0.26 .

While this is marginally flatter than the two-point fit listed in Table tab:uss_src ( = -1.41 0.07) it is sufficiently steep to qualify as a USS radio source. The flux density recovered by the GMRT is in good agreement with the multi-frequency fit to measurements from the literature. The postage stamp image of 8C 1020+675 presented in Figure fig:uss_src (lower panel) reveals a complex morphology, with extended radio emission on angular scales up to 120 arcsec.

gmw_00009112.name: SCG J101808+665632
gmw_00009112.overview: SCG J101808+665632 is a radio galaxy candidate that appears reasonably compact in Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope observations [arXiv:1607.04056]. It exhibits a steep radio spectrum and is reasonably bright at 325 MHz, though it lacks independent historic detections and redshift measurements [arXiv:1607.04056].

sources.summary: SCG J101808+665632 is compact in the GMRT image, has the steepest NVSS-counterpart spectrum and bright 325 MHz flux, but lacks independent historic detections or redshift confirmation for a high-redshift radio-galaxy interpretation.

quotes.quote: As can be seen in Figure fig:uss_src , SCG J101808+665632 and SCG J102253+672121 (panels two and three, respectively) appear reasonably compact at the resolution of the GMRT. SCG J101808+665632 has the steepest spectrum of all sources with NVSS counterparts ( = -1.53 0.06) and is reasonably bright at 325 MHz (an integrated flux density S = 59.30 4.23 mJy). These sources have not been detected in any other historic radio surveys, so their steep spectra cannot yet be independently confirmed; likewise no redshift measurements exist, so we cannot yet investigate whether these may be HzRGs. However, being both bright and compact, they present themselves as promising candidates.

gmw_00009113.name: SCG J101538+672844
gmw_00009113.overview: SCG J101538+672844 is a blazar that exhibits a steep rising radio spectrum [arXiv:1607.04056]. Although its spectral energy distribution shows no obvious signs of variability, monitoring data reveals that the source has been steadily increasing in flux density since 2009 [arXiv:1607.04056].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SCG J101538+672844/CGRaBS J1015+6728 as a blazar with a steep rising spectrum, provides its first sub-GHz flux density, and notes steady OVRO brightening despite a nonvariable-looking SED.

quotes.quote: SCG J101538+672844 We find a single source with a spectral index more than 3 _2 above the mean spectral index ( _2) of the flat-spectrum population (i.e. a steep rising spectrum). The two-point spectral index of this source is = 1.73 0.07 . This source has also been identified in the literature in a number of high-frequency radio surveys; for example, one identifier is CGRaBS J1015+6728 2008ApJS..175...97H . We present the flux densities listed in other catalogues, alongside the conversion factor required to bring them onto the 2012MNRAS.423L..30S flux density scale in Table tab:ussc . Previous work suggests that CGRaBS J1015+6728 is a blazar; flux density measurements from the literature between 1.4 and 15 GHz indicate a rising spectrum with a turnover at higher frequencies (see Table tab:ussc ).

We recover an integrated flux density of 3.09 0.31 mJy; a value that appears consistent with this behaviour. To our knowledge this is the first flux density measurement for this source below 1 GHz. Whilst the SED of CGRaBS J1015+6728 does not show any obvious signs of variability (see the top panel of Figure fig:gps_src ) the OVRO database Available online at http://www.astro.caltech.edu/ovroblazars/ indicates that this source has been increasing in flux density steadily since monitoring observations began in 2009.

gmw_00009114.name: SCG J101723+673633
gmw_00009114.overview: SCG J101723+673633 is a radio source that has been observed over several decades [arXiv:1607.04056]. Its flux density measurements appear stable over a period of several years, though low-frequency discrepancies suggest possible intrinsic variability [arXiv:1607.04056].

sources.summary: The paper describes SCG J101723+673633 as a decades-observed source whose flux densities look stable over several years, while higher-frequency monitoring and low-frequency discrepancies leave longer-term variability unresolved.

quotes.quote: SCG J101723+673633 This source is also known in the literature as 87GB 101339.1+675144. Observations of this source have been performed over the course of several decades. From Figure fig:gps_src (centre panel) the flux density measurements appear to be reasonably stable, at least over a period of several years. However, all measurements of the flux density at 4.85 and 8.4 GHz were taken in the period 1986--1999; more recent observations at higher frequencies are required to test the variability on longer timescales. At low frequencies, there is a discrepancy between the flux densities from this work and from WENSS; this may be due to intrinsic variability, or alternatively may be due to the general scatter in flux density ratio between the SCG and WENSS catalogues at low flux densities.

gmw_00009115.name: SCG J103401+683226
gmw_00009115.overview: SCG J103401+683226 is a radio source with an approximately flat spectrum between 325 MHz and 8.4 GHz [arXiv:1607.04056]. It exhibits signs of flux density variation at high frequencies, as well as potential intrinsic variability at lower frequencies [arXiv:1607.04056].

sources.summary: The paper describes SCG J103401+683226 as a long-observed source with an approximately flat 325 MHz-8.4 GHz spectrum, high-frequency variability hints, and a GMRT flux about 50 percent above WENSS at low frequencies.

quotes.quote: SCG J103401+683226 This source is also known in the literature as 87GB 103023.1+684757, and has been the subject of a number of observations since the 1980s. From Figure fig:gps_src (lower panel) it appears that the spectrum of SCG J103401+683226 is approximately flat between 325 MHz and 8.4 GHz, with signs of variation at high frequencies. Flux density measurements in the 5--8 GHz range appear to be approximately consistent for data taken between 1986--1990 ( 1991ApJS...75.1011G , 1992MNRAS.254..655P , 1996ApJS..103..427G ) and 1994--1999 ( 2007ApJS..171...61H ). More recent observations from the 2002--2003 period perhaps suggest variation on longer timescales, as the flux density has been observed to decrease at 5 GHz 2008ApJ...689..108L .

At lower frequencies, there is a discrepancy in the flux density recovered by the WENSS and this work. For this source, the GMRT recovers flux density in excess of the WENSS by approximately 50 per cent. This may be explained by intrinsic variability, although the general scatter in the flux density ratio between the SCG and WENSS catalogues at low flux densities may have some contribution to this difference.

gmw_00009116.name: D23H--1
gmw_00009116.overview: The host galaxy D23H--1 has a central region characterized by a low signal-to-noise ratio spectrum [arXiv:1706.08965]. Because of this low signal-to-noise ratio, model fitting is poorly constrained, preventing a robust measurement of the galaxy's central velocity dispersion [arXiv:1706.08965].

sources.summary: For the D23H--1 host, low-SNR spectra make velocity-dispersion fitting degenerate; the paper restricts Monte Carlo trials, adopts 77 km/s, and cannot robustly measure the central dispersion.

quotes.quote: For the spectrum of D23H--1, the relatively low SNR of the spectra causes a degeneracy in the best-fitting velocity dispersion. Due to the large errors in the observed spectrum, the ^2 minimization is not able to resolve the shallow, narrow absorption lines. Instead, the minimization procedure finds a good fit with larger values of few hundred , essentially fitting only a few broad absorption lines instead of the myriad of low SNR, low equivalent width absorption lines present in the spectrum. In this case, we use the trials corresponding to a limited (but conservatively large) range of to determine the best-fitting velocity dispersion. We perform Monte Carlo simulations until this limited range contains at least 1000 trials, to robustly estimate the uncertainty induced by the measurement errors.

We adopt = 77 18 \ in this case. We also note that extracting the central region of the host galaxy to D23H--1 results in a low SNR spectrum. The model fitting becomes less constrained and we are no longer able to robustly measure the central velocity dispersion of this galaxy.

gmw_00009117.name: D3--13
gmw_00009117.overview: The galaxy D3--13 hosts a supermassive black hole at its nucleus [arXiv:1706.08965]. This black hole is the most massive in its sample, exhibiting a tidal disruption event with the slowest decay timescale and an Eddington ratio of 0.07 [arXiv:1706.08965].

sources.summary: For D3--13, the paper measures a 133 km/s velocity dispersion consistent with earlier 120 km/s work, finds a 0.07 Eddington ratio among high-mass black-hole events, and identifies it as having the slowest decay timescale.

quotes.quote: The velocity dispersion of D3--13 was measured using a similar template fitting procedure by Gezari2006 , and was measured to be 120 10 km s ^ 1 . Using our resampling approach we find = 133 6 , slightly higher but consistent within the mutual uncertainties.

The peak luminosity of all TDEs is consistent with being at the Eddington limit except for the two events with the highest black hole masses, which have Eddington ratios of 0.02 for TDE1 and 0.07 for D3--13.
The lowest mass black hole (iPTF--16fnl) hosted the fastest decaying TDE (see Blago2017 ), and the most massive black hole (D3--13) has the slowest decay timescale.

gmw_00009118.name: ORC4
gmw_00009118.overview: The central galaxy of ORC4 is a massive, quiescent system at a redshift of z=0.4512 with an old stellar population and no signs of ongoing star formation [arXiv:2310.15162]. It hosts a 40-kiloparsec ionized gas nebula with high velocity dispersion, likely driven by a galactic wind shock, and exhibits radio emission attributed to an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2310.15162].

sources.summary: The central galaxy of ORC4 is a massive old system at z=0.4512 with extended high-EW ionized gas, large velocity gradients, likely AGN radio emission, and shock-driven infall linked to a galactic wind.

quotes.quote: Here we report spectroscopic data on an ORC including strong \ emission tracing ionized gas in the central galaxy of ORC4 at z=0.4512 . The physical extent of the \ emission is 40 kpc in diameter, larger than expected for a typical early-type galaxy but an order of magnitude smaller than the large-scale radio continuum emission. We detect a 200 \ velocity gradient across the \ nebula, as well as a high velocity dispersion of 180 . The \ equivalent width (EW, 50 ) is extremely high for a quiescent galaxy. The morphology, kinematics, and strength of the \ emission are consistent with the infall of shock ionized gas near the galaxy, following a larger-scale, outward moving shock driven by a galactic wind.

A spectral energy distribution (SED) fit to the optical and infrared emission in the central galaxy of ORC4 (Extended Data Fig. 2) reveals that the galaxy is massive (log M_*/M_ = 11.27 0.06 ) and has an old stellar population, with a stellar age of t_ age = 6.0 \ (+/-1.6) Gyr. While the SED fit has only a 3 contribution from an AGN, the radio continuum emission in this galaxy is likely due to an AGN, as there is no indication of on-going star formation. The observed radio flux of 1.43 mJy at 325 MHz corresponds to a luminosity of 1.24 10^ 24 \ W \ Hz^ 1 at a restframe frequency of 200 MHz, given the redshift and radio spectral index ; this is 1.5 orders of magnitude fainter than the break in the radio AGN luminosity function .

gmw_00009119.name: Canis Major (CMa) dwarf galaxy
gmw_00009119.overview: The Canis Major (CMa) dwarf galaxy is a disrupted dwarf galaxy, though its classification as a distinct remnant dwarf rather than a Galactic-disk overdensity has been a subject of debate [arXiv:1001.4289]. The system has experienced prolonged star and cluster formation spanning approximately 10 billion years [arXiv:1001.4289].

sources.summary: The paper argues Canis Major is a disrupted dwarf rather than a Galactic-disk overdensity, associating several clusters with it and finding an AMR consistent with prolonged star and cluster formation.

quotes.quote: Canis Major (CMa) dwarf galaxy Although its existence as a disrupted dwarf galaxy and its connection to the Monoceros overdensity is still the subject of debate (Mateu et al. 2009), there is good evidence supporting its distinct nature (Martin et al. 2004; Dinescu et al. 2005). Martin et al. (2004) found that four GCs (NGC 1851, 1902, 2298, 2808) occupied a tight location in phase space, close to their orbital model for CMa. Bellazzini et al. (2004) argued on the basis of position, distance and stellar populations that the old open clusters AM 2 and Tom 2 were also associated with the CMa dwarf.

Fig. 3 shows that the updated data for the Sgr and CMa dwarfs continue to reveal a similar AMR for both galaxies which is remarkably tight over a large range in age and metallicity. This further supports claims that CMa is indeed a distinct feature (i.e. a remnant dwarf galaxy) and not simply an overabundance or warp in the Galactic disk. Both galaxies appear to have been forming stars and star clusters for 10 Gyr enriching from about 1/100 solar to solar metallicity.

gmw_00009120.name: GMP 0144
gmw_00009120.overview: GMP 0144 is an early-type galaxy in the Coma cluster that exhibits a central isophotal twist and an off-center velocity dispersion peak [arXiv:0709.0691]. These features suggest the galaxy is triaxial near its center and hosts a dynamically hot, decoupled component [arXiv:0710.2190, arXiv:0709.0691].

sources.summary: The paper finds discrepant central velocity dispersions for GMP 0144 and interprets them as evidence for non-axisymmetry and a dynamically hot, decoupled central component.

quotes.quote: Fig. fig:comparison shows the comparison between the measurements of v , , H_3 , and H_4 along the minor axis of GMP 0144 obtained here and measurements of Paper II. There is a significant difference in velocity dispersions within 3 arcsec, that can be only partially attributed to the different instrumental set-up and seeing of the observing runs. The same is true for the velocity dispersion observed along the diagonal (Fig. fig:kinematics ) and major axis (Paper I). A likely explanation for the mismatch is that GMP 0144 deviates significantly from axisymmetry near its center and hosts a dynamically hot, decoupled component jthomas2006,jthomas2007 . Further observational support for this comes from the relatively large side-to-side asymmetries in all the measured slits and the strong isophotal twist ( 30^ ) measured in the very center from HST/WFPC2 imaging (Paper I).

sources.summary: The paper flags GMP0144 as a potentially triaxial Coma early-type galaxy with an off-center velocity-dispersion peak and central isophotal twist, omitting inner kinematics from modeling.

quotes.quote: GMP0144/NGC4957: The velocity dispersion peak of GMP0144 is significantly off the photometric centre. Furthermore, GMP0144 is the only galaxy in our sample that exhibits a significant isophotal twist towards the centre. Thus, GMP0144 is likely triaxial near its centre. To reduce the influence of potentially non-axisymmetric regions on our modelling, kinematic measurements inside < 4 are omitted.

gmw_00009121.name: HE2QS J2311 1417
gmw_00009121.overview: HE2QS J2311 1417 is a bright high-redshift quasar located at an emission redshift of 3.70 [arXiv:2012.07876]. It is characterized as a helium-transparent source whose far-ultraviolet spectrum displays resolved transmission spikes [arXiv:2012.07876].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HE2QS J2311 1417 as a bright z>3.5 He II-transparent quasar whose resolved transmission spikes trace highly ionized IGM regions.

quotes.quote: We present new high-resolution ( R= / 14,000 ) spectra of the two brightest He 2 transparent quasars in the far-UV (FUV) at z_ em > 3.5 , HE2QS J2311 1417 ( z_ em = 3.70 ) and HE2QS J1630 + 0435 ( z_ em = 3.81 ), obtained with the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (COS) on the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ). In the predominantly saturated He 2 absorption spectra, both sightlines show several isolated resolved (full width at half maximum FWHM 50 km s^ 1 ) transmission spikes in He 2 Ly and He 2 Ly . The incidence of such spikes decreases with increasing redshift, but both sightlines show significant spikes at z 3.5 , signaling the presence of fully ionized regions in the z 3.5 intergalactic medium (IGM).

gmw_00009122.name: HE2QS J1630 + 0435
gmw_00009122.overview: HE2QS J1630 + 0435 is a bright, high-redshift quasar located at a redshift of approximately 3.78 to 3.81 [arXiv:1806.03374, arXiv:2012.07876]. It features ultraviolet counterparts [arXiv:1806.03374], a large proximity zone [arXiv:1810.03391], and a constrained lifetime of approximately 5.8 million years [arXiv:1810.03391].

sources.summary: The paper describes J163056.335+043559.42 as a z=3.78 quasar with GALEX UV counterparts, nearby possible confusion sources, and prior but unpublished discovery history.

quotes.quote: J163056.335+043559.42 This quasar at z=3.78 also has GALEX near- and far-UV counterparts with magnitudes of 21.18 0.23 and 21.42 0.32 , respectively. The match distance between the SDSS and GALEX coordinates is 0 8 . There is a faint point source, J163056.48+043602.55, with i 22.46 close to the quasar at a separation of 3 84 that could technically be confused with the quasar in GALEX . Other nearby sources have separations of at least 10 . This object was independently discovered by Gabor Worseck (see HST GO proposal 13013) and further studied by Zheng2015 .
It has not been formally published in any quasar catalog and we therefore include the object in the newly identified quasars of the ELQS-N sample.

sources.summary: HE2QS J1630 + 0435 gives the paper's best lifetime constraint, with a large proximity zone and small redshift-derived uncertainty yielding log quasar lifetime near 0.76 Myr.

quotes.quote: It is apparent from the green histogram in Fig. fig:posterior_tQ that the best lifetime constraint we obtain is for the most luminous quasar in the sample HE2QS J1630 + 0435, which has R_ pz = 8.43 1.02 Mpc. From this distribution we deduce the lifetime to be log _ 10 t_ Q / Myr = 0.76^ +0.26 _ 0.28 . Similar to SDSS J1319 + 5202, the redshift error for this quasar translates into a small uncertainty of (R_ pz ) = 1.02 Mpc owing to the H emission line redshift. This constitutes a 12 per cent relative error on R_ pz , which allows us to constrain the lifetime with good precision.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes HE2QS J1630 + 0435 as a bright z>3.5 He II-transparent quasar whose resolved transmission spikes indicate ionized high-redshift IGM structure.

quotes.quote: We present new high-resolution ( R= / 14,000 ) spectra of the two brightest He 2 transparent quasars in the far-UV (FUV) at z_ em > 3.5 , HE2QS J2311 1417 ( z_ em = 3.70 ) and HE2QS J1630 + 0435 ( z_ em = 3.81 ), obtained with the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (COS) on the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ). In the predominantly saturated He 2 absorption spectra, both sightlines show several isolated resolved (full width at half maximum FWHM 50 km s^ 1 ) transmission spikes in He 2 Ly and He 2 Ly . The incidence of such spikes decreases with increasing redshift, but both sightlines show significant spikes at z 3.5 , signaling the presence of fully ionized regions in the z 3.5 intergalactic medium (IGM).

gmw_00009123.name: ND1
gmw_00009123.overview: ND1 is a near-infrared-dark 1.3 mm continuum source [arXiv:2204.06393]. It is located 3.1 arcseconds northeast of a primary system, suggesting it may be an associated companion galaxy that could eventually merge with it [arXiv:2204.06393].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ND1 as a NIR-dark 1.3 mm continuum source near the primary system, plausibly a companion that could merge, with FIR assumptions matching line and continuum nondetections.

quotes.quote: A nearby object is detected (9.4 ) in the 1.3 mm continuum map 3.''1 northeast of , corresponding to a projected separation of 16 kpc if the two sources are at the same redshift. We obtain the 1.3 mm band photometry of 247 65 Jy for this object within a 1.''0 radius aperture. We do not identify any line features in either the 1 mm or 3 mm band spectra extracted at the position of the nearby object, nor do we identify any counterpart in the deep multi-wavelength imaging data from HST and Spitzer . We refer to this NIR-dark nearby object as ``ND1'' and consider two interpretations for it being either a chance projection of a foreground galaxy or a companion object associated with .

Therefore, we speculate that ND1 is a companion system associated with \ which could eventually merge into a single system. Note that if we assume a typical modified blackbody (MBB) with T_ d = 35 K and _ d =1.8 for the FIR SED of ND1 and a typical L_ CII /L_ IR ratio of 10 ^ 3 , the expected \ line luminosity agrees with the non-detection of the \ line from ND1 based on the current 1 mm data. This FIR SED for ND1 is also consistent with the non-detection in the 3 mm continuum due to its faintness and the cosmic microwave background (CMB) effect .

gmw_00009124.name: 2FGL J0102.2+0943
gmw_00009124.overview: 2FGL J0102.2+0943 is a candidate high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:1703.09143]. This active galactic nucleus has a proposed redshift of approximately 0.5, based on multi-wavelength observations spanning radio, infrared, optical, and X-ray bands [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper proposes 2FGL J0102.2+0943 as an HSP blazar candidate at about redshift 0.5, based on one Swift/XRT counterpart with optical, infrared, and radio data.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J0102.2+0943 (3FGL J0102.1+0943) The error-box area of this S/N=7.09 Fermi detection was observed for a total of about 4 ksec by Swift XRT (details are reported in Appendix A). We found only one counterpart, for which we got data in X-ray ( Swift /XRT), optical (SDSS), infrared (2MASS) and radio (NVSS) bands. The diagnostic plots obtained by our blazar recognition code, with the MAD and _ ,min ^ 2 values corresponding to the best-fitting SED templates, are reported in Fig. fig:0102_metodo . The template with the minimum _ ,min ^ 2 corresponds to an HSP SED, for a best-fit redshift of z 0.5 . An HSP blazar at about such redshift is our proposed classification for 2FGL J0102.2+0943.

gmw_00009125.name: 2FGL J0116.6-6153
gmw_00009125.overview: 2FGL J0116.6-6153 is a blazar with a tentative redshift of approximately 0.4 [arXiv:1703.09143]. While previously classified as an intermediate-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacertae object, multi-wavelength analysis suggests its best-guess classification is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2FGL J0116.6-6153 as an ISP BL Lac in 3FGL/3LAC, finds one X-ray counterpart, and classifies its SED as an HSP blazar near redshift 0.4.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J0116.6-6153 (3FGL J0116.3-6153) This ray source is reported with a 9.9 significance in the 3FGL. It was unassociated in the 2FGL catalogue, but in the 3FGL and 3LAC catalogues it is classified as an ISP BL Lac object with unknown redshift. In the 3.3 ksec Swift /XRT image, we found only one X-ray source as a possible counterpart (Fig. 0116_ass ). The broad-band SED, obtained combining the multi-wavelength fluxes of this counterpart, was analysed with our method and the resulted plots are displayed in Fig. fig:0116_metodo . These indicate, as best-guess classification, an HSP blazar with a tentative redshift of 0.4 .

gmw_00009126.name: 2FGL J0143.6-5844
gmw_00009126.overview: 2FGL J0143.6-5844 is a bright gamma-ray source classified as a high-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1703.09143]. Multi-wavelength observations and spectral energy distribution modeling suggest the blazar is located at a redshift of approximately 0.1 to 0.3 [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2FGL J0143.6-5844 with a bright multi-wavelength X-ray counterpart and supports an HSP BL Lac interpretation at redshift 0.1-0.3.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J0143.6-5844 (3FGL J0143.7-5845) This bright ray source is classified as an UGS in the 2FGL, but as a BL Lac object with unknown redshift in the 3FGL and 3LAC catalogues The source was observed by Swift /XRT for about 4.5 ksec. As discussed in Sec. 0143_ass , within the 3FGL Fermi error-box a very bright X-ray source has been found with multi-wavelength counterparts and the resulting multi-wavelength SED (Fig. fig:0143_ass bottom panel) presents a good spectral coverage. Our blazar recognition tool (Fig. fig:0143_metodo ) indicates clearly a minimum _ min ^ 2 corresponding to an HSP template that fits the source luminosity data assuming a redshift of 0.1-0.3. Our proposed association and classification is in agreement with the 3LAC classification and with the optical spectrum reported in landoni2015 where the source is classified as a BL Lac object with unknown redshift for lack of optical features.

gmw_00009127.name: 2FGL J1129.5+3758
gmw_00009127.overview: 2FGL J1129.5+3758 is a high-energy gamma-ray emitter with XRT J1129-375857 identified as its likely X-ray counterpart [arXiv:1703.09143]. Multi-frequency spectral energy distribution modeling indicates that the source is a blazar, with a best-fit redshift of z ~ 1.6 [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper proposes XRT J1129-375857 as the likely counterpart of 2FGL J1129.5+3758 and finds an LSP blazar-like SED with high-redshift solutions.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J1129.5+3758 (3FGL J1129.0+3758) The error-box area of this S/N=10.25 ray emitter was observed for a total of about 4.7 ksec by Swift /XRT and the X-ray sky map is reported in Fig. fig:1129_ass . We proposed the object XRT J1129-375857 as the likely X-ray counterpart and we were able to build its multi-frequency SED spanning from radio to HE band. Our blazar like recognition code results in the diagnostic plots reported in Fig. fig:1129_metodo and the SED template with the minimum _ ,min ^ 2 corresponds to an LSP SED, for a best-fit redshift of z 1.6. However we can see a significant degeneracy with other solutions belong to the same class of LSP at z 1.2 to 1.5 and to the ISP class at z 0.5 to 1.2. In either case, a high value of the redshift is indicated.

gmw_00009128.name: 2FGL J1410.4+7411
gmw_00009128.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2FGL J1410.4+7411 is associated with the X-ray source XRT J141045+740509 [arXiv:1703.09143]. Its spectral energy distribution suggests it is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:1703.09143], while optical spectroscopy classifies the counterpart as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy at a redshift of 0.429 [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper proposes an X-ray counterpart for 2FGL J1410.4+7411, classifies the SED as an HSP object at tentative redshift 0.5-0.6, and notes an optical NLSY1 redshift.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J1410.4+7411 (3FGL J1410.9+7406) Thanks to the new reduced 3FGL error box of this Fermi UGS, we can find an X-ray source that can be proposed as likely counterpart for the source (see details in 1410_ass ). Despite the lack of a radio counterpart, that could help the tool to constrain the classification and the redshift for this object, we have a good spectral coverage at the other frequencies and we can build a multi-wavelength SED for the counterpart XRT J141045+740509. Based on our blazar like SED recognition tool (Fig. fig:1410_metodo ), we suggest that the nature of 2FGL J1410.4+7411 is an HSP object with a high tentative redshift of z =0.5-0.6. An optical classification of our proposed counterpart is provided by marchesini2016 . The optical spectrum shows emission lines allowing to classify the source as a new NLSY1 with a z = 0.429.

gmw_00009129.name: 2FGL J1502.1+5548
gmw_00009129.overview: The gamma-ray source 2FGL J1502.1+5548 is spatially coincident with a single X-ray and radio counterpart [arXiv:1703.09143]. It is classified as an uncertain blazar, with potential spectral energy distribution solutions pointing to either a high-redshift or a lower-redshift class [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper identifies a single X-ray/radio counterpart for 2FGL J1502.1+5548 and finds uncertain blazar classifications, with LSP high-redshift and ISP lower-redshift solutions.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J1502.1+5548 (3FGL J1502.2+5553) The source is still an UGS in the 3FGL catalogue with a detection significance of 12.6 . In the 3FGL error-box region of the source, the only X-ray source found is 1SXPS J150229.0+555204, which is spatially coincident with a radio source. We propose it as the likely X-ray counterpart for 2FGL J1502.1+5548 (the broad-band SED and details in Sect. 1502_ass ). The resulting plots by our blazar SED-recognition tool are shown in Fig. fig:1502_metodo . The best-fitting SED template, with minimum _ ,min ^ 2 , belongs to the LSP class at redshift 1.6-1.9, but a similarly good solution is found with a template of the ISP class at lower redshift (z 0.4-0.7). Hence for this source the blazar classification and redshift are uncertain (but a high redshift is indicated), probably due to the limited spectral coverage of the synchrotron peak for this source.

gmw_00009130.name: 2FGL J1511.8-0513
gmw_00009130.overview: 2FGL J1511.8-0513 is a blazar candidate [arXiv:1703.09143] that is classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1703.09143]. It is associated with a high-synchrotron-peaked source with a tentative redshift of 0.1 to 0.2 [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2FGL J1511.8-0513 as a blazar candidate, selects the brightest XRT source as counterpart, and finds an HSP/BL Lac interpretation with redshift 0.1-0.2.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J1511.8-0513 (3FGL J1511.8-0513) This object is present in the 3FGL and 3LAC catalogues with a significance of 10.59 and its new classification is a blazar candidate of uncertain type with unknown redshift. Two X-ray sources are found in the source region observed by Swift XRT (App. 1511_ass ), but only the brightest, XRT J151148-051348, is in the reduced 3FGL error ellipse and also proposed as counterpart in the Fermi catalogue. The diagnostic plots for the X-ray counterpart are shown in Fig. fig:1511_metodo and the best-fitting SED template corresponds to an HSP with a tentative redshift z= 0.1-0.2. Our classification is in agreement with the result reported in alvarez2016 , where the source is classified as a BL Lac object with unknown redshift, on the basis of its featureless optical spectrum.

gmw_00009131.name: 2FGL J1614.8+4703
gmw_00009131.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2FGL J1614.8+4703 is classified as an intermediate synchrotron peaked blazar [arXiv:1703.09143]. Its host galaxy is identified as an early-type spiral galaxy [arXiv:1703.09143] that lacks broad optical emission lines [arXiv:1504.07236].

sources.summary: The paper proposes XRT J161541+471110 as the counterpart of 2FGL J1614.8+4703, classifies it as an ISP object near redshift 0.3, and notes a possible spiral host.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J1614.8+4703 (3FGL J1615.8+4712) The multi-wavelength counterpart set for this source is discussed in Sec. 1614_ass and we propose the Swift source XRT J161541+471110 as the likely X-ray counterpart, in agreement with the association reported in the 3FGL and 3LAC catalogues. In Fig. fig:1614_ass , we show its multi-wavelength SED built from its counterpart set. Based on our blazar recognition tool (Fig. fig:1615_metodo ) and on the minimum value of _ min ^ 2 , we suggest that 2FGL J1614.8+4703 is an ISP object at redshift z =0.3. For this object, the SDSS survey has reported the presence of an early-type spiral (Sa) ( fig:1614_ass ) in the position of our optical counterpart, with a measured spectroscopic redshift of z=0.19, that may represent the host galaxy of a very faint low-z blazar .

sources.summary: The paper notes this source lacks broad optical emission lines, has a color index suggesting weak non-thermal host contrast, and was proposed as a possible LAT counterpart.

quotes.quote: The other source that does not show broad emission lines in its optical spectrum is SDSS J161541.21 + 471111.7 . The value that we found for its color index (u-) = 1.34, even if lower than the threshold (u-)^ established by , suggests a relatively weak non-thermal contribution with respect to the host galaxy. Moreover, this source was recently addressed by 2013ApJS..206...13M as a possible counterpart of the unidentified ray source (UGS) 2FGL J1614.8+4703 detected by LAT.

gmw_00009132.name: 2FGL J1704.3+1235
gmw_00009132.overview: 2FGL J1704.3+1235 is a gamma-ray emitting blazar [arXiv:1703.09143, arXiv:1711.05476] associated with the optical source SDSSJ170409+123421 [arXiv:1711.05476]. Its optical spectrum shows a redshift of 0.452 [arXiv:1711.05476], featuring stellar absorption lines alongside [O II] and [O III] emission lines [arXiv:1711.05476].

sources.summary: The paper finds a unique bright counterpart for 2FGL J1704.3+1235 and, using its blazar-like SED tool, reports an HSP solution at tentative redshift 0.3.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J1704.3+1235 (3FGL J1704.1+1234) Inside the error-box of this 3FGL S/N=9.43 Fermi source (details in Sect. 1704_12_ass ), we found only one bright X-ray counterpart with data in the radio, optical and IR. This appears to be as a robust and unique counterpart for 2FGL J1704.3+1235. For this source, the SDSS survey reports the presence of an un-resolved reddish object, in the source position, classified as a star . Based on our blazar like SED recognition tool (Fig. fig:1704_12_metodo ), we find two possible solutions. One is in terms of an HSP object with tentative redshift z =0.3.

sources.summary: The paper associates 3FGLJ1704.1+1234 with SDSSJ170409+123421, confirms z = 0.45, and detects stellar absorption plus [O II] and [O III] emission at z = 0.452.

quotes.quote: 3FGLJ1704.1+1234 On the basis of the X-ray imaging, in the 3FGL error box of this ray emitter we found an X-ray source XRTJ170409+123421 and we propose that it is associated to the optical source SDSSJ170409+123421 (g=19.1). The same association was also proposed by alvarez2016B who also obtained an optical spectrum, deriving a redshift of z = 0.45. Our better S/N spectrum confirms this redshift and, in addition to absorptions due to Ca II (3934 , 3968 ), G-band (4305 ) and Mg I (5157 ), we also detect the [ O II ] (3727 ) and [ O III ] (5007 ) emission features at the redshift z = 0.452.

gmw_00009133.name: 2FGL J2115.4+1213
gmw_00009133.overview: 2FGL J2115.4+1213 is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar located at a redshift of approximately 0.4 [arXiv:1703.09143]. Its proposed X-ray counterpart is the source XRT J211522+121801, which corresponds to an unresolved optical object [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper chooses the brighter X-ray source as the likely counterpart of 2FGL J2115.4+1213 and classifies the Fermi source as an HSP blazar at redshift 0.4.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J2115.4+1213 (3FGL J2115.2+1213) Of the two X-ray sources found in the 3FGL error-box of 2FGL J2115.4+1213, as discussed in App. 2115_ass , the fainter one has essentially no counterparts in other bands. Instead for the brighter X-ray source, XRT J211522+121801, we find counterparts in all bands and for this reason it is proposed as our likely X-ray counterpart. Based on our blazar recognition algorithm (see Fig. fig:2115_metodo ), we suggest that 2FGL J2115.4+1213 is a blazar of the HSP class at redshift z= 0.4. About the optical counterpart, the SDSS survey reports the presence of an un-resolved object that is classified as a star .

gmw_00009134.name: 2FGL J2246.3+1549
gmw_00009134.overview: 2FGL J2246.3+1549 is a gamma-ray emitting blazar candidate of unknown type [arXiv:1703.09143]. It is classified as an intermediate-synchrotron-peaked source with a tentative redshift between 0.3 and 0.8 [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper treats 2FGL J2246.3+1549 as a BCU-II blazar candidate, finds one faint X-ray counterpart, and fits an ISP SED with tentative redshift 0.3-0.8.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J2246.3+1549 (3FGL J2246.2+1547) This ray emitter is reported in the 3FGL and 3LAC catalogue with a detection significance of 9.5 and it is classified as a blazar candidate of unknown type (BCU-II) with an ISP SED classification and an unknown redshift. From the analysis of the XRT data covering the error-box field (discussed in Sect. 2246_ass ), we found only one faint X-ray source with positional counterparts in various bands. Despite this source is not within the 3FGL, we suggest this as X-ray counterpart because it is the only X-ray source detected around the 2FGL J2246.3+1549 sky region and moreover our proposal is in agreement with the 3FGL association. The plots based on our tool are shown in Fig. fig:2246_metodo . The best-fitting SED template indicates a classification as ISP object with a tentative redshift from z 0.3 to 0.8 , although the upper value corresponds to an only marginally acceptable MAD.

gmw_00009135.name: 2FGL J2347.2+0707
gmw_00009135.overview: 2FGL J2347.2+0707 is a blazar classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1703.09143]. It exhibits an intermediate-to-high-synchrotron-peaked spectral energy distribution and has an estimated redshift of approximately 0.17 to 0.2 [arXiv:1703.09143].

sources.summary: The paper links 2FGL J2347.2+0707 to a bright multi-band counterpart classified as BCU-II/ISP and supports an ISP-HSP BL Lac interpretation near redshift 0.2.

quotes.quote: 2FGL J2347.2+0707 (3FGL J2346.7+0705) Inside the 3FGL error-box of this S/N=13.83 Fermi source of 3 arc-mins (see Sec. 2347_ass ), we found a bright X-ray source with good counterparts in the radio, optical and IR. This counterpart set is in agreement with the 3FGL and 3LAC association where the source is classified as a BCU-II with a ISP SED and with unknown redshift. Based on our blazar recognition code (see Fig. fig:2346_metodo ), we suggest that the source is a blazar of the ISP-HSP class with a best-fit redshift of z= 0.2. The SDSS survey (dr12) reports the presence of a = 16.62 BL Lac object in our proposed counterpart position, for which a spectroscopic redshift of z 0.17 is provided by the SDSS automatic analysis procedure and that is in a good agreement and supportive of our result.

gmw_00009136.name: NGC 681
gmw_00009136.overview: NGC 681 is a galaxy characterized by a thin and large stellar disk that dissects its luminous halo [arXiv:1408.2602]. It is classified as an uncertain polar-ring candidate because image subtraction cannot definitively confirm whether its ring feature lies in the galaxy plane or is polar [arXiv:1408.2602].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 681 as an uncertain polar-ring candidate: its thin disk cuts across a luminous halo, and even a subtracted image cannot confirm whether the feature is polar or in-plane.

quotes.quote: Only three polar ring candidate galaxies were detected among the S ^ 4 G sample galaxies. Of these, NGC 660 e.g., driel95 and NGC 5122 e.g., reshetnikov01 are known to be polar ring galaxies, while NGC 681 is not known to be a polar ring galaxy. The detection of a polar ring in this galaxy is uncertain due to the thin and large disc that dissects the luminous halo of this galaxy. Even an image where the underlying disc/bulge component has been subtracted, cannot reveal with certainty whether this feature is a ring in the galaxy plane or a polar ring.

gmw_00009137.name: NGC 6870
gmw_00009137.overview: NGC 6870 is a galaxy included in the Spitzer Survey of Stellar Structure in Galaxies [arXiv:1408.2602]. Although a polar ring structure was previously proposed for this galaxy, the feature is considered exceedingly subtle and may not actually be a polar ring [arXiv:1408.2602].

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 6870 as an S4G galaxy with a previously proposed polar ring, but adopts the view that the ring is too subtle or ambiguous to accept.

quotes.quote: Other polar rings have been found in the S ^ 4 G sample galaxies NGC 2748, NGC 6870, and NGC 7465, by buta14 . Their tentative contours are marked in the appendices of comeron14 . However, those three polar rings are exceedingly subtle and/or hard to interpret. Therefore, they may actually not be polar rings. This is the position adopted in this paper.

gmw_00009138.name: NGC 632
gmw_00009138.overview: NGC 632 is an S0 galaxy that hosts a nuclear starburst [arXiv:astro-ph/0210534]. It features a nearby star-forming region and a faint tidal tail extending at least 50 kpc, which strongly supports a galaxy merger origin [arXiv:astro-ph/0210534].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 632 as an S0 host with a nuclear starburst, a nearby star-forming region, and a faint tidal tail supporting a merger origin.

quotes.quote: The most promising case during the last years was represented by SN 1998es in NGC 632. This SN, in fact, was classified as a 1991T-like by Jha et al. , who also noticed that the parent galaxy was classified as an S0, hosting a nuclear starburst (Pogge Eskridge ). Moreover, the SN was reported to be projected very close to a star forming region and an high resolution spectrum showed interstellar NaI D features very similar to those of 1991T (Patat et al. in preparation), indicating the presence of a significant amount of material in front of the SN ( EW =1.46 ).

The FORS2 images were very deep, the combined B frames reaching a 5 peak limiting magnitude of 27 for unresolved objects. Despite the fact that such deep data did not show the light echo, they led to the serendipitous discovery of a very faint tidal tail (Patat, Carraro Romaniello ), which extends for at least 50 kpc. This discovery strongly supports the suggestion made by Chitre Joshi that NGC 632 is probably the result of a galaxy merger, which in turn is the responsible for the observed nuclear/circum-nuclear starburst.

gmw_00009139.name: barred galaxy \ (RA: 13h00m39.1s, DEC: +02 ^ 30m05s, J2000)
gmw_00009139.overview: The barred galaxy (RA: 13h00m39.1s, DEC: +02^30m05s, J2000) is a SB(rs) galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:1003.4235]. It features young starbursts along its large-scale bar, a perpendicular dust lane, and composite nuclear activity [arXiv:1003.4235].

sources.summary: The paper studies a Virgo SB(rs) barred galaxy, finding young bursts along its large-scale bar, a perpendicular dust lane, older stars, and composite nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present a study of the stellar populations and gas in the central region of the barred galaxy \ (RA: 13h00m39.1s, DEC: +02 ^ 30m05s, J2000) using visible data obtained with the integral field unit OASIS (Optically Adaptive System for Imaging Spectroscopy). \ is a SB(rs) galaxy located in the Virgo cluster at a distance of 13.3 Mpc (using a redshift of 960 3 km s ^ 1 from NED and H _0 =72 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 ). It is located at about 12 ^ South-East of M87. It does not show any evidence of a recent interaction with other cluster members and 1997A&A...326..449M did not find any particular nuclear activity In 1999, \ hosted a supernova (SN 1999br) which was located on its outskirts, far from the region observed with OASIS. .

We find a few young bursts, with an age between 5.5 and 6.5 Myr, located along the galaxy large scale bar. We see an extended region of about 8 Myr superposed to a dust lane which is oriented perpendicular to the galaxy large scale bar. These young populations are superposed to a longer episode of star formation with an average age near 850 Myr. In the galaxy nucleus, the age of the old population reaches 940 Myr and there is a composite/transition region activity.

gmw_00009140.name: GP 0911+1831
gmw_00009140.overview: GP 0911+1831 is a Green Pea galaxy located at a systemic redshift of approximately 0.262236 [arXiv:2111.03671]. It is classified as a Lyman-alpha emission line galaxy with observed Lyman-alpha spectra that can be fitted using shell models to analyze its outflow velocity [arXiv:2111.03671].

sources.summary: The paper fits the observed Lyman-alpha spectrum of Green Pea galaxy GP 0911+1831, finding degenerate shell models that affect inferred outflow velocity and systemic redshift.

quotes.quote: Here we further show the quadruple degeneracy with a practical example. We fit an observed \ spectrum of a Green Pea galaxy, GP 0911+1831 ( z = 0.262236; Henry15 ) with our shell model grid. The spectrum is obtained from the \ Spectral Database (LASD http://lasd.lyman-alpha.com ; LASD ). Following Orlitova18 , we account for the spectral resolution of the HST Cosmic Origins Spectrograph (COS) by convolving the shell model spectra with a FWHM = 100 km s^ 1 Gaussian before comparing them to the observed \ spectrum Note that different from Orlitova18 , we do not consider the effect of dust, as the dust optical depth is usually a poorly constrained parameter and may introduce additional degeneracy Gronke15 . . We present two degenerate best-fit shell models in Figure fig:degeneracy_example . These two best-fit models, whose ^2 per degree of freedom are very close to each other, have the parameter degeneracy as described in sec:outflow_deg - the shell expansion velocity of the high temperature model is about a factor of two higher than the low temperature model, which consequently affects the fitted systemic redshift of the \ source.

gmw_00009141.name: companion galaxy
gmw_00009141.overview: The companion galaxy is located in the southeast quadrant of its system [arXiv:1911.06326]. It has been detected in CO line emission for the first time, revealing a narrow line profile and an estimated molecular gas mass of approximately 10^8 to 5 * 10^8 solar masses [arXiv:1911.06326].

sources.summary: The paper reports the first CO line detection of the southeast companion, gives its sky position, narrow line profile, CO flux, and inferred molecular gas mass.

quotes.quote: The left panel of Figure fig:hst_co shows extended CO emission at the location of the companion galaxy in the south-east quadrant. This is the first time this galaxy is detected in CO line emission. The peak is at R.A. 9:00:25.6 and Dec. + 39:03:49, and coincides with the position of the galaxy in SDSS i- band images and the HST (F814W). The line profile has a regular shape, is narrow ( =59 km s ^ 1 ), and peaks at v= 30 km s ^ 1 (bottom-left panel in Figure fig:hst_co ). The line has a flux of F_ CO(1-0) =0.8 Jy km s ^ 1 , which corresponds to a molecular gas mass of M_ mol 10^ 8 M_ assuming a conversion factor _ CO,ULIRG (Table tab:components ) and M_ mol 5 10^ 8 M_ assuming _ CO,MW .

gmw_00009142.name: AzTEC/C159
gmw_00009142.overview: AzTEC/C159 is a compact starburst galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 4.57 [arXiv:2011.05340]. It is optically faint and undetected in the F814W band [arXiv:1802.07751], but it exhibits a rotating disc signature and contains high stellar and molecular gas masses [arXiv:2011.05340].

sources.summary: The paper describes AzTEC/C159 as a compact z=4.57 starburst with a rotating disc signature, high stellar and molecular gas masses, and ALMA-resolved dynamics.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/C159 was first detected through the ASTE/AzTEC-COSMOS 1.1- survey Aretxaga+2011 . It is located at z 4.57 and has an estimated 700 and a stellar mass 4.5 0.4 10^ 10 Smolcic+2015,Gomez+2018 . Low angular resolution observations in and revealed a tentative double-peaked profile consistent with a rotating disc Jimenez+2018 . The total mass of molecular gas is _ 1.5 0.3 10^ 11 , estimated using an factor of 4.3 ( ^2)^ 1 see details in Jimenez+2018 . Gomez+2018 also estimated an effective radius of 460^ +60 _ 240 at rest-frame FIR wavelengths, which is indicative of a very compact object.

sources.summary: AzTEC/C159 is optically faint, excluded from the HST program, undetected in F814W, missing from the catalog, yet has compact FIR emission associated with IRAC detections.

quotes.quote: AzTEC/C159 was not in our HST program due to its faintness at near-IR wavelengths.

AzTEC/C159 : As mentioned in Section subsec:hst_data this source was excluded from the HST program and remains undetected in the F814W band image, so we do not have any constraints on its UV morphology. The rest-frame FIR emission is compact and associated with detections in the IRAC bands.
AzTEC/C159 is also missing from the catalog, due to its extreme faintness in the optical/near-IR.

gmw_00009143.name: IC 5325
gmw_00009143.overview: IC 5325 is a spiral galaxy with a stellar population modeled to be around 4 Gyr old [arXiv:2104.06236]. Its most recent star-formation activity began approximately 1 Gyr ago, and the galaxy contains an estimated dust mass of 3.75 × 10^7 solar masses [arXiv:2104.06236].

sources.summary: The paper models IC 5325 with a 4 Gyr-old stellar population, recent star formation beginning 1 Gyr ago, moderate dust attenuation, synchrotron emission, and a measured dust mass.

quotes.quote: IC 5325: The SFH for IC 5325 is best-fit with the oldest stellar population to be around 4000 Myr in age and with the onset of the most recent star-formation activity starting around 1000 Myr ago. Stellar emission was modeled using a stellar population having a metallicity of 0.02 and a separation age of 15 Myr between the young and old stellar populations. The dust attenuation is modeled without a UV bump and exhibits a colour excess of stellar continuum light (E(B V)*) for the young and old population as 0.30 and 0.05 , respectively. The dust emission was modeled by using a power-law slope of 2.3 , a moderate PAH fraction of 3.90 , a minimum radiation field value, U_ min = 1.5 and 90 of dust is exposed to the radiation between U_ min and U_ max . The slope of the synchrotron emission is 0.5 , and the FIR/radio correlation coefficient, q_ IR = 2.70 .
The dust mass is estimated to be 3.75 10^7 M _ .

gmw_00009144.name: zC-405501
gmw_00009144.overview: The high-redshift object zC-405501 is classified as a disk galaxy [arXiv:2109.02659]. It exhibits a broad disk-halo degeneracy due to the effects of asymmetric drift, allowing both low- and moderate-dark-matter models to fit its observed rotation curve [arXiv:2109.02659].

sources.summary: The paper uses zC-405501 as an extreme example where asymmetric drift creates broad disk-halo degeneracy, allowing low- and moderate-dark-matter models to fit similarly.

quotes.quote: We highlight an extreme example of the added complication of asymmetric drift to the disk-halo degeneracy in Figure fig:vrot_int_example : zC-405501. This galaxy has a very broad likelihood degeneracy between - that is strongly impacted by the asymmetric drift correction. In the left panel, we show a low dark matter fraction model, with =0.01 , =10.74 , and =60 km \ s^ 1 , while the right panel shows a moderately high dark matter fraction model of =0.52 , =10.46 , and =65 km \ s^ 1 . All other parameters are the same between both models (i.e., =0.07 , =6 kpc , =0.2 ).

If is very well constrained from the dispersion profile, the added impact of asymmetric drift can be partially mitigated, but for the case of zC-405501, the uncertainties on () (typically 10 15 km \ s^ 1 ) allow room for a slightly higher to better match the falloff for the 52 dark matter fraction case. Observations with higher spatial resolution can also help to break this added degeneracy, as the intrinsic rotation curves (solid black lines) exhibit shape differences at small radii.

gmw_00009145.name: RGB J0254 + 3931
gmw_00009145.overview: RGB J0254 + 3931 is classified as a broad-line radio galaxy and a quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. It features a one-sided jet that appears to terminate in a lobe or hot spot [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: The paper describes RGB J0254 + 3931 as a broad-line radio galaxy/quasar with a one-sided jet, lobe or hot spot, and matching VLA morphology.

quotes.quote: RGB J0254 + 3931. Optical spectra can be found in Marcha96 and LM98 who classified this source as a broad-line radio galaxy and quasar respectively. This source shows a one-sided jet on the combined A + C image which is most prominent on the A array image and appears to end in a lobe or hot spot (Figs. 1 () and (d)). A similar morphology is visible also on the map published by Tay96 based on VLA B array snapshot observations at 4.535 GHz.

gmw_00009146.name: RGB J2308 + 2008
gmw_00009146.overview: RGB J2308 + 2008 is a broad-line radio galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.2342 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. Although the galaxy is unresolved on C-array scales, high-resolution A-array imaging reveals a faint radio lobe close to its core [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: RGB J2308 + 2008 is described as a broad-line radio galaxy at z=0.2342, unresolved on C-array scales but showing a faint A-array lobe.

quotes.quote: RGB J2308 + 2008. An optical spectrum can be found in LM98 . Based on this and an absolute optical magnitude of M>-22 mag these authors classified this source as a broad-line radio galaxy. Falco98 give a slightly different redshift for this source of z=0.2342 based on four emission lines. The source is unresolved on C array scales, however, a faint lobe is visible close to the core on the A array image (Fig. 1(i)).

gmw_00009147.name: WGA J0106 1034
gmw_00009147.overview: WGA J0106 1034 is a broad-emission-line active galactic nucleus and quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. It is classified as a Fanaroff-Riley II radio source with asymmetric hot spots and a radio jet pointing toward its weaker lobe [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: The source is classified as a broad-emission-line AGN/quasar and has an FR II radio structure with asymmetric hot spots, an unusual jet direction, and a steep ATCA spectral index.

quotes.quote: WGA J0106 1034. Optical spectra of this source can be found in Wol98 and L01 who classify it as a broad-emission line AGN and quasar, respectively. This source is also part of the FIRST Bright Quasar Survey Beck01 and the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Schn03 . The A array image of this source (Fig. 1(k)) shows extended emission with hot spots on each side of the nucleus, making it clearly an FR II source. However, one of the two hot spots is considerably stronger than the other, and this source appears unusual in that the radio jet we see points towards the weaker of the two lobes. The source is unresolved on C array scales. Our ATCA snapshot observations of this source give a steeper spectral index of _ =0.67 (between 4.8 and 8.6 GHz) than the one listed in Table general ( _ =0.35 ).

gmw_00009148.name: WGA J0227 0847
gmw_00009148.overview: WGA J0227 0847 is classified as a quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. Radio observations show an unresolved core [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718], with a spectral index that suggests either variability or behavior as a Gigahertz peaked-spectrum candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: WGA J0227 0847 is classified as a quasar; its ATCA spectral index suggests variability or GPS behavior, while VLA/FIRST images show an unresolved radio core.

quotes.quote: WGA J0227 0847. An optical spectrum of this source can be found in L01 who classify it as a quasar. This source is also part of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Schn03 . Our ATCA snapshot observations of this source give a spectral index of _ =0.37 between 4.8 and 8.6 GHz, which is steeper than the one listed in Table general ( _ =-0.34 ). This is either due to variability or to the fact that this source is a Gigahertz peaked-spectrum (GPS) candidate e.g., ODea98 . The source remains unresolved in the radio on the scales imaged (Fig. 1(n)). Similarly, the VLA B array map from the FIRST survey at 20 cm shows only an unresolved core.

gmw_00009149.name: WGA J0259 + 1926
gmw_00009149.overview: WGA J0259 + 1926 is classified as a quasar with a broad-line optical spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. Its radio structure includes a jet-like feature extending several arcseconds from the core, as well as a slightly extended morphology on VLBA scales [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: The paper classifies WGA J0259 + 1926 as a quasar and reports a jet-like A-array feature plus slightly extended VLBA-scale morphology.

quotes.quote: WGA J0259 + 1926. An optical spectrum of this source can be found in Cac00 who classify it as a broad-line object. Our own high-quality spectrum of this source will be published shortly. We classify this source as a quasar. The A array image of this source (Fig. 1(o)) shows a jet-like feature extending a few arcseconds away from the core. A slightly extended morphology is visible even on the scales imaged with the VLBA at 8.4 GHz Ojha04 .

gmw_00009150.name: WGA J0435 0811
gmw_00009150.overview: WGA J0435 0811 is classified as a quasar based on its optical spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. The source appears unresolved on A-array scales, though its NVSS image is slightly extended and likely has resolved-out extended emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: WGA J0435 0811 is classified as a quasar; it appears unresolved in A-array data, but extended emission is likely resolved out and the NVSS image is slightly extended.

quotes.quote: WGA J0435 0811. Per98 published an optical spectrum and classified this source as a quasar. It is unresolved on A array scales (Fig. 1()), however, most likely its extended emission is resolved out. The NVSS image is slightly extended and the ratio of NVSS to total measured flux on our image for this source is the highest in the sample (see Table radio , column (6)).

gmw_00009151.name: WGA J0447 0322
gmw_00009151.overview: WGA J0447 0322 is a bright flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0611839] and broad-line object [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718] located at a redshift of z=0.773 [arXiv:astro-ph/0611839]. The source remains unresolved in radio images [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718] and exhibits modest, long-term optical variability [arXiv:astro-ph/0611839].

sources.summary: The paper presents 1WGA J0447.9-0322 as a bright flat-spectrum radio quasar at z=0.773, with a decades-long Asiago light curve, modest variability, a high X-ray/optical ratio, and UV-optical SED observations.

quotes.quote: We present the historic light curve of 1WGA J0447.9-0322, spanning the time interval from 1962 to 1991, built using the Asiago archive plates. The source shows small fluctuations of about 0.3 mag around B=16 until 1986 and a fast dimming of its average level by about 0.5 mag after that date, again with small short term variations. The variability pattern is within the values shown by other QSOs with long term monitoring, notwithstanding its high X-ray/optical ratio. We present also its overall SED using literature data and recent UV-optical SWIFT observations.

One of these is the radio source PMN J0447-0322 (Griffith et al. 1995), identified as an X-ray source in the WGA catalogue (White et al. 1995, 2000). It is present as a bright source in the Rosat All Sky Survey (Voges et al. 1999) and as such it was included in ROSAT-based catalogues of AGNs like the REX survey (Caccianiga et al. 2000). On the basis of its optical and radio spectrum it was classified as a bright Flat Spectrum Radio Quasar (FSRQ) by Caccianiga et al. (2000) with a redshift z=0.773. It is also present in the 2MASS infrared survey (Barkhouse Hall 2001).

sources.summary: WGA J0447 0322 is classified as a quasar/broad-line object, remains unresolved in the radio images, and has a steeper ATCA spectral index than tabulated.

quotes.quote: WGA J0447 0322. Optical spectra of this source can be found in Per98 and Cac00 who classified it as a quasar and broad-line object, respectively. This source remains unresolved on the scales imaged (Fig. 1(s)). Our ATCA snapshot observations give a steeper spectral index of _ =0.91 (between 4.8 and 8.6 GHz) than the one listed in Table general ( _ =0.47 ).

gmw_00009152.name: WGA J0544 2241
gmw_00009152.overview: WGA J0544 2241 is classified as a quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. It remains unresolved in radio observations, and its spectral index suggests it is either a variable source or a gigahertz peaked-spectrum candidate [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: The source is classified as a quasar, unresolved in the radio images, and has an ATCA spectral index suggesting variability or GPS-candidate behavior.

quotes.quote: WGA J0544 2241. Per98 published an optical spectrum and classified this source as a quasar. The source remains unresolved in the radio on the scales imaged (Fig. 1(t)). Our ATCA snapshot observations give a spectral index of _ =0.31 (between 4.8 and 8.6 GHz), which is steeper than the one listed in Table general ( _ =-0.44 ). This is either due to variability or to the fact that this source is a Gigahertz peaked-spectrum (GPS) candidate e.g., ODea98 .

gmw_00009153.name: WGA J1457 2818
gmw_00009153.overview: WGA J1457 2818 is classified as a quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718]. It exhibits slightly extended radio emission away from its core and has a steep spectral index [arXiv:astro-ph/0509718].

sources.summary: WGA J1457 2818 is classified as a quasar with slightly extended A-array emission and a steep ATCA spectral index consistent with the observed morphology.

quotes.quote: WGA J1457 2818. An optical spectrum of this source can be found in L01 who classify it as a quasar. Slightly extended emission away from the core is visible on A array scales (Fig. 1(v)). In better accordance with the observed morphology, our ATCA snapshot observations of this source give a steeper spectral index of _ =0.72 (between 4.8 and 8.6 GHz) than the one listed in Table general ( _ =0.40 ).

gmw_00009154.name: WGA J2239 0631
